mcq.q: Fernando asks you to design a live esports stats dashboard. What should you do FIRST?
options: Sketch a component tree to show structure quickly
Ask clarifying questions about users, scope, and scale before designing
List the React libraries you would reach for
Start drawing the backend architecture to anchor the conversation
mcq.explanation: Step 1 of the framework is requirements clarification — scoping the problem before solving it. Jumping into components or libraries signals you skip discovery, which is a common interview anti-pattern.

mcq.q: Which of these is a NON-functional requirement you should clarify in Step 1?
options: Whether users can favorite a match
Whether admins can ban accounts
Expected concurrent users during Worlds
Whether the homepage shows a leaderboard
mcq.explanation: Concurrent users is a scale (NFR) question. The other options are functional requirements describing what users can do.

mcq.q: You're 4 minutes into clarification and Fernando seems impatient. What's the right move?
options: Skip the summary and dive into architecture
Briefly summarize the requirements as a contract, then move on
Continue asking every question on your mental checklist
Apologize and restart from scratch
mcq.explanation: The framework caps Step 1 at ~5 minutes and ends with a summary that becomes a contract for the rest of the session. Skipping the summary loses alignment; over-asking burns the clock.

mcq.q: Why explicitly write down requirements on the shared drawing surface?
options: To slow the interview down so you have thinking time
It creates a visible contract you can refer back to and prevents scope creep
Interviewers grade you on penmanship
It's required by Riot's interview rubric
mcq.explanation: Writing requirements down anchors the rest of the discussion and lets you push back on scope creep later by pointing to what was agreed.

recall.q: Why ask 'what are we NOT building?' during Step 1?
recall.model: Explicit non-goals prevent scope creep and help you allocate time. Saying 'admin moderation is out of scope' frees you from designing it and gives the interviewer a chance to correct you if it actually IS in scope.

recall.q: Give three non-functional requirement dimensions worth asking about for a Riot match-stats site.
recall.model: Scale (concurrent viewers during Worlds), latency (real-time vs eventually consistent stat updates), and accessibility/i18n (WCAG level, languages and regions Riot ships to). Offline support is usually a fourth.

recall.q: Why is 'who is the user?' the first functional question to ask?
recall.model: User identity drives every downstream decision — players, admins, broadcasters, and API consumers have wildly different needs, scale, auth, and UX. Building for the wrong persona invalidates the rest of the design.

recall.q: What's the concrete output of Step 1 that you carry into Step 2?
recall.model: A short, visible summary on the shared surface that lists users, core flows, scope boundaries, scale, and any latency or accessibility constraints. It becomes the contract for the rest of the design.

recall.q: Why ask about platform (web only? mobile web? native?) early?
recall.model: Platform changes everything — bundle size budgets, offline strategy, input model, viewport assumptions, and even framework choice. Discovering 'oh, this needs to work on a low-end Android' after you've designed for desktop wastes time.

scenarios.q: Fernando says: 'Design a Worlds bracket viewer.' You have 45 minutes. How do you spend your first 5?
scenarios.model: Ask: who views it (fans vs broadcasters vs admins)? Scale (millions concurrent during finals)? Real-time updates as matches end, or refresh on load? Multi-language for global audience? Mobile web priority? Then summarize back: 'Public-facing fan viewer, ~5M concurrent peak, near-real-time bracket updates, mobile-first, en/ko/zh/es. Out of scope: predictions, betting, admin tools.' Write it on the canvas.

scenarios.q: Mid-Step-1 the interviewer says 'assume whatever scale you want.' How do you handle it?
scenarios.model: Pick a concrete number out loud and justify it: 'I'll assume 1M concurrent during a finals match — that drives a CDN-heavy design and a separate WebSocket gateway.' Concrete numbers force concrete tradeoffs and show you can reason about scale instead of waving hands.

mcq.q: Why should app servers be stateless in a Riot-scale architecture?
options: Stateless servers are faster per request
Any server can handle any request, enabling horizontal scaling and rolling deploys
It removes the need for a database
Stateless is required by HTTP/2
mcq.explanation: Statelessness lets a load balancer route any request to any server, which is the prerequisite for horizontal scaling and zero-downtime deploys. Session state moves to Redis.

mcq.q: You're using WebSockets for live match updates. Where should the WebSocket layer live?
options: Inside the same process as the REST app servers
On a dedicated WebSocket gateway that subscribes to a message queue
Inside the CDN edge layer
Inside the database via LISTEN/NOTIFY
mcq.explanation: A dedicated gateway decouples persistent connections from request/response app servers and lets each scale independently. The gateway subscribes to Kafka/Redis Pub/Sub for game events and fans them out to clients.

mcq.q: Why cache completed match data at the CDN but NOT live match state?
options: CDNs only support static files
Completed matches are immutable and benefit from long TTLs; live state changes per-second so caching causes staleness
Live state is too large to cache
CDNs charge more for dynamic content
mcq.explanation: Immutable data is the ideal CDN payload — set a long TTL and serve from edge globally. Live state mutates constantly, so any TTL that helps load also hurts correctness; push it via WebSocket instead.

mcq.q: When would you choose Postgres over MongoDB for a Riot prediction game?
options: When you need flexible schemaless event blobs
When you have relational data (users, predictions, matches) and need transactional consistency
Mongo cannot scale past 1000 RPS
Postgres is always faster
mcq.explanation: Choose by query pattern, not hype. Predictions involve relations and money-like correctness — Postgres' relational model and ACID transactions fit. Mongo is better for flexible schemas like raw game event blobs.

mcq.q: What's the role of Kafka (or any message queue) in a real-time stats pipeline?
options: It replaces the database
It decouples game-event producers from WebSocket fan-out, so each side scales independently
It accelerates SQL queries
It serves static assets
mcq.explanation: The queue is the seam between the game-event pipeline and client delivery. Producers publish events without caring how many clients exist; the WebSocket gateway consumes and fans out.

recall.q: Why mention sticky sessions when discussing the load balancer for WebSockets?
recall.model: WebSockets are persistent connections, so a client must stay pinned to one server for the lifetime of the socket — that's sticky sessions. The cleaner alternative is a separate WebSocket gateway so the main app servers stay stateless.

recall.q: Name three things Redis is commonly used for in this architecture and why.
recall.model: Session storage (so app servers stay stateless), hot data like live scores and leaderboards (sub-millisecond reads), and rate-limiting counters (atomic INCR with TTL). All three need low-latency reads and tolerate eviction.

recall.q: Why hashed filenames on CDN bundles?
recall.model: Content-hashed filenames let you set effectively infinite cache TTLs because any change produces a new filename. The HTML referencing the bundles uses a short TTL so users pick up the new hashes immediately.

recall.q: What does 'choose database based on query pattern, not hype' mean in practice?
recall.model: If your access pattern is relational with joins and transactions, pick SQL. If it's append-mostly schemaless events read by ID, pick NoSQL. Don't pick Mongo because it's trendy or Postgres because it's safe — pick based on what queries you'll run.

recall.q: Why draw the backend even though this is a frontend interview?
recall.model: It shows Fernando you understand how the frontend fits into the larger system — where data comes from, where caching happens, where real-time pipelines live. Drawing it briefly buys credibility and surfaces seams the frontend will integrate with.

recall.q: What's the tradeoff of putting an API response on the CDN?
recall.model: You get global low-latency reads and offload origin traffic, but you trade off freshness — anything cached at edge is stale until TTL expires or you purge. Only safe for immutable or slowly changing public data like completed match results.

scenarios.q: Fernando asks: 'Walk me through what happens from a game server emitting a kill event to a fan seeing it on the live stats page.'
scenarios.model: Game server publishes the event to Kafka. A processing service consumes, enriches it (player names, KDA), and publishes to a player-stats topic. The WebSocket gateway subscribes to that topic and pushes to the subset of clients watching that match. The client merges the event into its local cache and re-renders the affected component. The REST API also writes to Postgres so a late joiner gets the same data on initial fetch.

scenarios.q: The interviewer pushes: 'Why not just have the React app poll /stats every second?'
scenarios.model: Polling at scale wastes resources — 1M clients * 1 req/s = 1M RPS hitting your edge for data that hasn't usually changed. WebSockets push only on change, scale linearly with events not clients, and give lower latency. The seam at the queue also means you don't couple every client to every game-event producer.

scenarios.q: How would you handle a sudden traffic spike when Worlds finals start?
scenarios.model: CDN absorbs static assets and any cacheable API responses globally. Stateless app servers autoscale horizontally behind the LB. WebSocket gateways scale separately based on connection count. Redis fronts hot reads (leaderboards, live scores). Pre-warm caches and CDN before known spikes. Apply backpressure / rate limits at the edge to protect origins.

mcq.q: When breaking a UI into components, what's the right starting granularity?
options: Start with leaf-level atoms like Button and Input
Start at the page/route level and decompose downward into regions and components
Start with the Redux store shape
Start with the API endpoints and mirror them 1:1
mcq.explanation: Top-down decomposition (page -> regions -> components -> shared atoms) keeps the discussion structured. Starting at atoms is bottom-up and tends to lose the overall picture.

mcq.q: What's the main reason to call out a 'shared components' bucket explicitly?
options: It increases bundle size
It surfaces reuse opportunities and signals you're thinking about a design system
Shared components are required by React
It lets you skip prop typing
mcq.explanation: Naming Button/Input/Modal/ErrorBoundary shows you're thinking in terms of a design system and reuse — exactly what a senior frontend engineer is expected to surface.

mcq.q: Where does an ErrorBoundary belong in the component tree for a match-detail page?
options: Wrapping the entire App so one error blanks everything
Around isolatable risky regions like the VideoPlayer or StatsOverlay so a crash doesn't take down the whole page
It belongs in the backend
Inside every leaf component
mcq.explanation: Boundaries should isolate failure domains. Wrapping risky regions means a crash in the video player still leaves the rest of the page usable; wrapping the whole app makes any error catastrophic.

mcq.q: Atomic design principles — what's the key idea?
options: Components must be implemented in vanilla JS
Compose larger views from smaller, single-responsibility leaf components
Every component must be a class component
Atoms must use Web Components
mcq.explanation: Atomic design is about composition: atoms -> molecules -> organisms -> templates -> pages. The leaves are reusable, page-level components compose them.

mcq.q: You're drawing a tree and Fernando asks 'where does the LoadingSkeleton live?' What's the best answer?
options: Inside each page, duplicated
In a Shared bucket so any page can drop it in for any async region
Inside the backend
Inside the router
mcq.explanation: Loading skeletons are cross-cutting and reused across views, so they belong in shared/leaf components. Duplication invites inconsistency.

recall.q: Why decompose top-down (page -> region -> component) instead of bottom-up?
recall.model: Top-down keeps the conversation anchored to user value (what's on each page) and makes it easy to spot which regions are reusable across pages. Bottom-up risks getting lost in atoms and never showing the full picture.

recall.q: What does 'single responsibility' mean for a component?
recall.model: A component should own one concern — rendering a match card, handling a form field, displaying an error. Multi-responsibility components become hard to test, reuse, and reason about state for.

recall.q: Why is identifying leaf components important in an interview?
recall.model: Leaves are the reuse currency — Button, Input, Modal, Card. Naming them shows you think in design-system terms and that you're not duplicating UI primitives across pages.

recall.q: When designing a MatchListPage, what regions would you call out?
recall.model: Header/nav (probably from shared Layout), a SearchBar and FilterPanel for query controls, a results region rendering MatchCard[], pagination controls, and an empty/loading/error state. Possibly a sidebar for filters on desktop that collapses on mobile.

recall.q: Why mention the route layer (e.g. /match/:id) in your tree?
recall.model: It maps the component tree to URLs, which is also where URL state lives. Calling out routes early sets up the Step 4 state discussion and makes code-splitting boundaries obvious.

recall.q: How does a clear component tree help when you get to Step 4 (state design)?
recall.model: Once the tree is drawn, you can point at each subtree and say 'this state lives here' — local in the leaf, lifted to the page, in the URL, or in a server-state cache. Without the tree the state discussion becomes abstract.
keyPoints: state lives at the right level

scenarios.q: You drew a MatchDetailPage with VideoPlayer, StatsOverlay, ChatPanel. Fernando asks 'why these three?'
scenarios.model: Each is a distinct failure and update domain: VideoPlayer is heavy and may use a third-party SDK (isolate behind ErrorBoundary, lazy-load); StatsOverlay updates in real-time from WebSocket; ChatPanel is its own socket and moderation surface. Splitting them lets each have its own loading, error, and update strategy without coupling.

scenarios.q: Fernando says 'show me how you'd reuse the MatchCard component.' Walk through it.
scenarios.model: MatchCard takes a match prop and renders teams, score, status, time. It's used on the homepage (recent matches), MatchListPage (search results), team pages (team's matches), and player profiles (player's matches). Variants via props (compact vs full) rather than separate components. No data fetching inside — parent supplies data so the same card works in any context.

scenarios.q: How would you handle a ChatPanel that may not be available (server down)?
scenarios.model: Wrap it in an ErrorBoundary with a friendly fallback ('chat unavailable'). Lazy-load the chat code so a 404 on its bundle doesn't break the page. Keep the rest of the page (video, stats) fully functional. Surface the connection state in the UI and retry quietly in the background.

mcq.q: Where should auth tokens live in the browser?
options: localStorage so they survive refreshes
sessionStorage so they're scoped to the tab
HttpOnly Secure SameSite cookies set by the server
Redux store
mcq.explanation: HttpOnly cookies are unreadable from JavaScript, so an XSS payload can't exfiltrate them. Secure restricts to HTTPS, SameSite mitigates CSRF. localStorage is JS-readable and a single XSS empties your auth.

mcq.q: Which state belongs in the URL?
options: Whether a dropdown is currently open
The text the user is typing into a chat input
Search query, filters, sort, and current page on a results view
The user's auth token
mcq.explanation: Anything the user should be able to share, bookmark, or reload into goes in the URL — search, filters, pagination. Transient UI state (dropdown open) and secrets (auth) do not.

mcq.q: Why use React Query (or SWR) for server state instead of useState + useEffect?
options: It's smaller in bundle size
It gives you caching, background refetch, request dedup, and stale-while-revalidate for free
It replaces Redux and React Router
It runs on the server
mcq.explanation: Server state has unique needs — caching, dedup, invalidation, background sync, stale-while-revalidate. Hand-rolling these on top of useEffect is where bugs and race conditions live.

mcq.q: What does 'stale-while-revalidate' mean?
options: Show cached data immediately and fetch a fresh copy in the background; swap when it arrives
Block the UI until fresh data loads
Always show a loading spinner
Disable caching
mcq.explanation: SWR shows the user something instantly (the stale cache) while quietly refreshing in the background. Perceived latency drops to zero on cached views.

mcq.q: You implement an optimistic 'favorite this match' button. The server returns an error. What should happen?
options: Leave the optimistic state and hope the user retries
Roll back the optimistic update and surface an error to the user
Force a full page reload
Log out the user
mcq.explanation: Optimistic updates require a rollback path on failure plus user feedback. Without rollback the UI lies about the server state — worse than just showing a spinner.

mcq.q: Why prefer cursor pagination over offset pagination for an infinite-scroll match feed?
options: Offset is deprecated in HTTP
Cursors are stable when items are inserted/deleted; offset can skip or duplicate items and gets slow at deep pages
Cursors use less bandwidth always
Offset doesn't work on Postgres
mcq.explanation: With offset, an insert at the top shifts every page boundary, causing duplicates or skips on the next page. Cursors anchor to a specific row so concurrent inserts don't break pagination, and they avoid expensive OFFSET N scans.

mcq.q: When IS Redux actually a good choice?
options: Always — it's the React standard
When you have truly global cross-cutting client state with complex update logic shared across many distant components
For server state caching
For form state
mcq.explanation: Redux earns its weight only when many distant components share complex client state. For server state use React Query, for URL state use the router, for form state use a form library — Redux is usually overkill.

mcq.q: A WebSocket pushes a kill event for a match the user is viewing. The match data is also in the React Query cache. What's the correct pattern?
options: Ignore the cache and re-render from raw socket state
Refetch the entire match from REST on every event
Apply the event to the cached entry via setQueryData (or equivalent) so React Query stays the source of truth
Store the event in localStorage
mcq.explanation: Reconciling real-time updates into the existing cache keeps one source of truth. Components reading the cache re-render automatically, REST refetch as a fallback covers reconnection drift.

recall.q: Walk through the five categories of state and where each lives.
recall.model: Server state -> React Query/SWR cache (caching, refetch, dedup). Client state -> useState/Context (local UI). URL state -> router (shareable, bookmarkable). Persistent state -> cookies for auth, localStorage for prefs (survives refresh). Real-time state -> WebSocket/SSE reconciled into the server-state cache.

recall.q: Why is localStorage the wrong place for auth tokens?
recall.model: localStorage is readable from any JavaScript on the page, so a single XSS — even from a third-party script — exfiltrates the token. HttpOnly cookies are invisible to JS, so XSS can't steal them; Secure forces HTTPS, SameSite mitigates CSRF.

recall.q: Explain optimistic updates and the rollback path.
recall.model: On user action, mutate the local cache immediately so the UI feels instant, then fire the request. On success, replace optimistic data with the server response. On failure, roll back to the previous cache snapshot and surface an error toast. React Query's onMutate/onError hooks formalize this.

recall.q: Why is URL state often underrated?
recall.model: It's the only state that's shareable, bookmarkable, survives refresh, and works with browser back/forward for free. Putting filters in component state breaks all of those. Also it's the only state your support team can ask a user to copy and paste.

recall.q: Cursor vs offset pagination — when is each appropriate?
recall.model: Cursor pagination wins for live, frequently-changing feeds (matches, chat, social) because it's stable under inserts and cheap at any depth. Offset is fine for static or rarely-changing data with jump-to-page UX (admin tables, archive). The wrong choice causes duplicate/missing rows or slow deep queries.

recall.q: Why is reconciling WebSocket events into the React Query cache better than maintaining a parallel store?
recall.model: One source of truth means components don't have to choose which store to read or merge two views. Cache invalidation, refetch on focus, and SWR all keep working for free. A parallel store doubles state and creates merge bugs at boundaries.

recall.q: What's the danger of putting server state in Redux?
recall.model: You end up reimplementing caching, dedup, invalidation, refetch-on-focus, and stale-while-revalidate by hand — every one a known footgun. React Query exists because every Redux app rebuilt this badly. Redux is for client state, not API responses.

recall.q: How does stale-while-revalidate change perceived performance?
recall.model: Repeat views show instantly from cache (stale data) while a background fetch revalidates. The user sees content in 0ms instead of waiting for a network round trip; if data changed, it updates seamlessly. This collapses spinner-driven UX into instant navigation.

recall.q: Why lift form state up to a form library (e.g. React Hook Form) instead of useState per field?
recall.model: Form libraries handle validation, dirty tracking, error display, submission state, and re-render minimization in one place. Per-field useState scatters logic and re-renders the whole form on every keystroke. For complex forms the library is a clear win; for a single search input, useState is fine.

recall.q: Auth lives in HttpOnly cookies. How does the SPA know if the user is logged in?
recall.model: Hit a /me (or similar) endpoint on app load — the cookie is sent automatically and the server returns the user record or 401. Cache the result in React Query so children components read user info synchronously. Never try to read the token itself in JS.

scenarios.q: Design state for a Worlds bracket page that supports filtering by region and showing live match scores.
scenarios.model: URL state: selected region, expanded round (shareable). Server state: bracket structure and completed matches in React Query, long staleTime since brackets change rarely. Real-time state: live match scores via WebSocket, reconciled into the React Query cache via setQueryData on each event so the same MatchCard component reads one source. Client state: hover/expand interactions in useState. Persistent: theme and language in cookies; auth in HttpOnly cookie. No Redux needed.

scenarios.q: User clicks 'Predict winner' on an upcoming match. Walk through the state flow.
scenarios.model: Optimistically update the React Query cache for that match's prediction so the UI flips instantly. Fire the mutation. On success, replace optimistic data with server response. On failure, roll back to the snapshot and toast 'prediction failed, try again.' Invalidate the user's predictions list query so other views refresh. If the user is on a slow network, the optimistic UI hides the latency entirely.

scenarios.q: WebSocket disconnects during a live match. How do you reconcile state on reconnect?
scenarios.model: On disconnect, mark the connection state in UI ('reconnecting...'). On reconnect, refetch the affected match query from REST to get the canonical current state — that snapshot fills any events missed during the gap. Then resume processing live events into the cache. This pattern (snapshot + stream) is the standard fix for missed-event reconciliation.

scenarios.q: Fernando: 'Why not just use Redux for everything? It's one mental model.'
scenarios.model: Redux is one mental model but the wrong one for several state types. Server state needs caching/refetch/dedup — React Query does this far better. URL state needs to live in the URL or it's not shareable. Auth needs to live in HttpOnly cookies or XSS wins. Form state has its own libraries. Redux for global client state is fine; Redux for everything reimplements four other libraries badly and ships more code.

mcq.q: When designing the API contract during a frontend system design interview, what is the most appropriate framing to open with?
options: Define every backend microservice boundary so the contract is unambiguous
Define the interface from the frontend's perspective — what data is needed and in what shape
Pick REST vs GraphQL based on personal preference and defend it for the rest of the round
Skip the contract and let the backend team decide after launch
mcq.explanation: Step 5 is explicitly framed as designing what the frontend needs, not designing the backend. Leading with 'from the frontend's perspective' signals product-engineer thinking and keeps the conversation in your wheelhouse.

mcq.q: For a live esports match list where matches start, finish, and re-sort constantly, which pagination strategy is most appropriate?
options: Offset-based pagination (page=1&limit=20) for stable URLs
Cursor-based pagination because items shift between requests
Load all matches in one call and paginate client-side
Random sampling to avoid hot keys on the backend
mcq.explanation: Offset pagination on a shifting list causes duplicates and skips because rows move between requests. Cursors anchor to a stable item, so the next page picks up correctly even when new matches are inserted.

mcq.q: Why is GraphQL often a strong default for a Riot-style esports product?
options: It is faster than REST on the wire in every case
It avoids needing a CDN because queries are dynamic
Multiple clients (web, mobile, OBS overlays) need different shapes of deeply nested data, and one schema can serve all of them
It removes the need for authentication
mcq.explanation: GraphQL shines when you have heterogeneous clients pulling different slices of nested domain data. For a flat CRUD app, REST with cache headers is often simpler — the answer is about fit, not fashion.

mcq.q: What is the right transport choice for live match score updates if you want bi-directional capability and broad infrastructure support?
options: Long-polling every 500ms
GraphQL subscriptions over WebSocket, with SSE as a fallback
Webhooks delivered to the browser
FTP polling
mcq.explanation: WebSocket gives you full-duplex, low-latency delivery; SSE is a simpler unidirectional fallback for environments that block WS. Subscriptions wrap this in your existing GraphQL schema so the client API stays uniform.

mcq.q: Which error response shape best supports a frontend that wants to render field-level validation and a global toast?
options: Plain HTTP status code only
{ error: { code, message, field? } } — standardized envelope
A free-form HTML error page
Stack trace serialized as JSON
mcq.explanation: A standardized envelope with a machine-readable code, human message, and optional field lets the UI branch cleanly: code drives behavior (retry vs logout), message drives display, field drives form highlighting.

recall.q: Why design the API contract from the frontend's perspective rather than the backend's?
recall.model: Because the frontend's job in this interview is to advocate for the user experience. Designing from the frontend out forces you to ask 'what does the screen need?' which prevents under-fetching (waterfalls) and over-fetching (wasted bytes). It also surfaces real product questions early — pagination shape, error semantics, real-time needs — instead of inheriting whatever the backend happens to expose.
keyPoints: Keeps the conversation in your domain
Backend can be designed to serve the contract, not vice versa

recall.q: When would you prefer REST over GraphQL even on a Fernando-style team?
recall.model: When the data is flat and CRUD-shaped, when you need aggressive HTTP/CDN caching with simple max-age headers, when the operation is a single resource fetch (e.g., a static team logo manifest), or when the consumers are homogeneous and don't need flexible shapes. REST + cache headers is also easier to debug in DevTools and cheaper to operate.

recall.q: Why prefer cursor pagination for real-time lists?
recall.model: Offset pagination assumes the underlying ordered list is stable. In a live matches list, items are inserted and reordered between page fetches, so offset 20 on page 2 might show items the user already saw on page 1, or skip new ones. Cursors anchor to an item ID + sort key, so 'after this cursor' is well-defined regardless of how the list mutates.
keyPoints: Cursor anchors to a stable item

recall.q: Why standardize error response shapes across all endpoints?
recall.model: A consistent shape lets the frontend implement one error boundary, one toast pipeline, and one retry policy. Without it, every screen has to special-case error parsing. A code field enables programmatic branching (e.g., AUTH_EXPIRED -> refresh), message enables display, and field enables inline form validation. It also makes observability easier — you can group by code in logs.
keyPoints: One error handler instead of per-screen logic
code drives behavior, message drives display

recall.q: How do you decide what fields to include in a GraphQL query for a match detail view?
recall.model: Start from the design — every visible piece of data corresponds to a field. Group nested data (teams, score, games) under their natural parent so the resolver can be efficient. Avoid selecting fields you might want later — GraphQL's whole point is asking for exactly what you render. If two views need different shapes, write two queries; don't bloat one to serve both.

recall.q: Why call out the contract before drawing data flow?
recall.model: Because the contract pins down the units the data flow has to move. If you walk the click-to-screen path before deciding the shape, you end up hand-waving 'and then we get the data'. Defining the query/mutation first lets Step 6 talk concretely about what gets cached, what gets invalidated, and what the optimistic update looks like.
keyPoints: Contract defines the units of data flow

scenarios.q: Fernando says: 'OK, you've picked GraphQL. But our CDN team wants this cacheable at the edge. How do you reconcile that?' Walk through your answer.
scenarios.model: I'd use persisted queries — at build time, the client queries are hashed and registered with the server, so the runtime request becomes GET /graphql?id=abc123&variables=... instead of a POST. GETs are cacheable by any HTTP CDN with a Cache-Control header. For per-user data I'd add Vary: Authorization or route through an auth-aware edge worker. Mutations and subscriptions stay on POST/WS and are not cached. This gives me GraphQL ergonomics with REST-style edge caching.
keyPoints: Vary or edge worker for auth

scenarios.q: You're designing the API for a prediction submission. How do you shape the request and response to support optimistic UI?
scenarios.model: Request: POST /predictions { matchId, teamId, clientRequestId }. The clientRequestId is a UUID I generate so I can dedupe retries and reconcile the optimistic record with the server's canonical version. Response: { prediction: { id, matchId, teamId, createdAt, clientRequestId } }. The frontend writes the optimistic prediction into cache keyed by clientRequestId, then on success replaces it with the server record (matching on clientRequestId), and on error rolls it back. Status codes: 201 success, 409 if already predicted, 401 for auth.
keyPoints: Distinct status codes for distinct UI branches

scenarios.q: An interviewer asks: 'What if your live match query returns 50 fields but only 5 change every second? Doesn't that waste bandwidth on subscriptions?' How do you respond?
scenarios.model: Good catch — I'd split it. The initial GetMatch query fetches the full shape once. Then OnMatchUpdate subscription returns only the volatile fields: score, status, liveStats. The Apollo/Relay cache merges the subscription payload into the existing match object by ID, so the UI updates without refetching the static fields like team logos and player photos. This keeps each subscription frame tiny and the WebSocket cheap.

mcq.q: What is the purpose of the Step 6 data flow walkthrough?
options: To enumerate every API in the system
To trace one critical user flow from click to screen, including state, cache, and error handling
To rewrite the architecture diagram in more detail
To talk about deployment topology
mcq.explanation: Step 6 is about depth on one path, not breadth. Picking the most important flow and walking it end-to-end demonstrates that you actually know how the pieces interact under your design.

mcq.q: In an optimistic update flow for submitting a prediction, what should happen on server error?
options: Leave the optimistic UI in place and hope the user doesn't notice
Roll back the optimistic update and surface an error with a retry action
Force a full page reload
Log out the user
mcq.explanation: Optimistic updates are a contract: we promise to roll back if the server disagrees. Rolling back plus a clear error with retry preserves trust and gives the user agency.

mcq.q: After a successful mutation, why invalidate the relevant React Query keys instead of manually patching the cache everywhere?
options: Invalidation is faster than patching
Invalidation marks dependent queries stale and lets React Query refetch in the background, keeping the UI consistent without you tracking every consumer
Manual patching is forbidden by React Query
Invalidation triggers a full page reload
mcq.explanation: Invalidation is a declarative way of saying 'this data changed' and lets the cache figure out who needs to know. Manual patching is fine for hot paths but doesn't scale to many consumers.

mcq.q: Why is it important to mention side effects (URL update, analytics, notifications) in the data flow walkthrough?
options: It pads the answer to the time budget
It shows you think about the full product surface, not just the happy-path render
Analytics is the most important part of any system
URL updates are required for SEO on every action
mcq.explanation: Senior frontend engineers think beyond pixels. Mentioning URL state, analytics, and notifications signals that you understand the flow has consequences outside the component.

recall.q: Why pick the most critical flow rather than walking through several?
recall.model: Time pressure, and depth-over-breadth signals seniority. If you walk three flows shallowly, you sound like you're enumerating; if you walk one flow deeply — including loading, error, race conditions, side effects — you demonstrate you actually know how your design behaves. The interviewer can always ask about a second flow if they want.

recall.q: What is the difference between optimistic UI and a loading spinner, and when do you use each?
recall.model: A loading spinner is honest: we don't know yet, please wait. It's right when failure is likely, when the action is destructive or slow, or when the user needs the server's answer to proceed. Optimistic UI predicts the success state and applies it immediately. It's right when failure is rare, the action is small and reversible, and latency hurts UX (likes, predictions, toggles). Optimistic without rollback handling is a bug, not a feature.
keyPoints: Optimistic = rare failure, small reversible ops

recall.q: Why should you mention cache invalidation as a distinct step in the data flow?
recall.model: Because forgetting it is the most common bug in client-cached apps. The mutation succeeded, the server has the new state, but every other screen still shows the old data because nothing told the cache. Calling out invalidation explicitly proves you've thought about consistency across views, not just the one screen you mutated from.
keyPoints: Alternative is manual cache patching for hot paths

recall.q: Why update the URL as part of certain user flows?
recall.model: URL is shareable state. If a user predicts a match and then sends the link to a friend, the friend should see the same match in the same state. URL also enables back-button correctness — if state is only in component memory, navigation breaks. For filters, modals, and selections that the user might want to share or return to, URL is the right home.

recall.q: What does it mean to 'cancel stale requests on navigation' and why does it matter?
recall.model: If a user navigates from /match/1 to /match/2 quickly, the request for match 1 may resolve after match 2's request. Without cancellation, the late response can overwrite the new screen — a race condition. AbortController on the fetch (and React Query handles this for you on query key change) cancels in-flight requests on unmount or key change, so only the latest data wins.
keyPoints: React Query handles via query keys

scenarios.q: Walk Fernando through the data flow for a user clicking 'Predict T1' on a live match card.
scenarios.model: User clicks the PredictionButton. onClick fires and calls a mutation hook (React Query useMutation). Before the network request, I apply an optimistic update: write { matchId, teamId: 'T1', pending: true } into the cache for ['predictions', matchId], so the card immediately renders 'T1 selected'. The mutation fires POST /predictions with a clientRequestId. On success, the response replaces the optimistic record, I invalidate ['predictions', matchId] and ['user', 'predictions'] so other screens (profile, history) refetch. On error, I roll back the optimistic write, show an error toast with a Retry button, and log the failure to analytics. I also update the URL to ?predicted=true so the action survives a refresh and is shareable. Throughout, the button is disabled while pending and announces 'Prediction submitted' via aria-live for screen readers.

scenarios.q: An interviewer asks: 'What if the WebSocket pushes a score update while the user is mid-mutation on the same match? How does the data flow handle that?'
scenarios.model: These two flows touch different fields of the same cache entry. The mutation is updating predictions; the WS is updating score. As long as my cache merge is field-level (Apollo's normalized cache or a hand-rolled reducer that merges by ID), both updates apply cleanly without clobbering each other. If they did touch the same field, last-write-wins by server timestamp, with the server as the source of truth — and I'd reconcile on the next refetch. The key is that optimistic state is namespaced by clientRequestId so a concurrent server push can't corrupt it.

mcq.q: Which loading optimization gives the biggest first-paint win on a Riot-scale landing page with many routes?
options: Inlining every JavaScript module
Route-level code splitting with React.lazy + Suspense so users only download the bundle for the page they hit
Switching from React to vanilla JS
Removing all images
mcq.explanation: Route splitting is the highest-leverage loading optimization because it directly cuts the bytes a first-time visitor must download. The other levers (preload, image optimization, critical CSS) compound on top of it.

mcq.q: When does React.memo actually help performance?
options: Always — wrap every component in React.memo
When a component is expensive to render and its props are referentially stable across parent re-renders
Only when the component has no children
Only in class components
mcq.explanation: React.memo skips re-renders when props are shallow-equal. It's only useful if (a) the render is actually expensive and (b) props don't change identity every render. Wrapping cheap components or components with always-new props makes things slower, not faster.

mcq.q: Why virtualize a long list of completed matches with react-window instead of just rendering all 5000 rows?
options: react-window is required by React for any list
DOM nodes are expensive to create and maintain; virtualizing renders only the visible window plus a small overscan, keeping memory and render time bounded regardless of list size
It improves SEO
It enables server-side rendering
mcq.explanation: Virtualization decouples list size from DOM size. With 5000 rows, react-window may only have 20 nodes in the DOM at any time, so scroll perf and memory stay flat as the list grows.

mcq.q: What is stale-while-revalidate, and why is it a good fit for live match data?
options: A backend rate-limiting strategy
A cache strategy where the cached response is returned immediately and a background refetch refreshes it for next time — perfect for data that should feel instant but stay fresh
A WebSocket protocol
A way to delete stale tokens
mcq.explanation: SWR delivers instant UI from cache while quietly updating in the background. For match data the user expects instant load and freshness within seconds — SWR plus a WebSocket for true live deltas hits both.

mcq.q: Why batch WebSocket-driven UI updates with requestAnimationFrame instead of setting state on every message?
options: requestAnimationFrame is required by WebSocket
If 100 messages/sec each trigger a setState, React re-renders 100 times per second. rAF coalesces them into one update per frame (~60fps), which is the maximum the screen can show anyway
It encrypts the messages
It avoids the need for a cache
mcq.explanation: The screen can only show 60 frames per second (or 120 on high-refresh displays). Re-rendering more often is wasted CPU. rAF batches messages into the next paint window, preserving freshness without thrashing React.

mcq.q: Which work belongs in a Web Worker rather than the main thread?
options: DOM updates
CPU-heavy parsing or transformation (e.g., decoding a large match-history JSON, computing aggregate stats) that would otherwise block input and rendering
React state updates
Reading from localStorage
mcq.explanation: Workers are for CPU-bound, non-DOM work. Anything that touches the DOM or React state must stay on the main thread. Big parses, transforms, and crypto are the canonical wins.

mcq.q: Why prefetch the next likely route's data on link hover/focus?
options: To reduce server load
To turn perceived navigation latency into zero — by the time the user clicks, the data is already in cache
Because React requires it
To avoid using a CDN
mcq.explanation: Hover/focus is a strong intent signal that lands ~200ms before click. Prefetching during that window means the click resolves instantly. React Router and Next.js both expose this.

mcq.q: What is the key insight about caching tiers at Worlds-scale traffic?
options: Everything must be real-time or the experience feels broken

Most data is cacheable (logos, photos, completed match results); only live game state needs real-time delivery, so you can absorb huge traffic on the CDN and only spend WebSocket capacity on the volatile slice
You should cache nothing because freshness matters
You should cache only at the database layer
mcq.explanation: This is the Fernando-bait answer. Recognizing that ~95% of payload bytes are static-ish (assets, completed matches, team metadata) lets you serve them from CDN edges and reserve scarce WebSocket fan-out for the small live slice.

recall.q: Why is bundle size budget worth setting and enforcing in CI?
recall.model: Without a budget, bundles only grow. Every PR that adds a dependency or a 'small' helper compounds, and a year later first paint is 4 seconds on mobile. A budget enforced in CI (e.g., main bundle < 200KB gzipped) makes regressions visible at the moment they're introduced, when they're cheap to fix. It also forces conversations about whether a new dependency is worth its weight — the kind of conversation senior engineers want to have.
keyPoints: CI catches regressions at PR time

recall.q: Why prefer responsive srcset + WebP/AVIF over a single high-resolution JPEG?
recall.model: A single high-res image punishes everyone — mobile users download desktop bytes, modern browsers download legacy formats. srcset + sizes lets the browser pick the right resolution for the actual viewport and DPR, often cutting download by 4-10x. WebP/AVIF compress 25-50% smaller than JPEG at the same quality. Combined with lazy loading below the fold, the first-screen image bytes can drop by an order of magnitude.

recall.q: Why inline critical CSS but load the rest async?
recall.model: Render-blocking CSS delays first paint until the stylesheet downloads and parses. Critical CSS — the rules for above-the-fold content — inlined in the HTML head means the first paint can happen immediately, with no extra network round trip. The rest of the CSS loads async via <link rel='preload' as='style'> + onload swap, so it's ready by the time the user scrolls. Net effect: faster perceived load without losing styling.
keyPoints: Inline critical = no extra RTT for first paint

recall.q: When is useMemo a footgun rather than a win?
recall.model: When the memoized computation is cheaper than the equality check and bookkeeping it adds. Memoizing { x: 1 } or 'a' + 'b' is pure overhead. Worse, if the dependency array includes a value that changes every render (an inline object or function), the memo never hits and you've added cost with no benefit. Use useMemo when the computation is genuinely expensive (sorts, large maps, derived data structures) and the deps are stable.

recall.q: Why debounce search input by ~300ms instead of firing on every keystroke?
recall.model: Each keystroke firing a request wastes server capacity, costs the user bandwidth, and creates race conditions where an earlier slow response overwrites a later one. 300ms is short enough to feel instant when the user pauses, long enough to skip the 5 keystrokes between 'val' and 'valorant'. Combined with cancelling in-flight requests on each new keystroke, you get one request per pause and one render per result.

recall.q: Why use a single multiplexed WebSocket instead of one per data stream?
recall.model: Browsers cap concurrent WebSocket connections per origin (around 200 in Chrome but practically much lower with intermediaries), and each connection costs RAM, file descriptors, and TLS handshake time on both ends. One WebSocket carrying multiple logical channels (subscribe to match:123, match:456, chat:lobby) gives you the same product capability with one connection's worth of overhead. This is how GraphQL subscriptions and most real-time platforms work.
keyPoints: TLS and RAM cost per connection

recall.q: Why use exponential backoff for WebSocket reconnection?
recall.model: If a server is down and 10 million clients all retry every second, the server can never recover — it gets thundering-herded as soon as it boots. Exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, capped) plus jitter spreads the retry storm across time, giving the server room to come up. From the user's POV the cost is small because you're showing 'reconnecting...' anyway. It's a courtesy to your own infra.

recall.q: Why show connection status to the user during a live event?
recall.model: During a live match, users need to know whether what they're seeing is current. If the WebSocket dropped and they're staring at a 30-second-old score, that's worse than knowing the connection is down — they might bet, predict, or share based on stale data. A small banner ('Live' / 'Reconnecting...' / 'Offline — showing cached') turns silent failure into honest feedback and is the difference between a trustworthy product and a confusing one.
keyPoints: Stale data is worse than known offline

recall.q: Why are persisted GraphQL queries good for CDN caching at Riot scale?
recall.model: Default GraphQL is POST with the query in the body, which CDNs can't cache. With persisted queries the client sends an ID (a hash) instead of the query text, so the request becomes GET /graphql?id=abc123&variables=... — a cacheable URL. The CDN can serve it from any edge, and only auth-varying or volatile queries hit the origin. At Worlds scale this is the difference between a few origin servers and a few hundred.
keyPoints: POST is uncacheable by HTTP CDNs

recall.q: What does 'caching in tiers' mean and why is it the answer for Riot-scale traffic?
recall.model: Tiered caching means data lives in multiple layers, each closer and faster than the next: browser memory (React Query / Apollo cache, microseconds), service worker / disk (milliseconds), CDN edge (tens of ms), origin Redis (low hundreds of ms), database (slowest). Each tier absorbs traffic the next can't handle. At Worlds, the browser cache absorbs repeats, the CDN absorbs cold visitors to static pages, Redis absorbs the live queries, and Postgres only sees writes. Done right, no single tier is the bottleneck.
keyPoints: Multiple layers from in-memory to DB

scenarios.q: Fernando says: 'Worlds finals — 5 million concurrent viewers on this match page. Walk me through everything you'd do to keep it fast.'
scenarios.model: First, recognize the bytes. Most of what's on the page — team logos, player photos, the match metadata, the bracket — is cacheable. I'd put all static assets behind a CDN with hashed filenames and year-long max-age. The match shell page is server-rendered and edge-cached with a short TTL or stale-while-revalidate. Persisted GraphQL queries make read traffic cacheable at the edge too, so 95% of HTTP requests never reach origin. Loading: route-split bundles, critical CSS inlined, hero image with srcset+WebP, font-display: swap, bundle budget enforced in CI. Runtime: virtualize the play-by-play list, memoize the scoreboard, debounce any search. Real-time: one WebSocket per client multiplexing match score, chat, and stats, with rAF-batched UI updates so 100 msgs/sec become 60 paints/sec. Reconnection uses exponential backoff with jitter to avoid thundering the WS gateway after a blip. Degradation: if the WS layer is overloaded, fall back to 5-second polling so the page still updates. Capacity-wise, the WebSocket fan-out is the scary part — I'd shard subscribers across many edge nodes that each subscribe once to the upstream and fan out locally, so 5M clients become a few hundred upstream subs.
keyPoints: Degradation to polling on WS overload

scenarios.q: An interviewer says: 'Your bundle is 1.2MB gzipped. The product team won't cut features. What do you do?'
scenarios.model: First, measure where it's coming from with source-map-explorer or webpack-bundle-analyzer — the answer is always concentrated in a few culprits, not spread evenly. Common wins: replace moment with date-fns or Temporal (~70KB saved), import lodash functions individually instead of the whole library, lazy-load heavy editor/chart libraries that only some routes use, drop polyfills for browsers we don't support, deduplicate React versions if a dep brought its own. Then route-split: every route gets its own chunk via React.lazy, so no user downloads code for pages they don't visit. Set a CI budget at the new size so we don't regress. If after all that we're still over, I'd negotiate with product on a feature that costs the most KB per user — but it almost never gets that far.
keyPoints: Replace moment, fix lodash, drop polyfills

scenarios.q: Fernando asks: 'Your match page is dropping frames during heavy WebSocket traffic. How do you debug and fix it?'
scenarios.model: I'd start in the Performance tab of DevTools, recording 5 seconds of activity. The flame chart usually shows one of three culprits: too many React renders (you'll see stacks of reconciliation work), expensive renders (one component dominating), or main thread work outside React (parsing big JSON payloads). For too-many-renders: batch WebSocket updates with requestAnimationFrame so 100 msgs/sec become at most 60 setState/sec, and split global state so unrelated subtrees don't re-render together. For expensive renders: profile with React DevTools, add React.memo on hot leaves with stable props, virtualize long lists. For parsing: move JSON.parse and any aggregation into a Web Worker so the main thread stays free for paint and input. After each fix, re-measure — never optimize without numbers.
keyPoints: Performance tab + React DevTools profiler first
Split global state to localize re-renders

scenarios.q: You're told: 'For 10x the current users, what would you change?' How do you answer?
scenarios.model: Three buckets. One: caching. Push more reads to the edge via persisted GraphQL queries, longer CDN TTLs on semi-static data (completed matches, team rosters), and an in-memory tier (Redis) in front of the database for live queries. Two: edge compute. Move things like personalization, A/B test bucketing, and auth verification to edge workers (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge) so origins only see writes and uncacheable reads. Three: real-time fan-out. Shard the WebSocket layer regionally with a tree topology — one upstream subscription per region feeding many local subscribers — so growth is logarithmic in clients, not linear. On the data side, shard hot tables by tournament or region so no single DB node is the bottleneck. And I'd add observability everywhere — at 10x you can't debug what you can't see.

mcq.q: Why prefer skeleton screens over spinners for data-dependent views?
options: Spinners are forbidden by accessibility guidelines
Skeletons mimic the final layout, so the user perceives faster load and content doesn't shift in when it arrives
Skeletons load faster on the network
Spinners only work in Chrome
mcq.explanation: Skeletons pre-reserve space matching the real content. The user's eye locks onto the layout and the actual data slots in without jank. They feel faster than spinners even when the wall-clock time is identical.

mcq.q: What is the right pattern for handling a 401 Unauthorized response globally?
options: Show an error toast and let the user retry manually
Intercept 401s in a single auth interceptor, attempt a token refresh, and only redirect to login if the refresh itself fails
Reload the page
Log the user out immediately on the first 401
mcq.explanation: Centralizing 401 handling avoids per-call branching, makes refresh-once-and-replay possible, and prevents the harsh UX of yanking the user to login when their token simply expired silently.

mcq.q: Why use AbortController to cancel stale requests on navigation or new search input?
options: It saves the server money
It prevents a slow earlier response from overwriting a fast later one — a classic race condition
It's required by React
It encrypts the request
mcq.explanation: Network is non-deterministic. Without cancellation, response order can be the opposite of request order, and the late response wins. AbortController lets you say 'I no longer care about this request,' and React Query does this automatically on key change.

mcq.q: Why announce live score updates with aria-live regions for screen readers?
options: Because aria-live improves SEO
Because visual updates are invisible to screen reader users; aria-live tells assistive tech 'this region changed, announce it' so blind users get the same live experience
Because all React apps require it
Because it speeds up rendering
mcq.explanation: Live esports without live announcements excludes a real audience. aria-live='polite' on the score region means screen readers announce changes without interrupting current speech, giving parity to the non-visual experience.

mcq.q: What is the 'fifth state' the prep document recommends for live event apps, beyond loading/success/error/empty?
options: Authenticated
Degraded — real-time connection lost but falling back to polling
Mobile
Server-rendered
mcq.explanation: Live apps fail in a way generic apps don't: the connection dies but the data is still sort of usable via polling. Naming this as a distinct state lets you design for it intentionally — banner, slower update cadence, clear messaging.

recall.q: Why design for four states per view (loading, success, error, empty) instead of just success?
recall.model: Because three out of four times the user lands on a screen, they're not in the success state. They might be waiting on data, hitting an error, or seeing nothing because they have no items yet. Skipping any of these states means a real user will land on a broken-looking page. Designing all four upfront forces conversations with design and product, and is a cheap way to look senior in an interview.
keyPoints: Real users hit non-success states constantly
Skipping any one is a visible bug

recall.q: Why is 'Something went wrong' a bad error message?
recall.model: It tells the user nothing they didn't already know. They can't tell if it's their fault, your fault, transient, or fatal. They don't know what to do next. A good error message names the problem ('Couldn't load matches'), suggests an action ('Check your connection or'), and provides one ('try again'). It also helps you triage support tickets because users can quote a specific message.

recall.q: Why queue mutations for retry when offline instead of failing them?
recall.model: Because the user's intent doesn't change just because their connection blipped. If they tapped 'predict T1' on the subway, they meant it, and surfacing 'failed' makes them tap again later — except they've already moved on. Queuing the mutation in IndexedDB with the clientRequestId, then replaying when online, preserves intent and survives even a tab close. The clientRequestId prevents double-submit if the original request actually made it through.

recall.q: Why is internationalization an edge case worth mentioning, especially for Riot?
recall.model: Riot ships in dozens of locales, including RTL languages and ones with very different text widths (German is 30% wider than English; Japanese is much narrower; Arabic flows right-to-left and mirrors layouts). A button that fits 'Predict' in English may break in German. A leaderboard sorted left-to-right looks wrong in Arabic. Designing for i18n upfront — flexible widths, logical CSS properties (margin-inline-start), locale-aware date/number formatting, RTL-safe icons — avoids a rewrite later. Mentioning it in a Riot interview signals you know the company.
keyPoints: Riot ships in many locales, including RTL

recall.q: Why focus management on route changes is an a11y must, not nice-to-have?
recall.model: When a sighted user navigates, their eye jumps to the new content. A keyboard or screen reader user has no such cue — focus stays on whatever it was on before, which might be unmounted, leaving focus on document.body. They have no idea where they are. Programmatically moving focus to the new page's main heading on route change restores parity, lets screen readers announce the new context, and unblocks keyboard navigation.
keyPoints: Sighted users have visual jump cue
Move focus to new page heading

recall.q: Why should the empty state have a CTA, not just 'No results'?
recall.model: Empty isn't a dead end — it's a moment. 'No matches today' is a wall. 'No matches today — set a notification for tomorrow' is a product. Empty states are where you teach users about the next action, and they're often the highest-engagement-per-pixel spot in the UI because the user has nothing else to look at. Skipping the CTA leaves user attention on the floor.
keyPoints: Empty is a moment, not a wall

scenarios.q: Walk through every state your match-detail page could be in and how you'd design each.
scenarios.model: Loading: skeleton with the same layout shape as the loaded view — placeholder boxes for team logos, score, stats panel — so when data arrives nothing shifts. Success: the real content. Error: a centered card with a title ('Couldn't load this match'), a likely cause if I can detect it from the error code, and a Retry button that re-runs the query — not a 'go home' button that throws away the user's intent. Empty: relevant when a match exists but has no data yet (pre-game). 'Match starts in 12 minutes — set a reminder.' Degraded: the WebSocket dropped but the initial fetch succeeded, so I show a yellow banner 'Live updates paused — reconnecting' and fall back to polling every 5s. Auth-expired: handled globally at the interceptor; the user briefly sees the loading state while a token refresh runs, transparent if successful. Offline: the same banner as degraded plus 'You're offline — showing cached data,' with mutations queued.
keyPoints: Empty is a CTA, not a wall

scenarios.q: An interviewer says: 'Tell me about a race condition in your design and how you handle it.'
scenarios.model: The classic one: a user types 'val' in search, triggering a query, then types 'valo' before the first response returns. If the 'val' response arrives after 'valo's, the UI shows results for 'val' even though the input says 'valo'. I handle this two ways. First, debounce the input (~300ms) so most fast typing fires only the final query. Second, AbortController on each new query — when 'valo' fires, the in-flight 'val' request is aborted. React Query does this for free if I use the input as part of the queryKey: when the key changes, the old query is cancelled and unsubscribed. I'd also make sure setState only happens for the still-current query, not via a stale closure.

scenarios.q: Fernando says: 'Your live match page is open in a tab the user hasn't touched in an hour. What should happen?'
scenarios.model: Two concerns: stale data and resource cost. The tab has been backgrounded, so requestAnimationFrame is throttled and the WebSocket may have been closed by the browser or the server. When the tab comes back to foreground (visibilitychange event), I'd: (1) check connection state, reconnect WS if needed with exponential backoff; (2) refetch the queries that have gone stale based on staleTime, which React Query does automatically with refetchOnWindowFocus; (3) show a brief loading or 'updating' indicator if the data is meaningfully behind. While the tab is backgrounded I'd actually want to back off updates — close the WS after a few minutes hidden, stop polling, save battery. The page is invisible; doing work for it is waste. visibilitychange is the lever for both.
keyPoints: Background tabs throttle rAF and may drop WS
refetchOnWindowFocus does this in React Query

mcq.q: Fernando asks: 'How does data get from the game server to the user's screen?' What is the canonical path you should describe?
options: Game server -> direct WebSocket to browser
Game server -> Kafka -> stream processor -> Redis -> WebSocket gateway -> browser UI
Game server -> SQL database -> browser polls every minute
Game server -> email -> browser
mcq.explanation: This is the pipeline the prep doc highlights as Fernando's favorite. Naming each hop (queue, processor, hot store, fan-out, client) shows you understand the full event flow, not just the frontend.

mcq.q: Fernando asks: 'What happens under heavy load?' What's the structure of a strong answer?
options: Promise it won't happen
Degradation strategy (graceful feature reduction), caching tiers (browser/CDN/Redis), and CDN absorption of static traffic
Add more backend servers and call it done
Move everything to serverless
mcq.explanation: Senior answers acknowledge that perfect uptime under load is a fantasy. You design for graceful degradation, push as much as possible to caches the user never sees, and let the CDN take the brunt. Adding servers alone doesn't solve fan-out problems.

mcq.q: Fernando asks: 'How would you test this?' What testing pyramid should you describe?
options: Manual QA only
Unit tests for components, integration tests for data flows, E2E tests for critical user paths, plus k6 load tests at the network layer
Just E2E tests because they cover everything
Snapshot tests for every file
mcq.explanation: The doc explicitly lists this stack. Each layer catches different bugs: units for logic, integration for cache/state interactions, E2E for the real browser flow, k6 for capacity. Skipping any layer leaves a class of bugs uncaught.

mcq.q: Fernando asks: 'What about cold starts?' What's the right framing?
options: Cold starts are a backend problem, ignore them
Provisioned concurrency for serverless, bundle size optimization for frontend, lazy module loading so the cold path is small
Ship more JavaScript to compensate
Always use dedicated VMs
mcq.explanation: Cold starts apply at every layer. Backend: keep functions warm. Frontend: minimize the bytes a first-time visitor must parse and execute. Lazy load anything not needed for first paint.

mcq.q: Fernando asks: 'What would change for 10x users?' What buckets should your answer hit?
options: Hire more engineers
More aggressive caching, edge compute, connection pooling, sharding (data and real-time fan-out)
Switch frameworks
Drop features
mcq.explanation: These are the doc's named buckets. Caching reduces work, edge compute moves work closer to users, pooling reuses expensive connections, and sharding spreads load across more nodes — including the WebSocket fan-out tree, which is often the actual bottleneck.

mcq.q: Fernando asks: 'Walk me through the backend.' What's the right opening move for a frontend candidate?
options: Refuse — you're a frontend candidate
Start with a box diagram: stateless app servers, DB choice (SQL vs NoSQL), Redis caching, message queues for async — and explain trade-offs at each box
Describe Kubernetes manifests
Talk about CI/CD only
mcq.explanation: Senior frontend engineers can sketch a competent backend even if they don't build it daily. Boxes + trade-offs at each box demonstrates fluency without overclaiming.

mcq.q: Fernando asks: 'How would you store this data?' What does a strong answer reference first?
options: Always use Postgres
Ask about the query patterns first — Postgres for relational, Redis sorted sets for leaderboards, Elasticsearch for search, S3 for blobs
Always use a NoSQL document store
Pick whatever the team already uses
mcq.explanation: Storage choices are downstream of access patterns. Asking about reads vs writes, latency budget, and shape signals you treat tools as means, not defaults.

mcq.q: Fernando asks: 'How would you make this performant?' What's the right first move?
options: List every optimization you know
Ask what 'performant' means — which metrics, on what device, at what percentile — then code-split, cache, virtualize, and measure
Rewrite in vanilla JS
Increase server capacity
mcq.explanation: Performance has no meaning without metrics. Asking 'LCP on mobile p75? interaction latency? bundle size?' shows you treat perf as engineering, not vibes. Then apply the standard levers and measure each change.

recall.q: Walk through the full event pipeline from game server to user's screen.
recall.model: Game server emits an event (kill, objective taken, score change). It writes to a Kafka topic, which acts as a durable buffer. A stream processor (Flink, Kafka Streams, or a custom consumer) reads the topic, aggregates and shapes the events into UI-ready records (current score, latest play), and writes the hot state into Redis. A WebSocket gateway subscribes to Redis pub/sub or polls Redis on a tick, then fans out the updates to all subscribed clients via persistent WebSocket connections. The client receives the message, merges it into the normalized cache by entity ID, and React re-renders the affected components. Each hop is independently scalable and a failure at any hop has a defined fallback (Kafka retains, Redis is rebuilt from Kafka, WS clients reconnect with backoff).

recall.q: What does 'graceful degradation' mean in the context of a live esports app under heavy load?
recall.model: It means the app keeps working but with reduced fidelity rather than crashing. Tier 1 degradation: WebSocket overloaded, fall back to polling every 5s; users see live updates a few seconds late instead of real-time. Tier 2: stats panel fails, show the score panel only. Tier 3: live page fails entirely, show the cached pre-match shell with a banner. Tier 4: cached HTML from CDN even when origin is down. The user always sees something, and what they see is honestly labeled. The opposite — full failure — is what you avoid by designing each feature with a fallback.
keyPoints: WS -> polling -> cached shell -> CDN HTML

recall.q: Why do unit, integration, E2E, and load tests catch different classes of bugs?
recall.model: Unit tests catch logic bugs in isolation: the wrong calculation, the missed branch. Integration tests catch wiring bugs: the cache doesn't invalidate on this mutation, the reducer ignores this action. E2E tests catch full-stack bugs: the API contract drifted, the auth flow breaks for new users, the deploy missed an env var. Load tests catch capacity bugs: the system works fine for one user but melts at 10K because of an N+1 query or a thundering reconnection storm. Each layer is cheap relative to what it catches, and skipping any layer means another layer has to absorb its bugs (usually production).
keyPoints: Skipping any layer = bugs in prod

recall.q: Why is bundle size optimization the frontend's answer to cold starts?
recall.model: Cold start on the frontend is the first-time visitor's first paint. They have nothing cached, so every byte you ship costs them parse and execute time on whatever device they're holding. Cutting bundle size — code-splitting routes, lazy-loading non-critical features, dropping polyfills for unsupported browsers — directly reduces the work the user's device does on the cold path. A 1MB bundle takes ~1 second to parse on a mid-range Android. A 200KB bundle takes ~200ms. Same product, 5x faster cold start.
keyPoints: Cold path = first-time visitor's first paint
Mid-range Android is the worst case
Route splitting, lazy load, drop polyfills

recall.q: Why is edge compute valuable when scaling to 10x users?
recall.model: Origin servers are a small number of locations; users are everywhere. Every request has to pay the round-trip latency to origin. Edge compute (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge) runs your code at hundreds of points-of-presence near users, so requests that can be handled at the edge — auth check, A/B bucketing, cache lookup, simple personalization — never travel further. This both reduces user-perceived latency and offloads origin, freeing it for the work that genuinely needs it. At 10x scale, the origin is the bottleneck; edge is how you stop scaling it.
keyPoints: Origin is few locations, users are everywhere
Lets origin focus on writes and uncacheable reads

recall.q: Why does Fernando like the question 'walk me through the backend' for a frontend candidate?
recall.model: It distinguishes a UI-only engineer from a systems-aware product engineer. A senior frontend engineer can name the boxes (app servers, DB, cache, queue), explain the trade-offs at each (SQL vs NoSQL, sync vs async, push vs pull), and connect them back to frontend concerns (latency budget, real-time fan-out, error semantics). A weak answer dodges or hand-waves; a strong answer is a five-minute box diagram with confident vocabulary. Fernando's background means he can tell instantly which one he's getting.
keyPoints: Box diagram + trade-offs at each box

recall.q: Why ask about query patterns before recommending a database?
recall.model: Because the same data can be stored in five different databases and the right answer depends entirely on how you read it. A leaderboard is trivial in Redis sorted sets and painful in Postgres; a relational join is trivial in Postgres and painful in DynamoDB; full-text search is trivial in Elasticsearch and painful in everything else. Asking 'what does the read look like?' is the senior reflex — it pins down the constraint that determines the right tool, instead of forcing the data into whatever you're comfortable with.
keyPoints: Same data, different stores per query pattern

recall.q: Why is 'measure first' the right reflex when asked about performance?
recall.model: Optimization without measurement is a guess, and guesses are usually wrong. The bottleneck you imagine is rarely the bottleneck you have. Measuring with real tools (Chrome DevTools Performance tab, Lighthouse, WebPageTest, RUM) tells you where the actual time goes, and lets you pick the highest-leverage fix instead of the most familiar one. It also gives you a baseline so you can prove your fix worked. In an interview, saying 'I'd measure first with X tool, then attack the biggest contributor' instantly raises your seniority signal.
keyPoints: Baseline lets you prove the fix

recall.q: How do connection pooling and sharding both help at 10x?
recall.model: Pooling reuses expensive resources. Opening a database or WS connection is slow (handshakes, auth) and bounded (servers cap concurrent connections). A pool keeps a small set of open connections and hands them out, so 10x request volume doesn't mean 10x connections. Sharding splits load across more nodes. If one DB or one WS gateway can handle 100K users, sharding by tournament ID or region lets 10 of them handle 1M users without any one being a hotspot. Pooling makes each node more efficient; sharding adds more nodes intelligently. You usually need both.

recall.q: Why mention observability when answering a 10x question?
recall.model: At small scale you can debug by reproducing the bug locally. At 10x you can't — the bug only happens for 0.1% of users at 3am in Korea, and it's gone before you can repro. Observability (structured logs, distributed tracing, metrics, real user monitoring) is how you debug what you can't see. Mentioning it in a scaling answer signals that you've actually operated systems at scale, not just designed them on paper. It's also where most production fires actually live — without observability you don't know which fire to fight first.

scenarios.q: Fernando asks: 'How does the data get from the game server to the user's screen?' Give a complete answer.
scenarios.model: I'd trace it hop by hop. The game server emits structured events into a Kafka topic — Kafka acts as a durable buffer so a slow downstream doesn't backpressure the game. A stream processor (say, Flink or a custom Node consumer) reads the topic, performs any aggregation or shaping the UI needs (current score, recent kills, gold differential), and writes the result into Redis as the hot state for that match. A WebSocket gateway subscribes to Redis pub/sub on the relevant keys; when state changes, it pushes the delta to every subscribed client over their persistent WebSocket connection. The client receives the message, merges it into the normalized cache by match ID, and React re-renders the affected components — but throttled with requestAnimationFrame so 100 messages a second still only paint 60 times a second. Each hop is independently scalable, each has a fallback (Kafka retains, Redis rebuilds, WS clients reconnect with backoff). The frontend sees a clean stream of updates and never knows about the rest.
keyPoints: Game -> Kafka -> processor -> Redis -> WS -> client

scenarios.q: Fernando asks: 'What happens to your design under heavy load — say, 10 million people watching Worlds finals?' Give the senior answer.
scenarios.model: Three things have to be true. First, the static slice is on the CDN. Team logos, player photos, VOD thumbnails, pre-match HTML — none of this changes during the match, all of it goes to edge with long cache TTLs. That's 95% of bytes never touching origin. Second, the read slice is cached aggressively. Persisted GraphQL queries become GET requests cacheable at the edge; Redis sits in front of the database with a few seconds of TTL on live data. Origin databases only see writes and uncacheable personalized reads. Third, the live slice has a real fan-out plan. A single WebSocket gateway can't serve 10M clients; you build a tree where regional gateways subscribe once to upstream and fan out locally to their region's clients. Reconnection uses exponential backoff with jitter so a blip doesn't cause a thundering herd. And the whole thing has graceful degradation: if the WS layer is overloaded, clients fall back to 5-second polling; if even that fails, they show the cached shell with a 'live updates unavailable' banner. The product still works, just with lower fidelity.

scenarios.q: Fernando asks: 'How would you test all this?' Give a concrete plan.
scenarios.model: Four layers. Unit tests with Vitest or Jest for component logic and pure functions — fast, run on every save, cover the branching. Integration tests with React Testing Library for component-plus-hook-plus-cache flows — does clicking Predict optimistically update the right cache key, roll back on error, invalidate the right queries? Mock the network at the fetch layer, not the component. E2E tests with Playwright for the critical user paths — login, view a live match, submit a prediction, see it persist after refresh — running against a real backend in a staging environment. Load tests with k6 hitting the WebSocket gateway and the GraphQL endpoint at projected Worlds scale, looking for the breaking point and the failure mode. I'd also add visual regression tests (Chromatic or Percy) on key screens, and synthetic monitoring in production hitting the same critical paths every minute. Each layer is independent and catches different bugs.
keyPoints: Load: k6 on WS and GraphQL

scenarios.q: Fernando asks: 'What would you change if you needed to support 10x the users?' Walk through your reasoning.
scenarios.model: I'd resist the urge to just 'add servers' and instead ask where the work goes today. Most likely culprits at 10x: database write throughput, WebSocket fan-out, origin egress bandwidth. So: caching first — push everything cacheable to the edge with persisted queries and longer TTLs, add a Redis read-through layer in front of Postgres for hot live data. Edge compute next — move auth verification, A/B bucketing, and personalization to Cloudflare Workers so origin only handles the irreducible work. Real-time fan-out: shard the WebSocket layer regionally with a tree topology, each regional node subscribing once upstream and fanning out locally — that turns 10M direct subs into a few hundred upstream subs. Database sharding by tournament or region so no one node is a hot spot, with read replicas for the read traffic that escapes the cache. Connection pooling everywhere expensive connections live. And critically: observability — distributed tracing, RUM, structured logs, alerting on the right SLOs — because at 10x you can't debug what you can't see, and most production fires only matter if you find them quickly. I'd also do a load test to verify, not assume, that each change holds at the new scale.
keyPoints: Don't just add servers — find the bottleneck

mcq.q: Why does the Riot login design store access and refresh tokens in HttpOnly Secure cookies instead of localStorage?
options: localStorage has a smaller quota than cookies, so tokens may be evicted
HttpOnly cookies cannot be read by JavaScript, so XSS cannot exfiltrate them
