Cookies are automatically encrypted by the browser, localStorage is not
localStorage is not supported in incognito mode on most mobile browsers
mcq.explanation: The whole point of HttpOnly is that JS (including injected XSS payloads) cannot read the cookie. Secure forces HTTPS, SameSite=Lax mitigates CSRF. localStorage is reachable from any script on the page, which makes it a single XSS away from full account takeover.

mcq.q: Why does the design use PKCE for the social OAuth flow (Google/Apple/Discord) rather than the classic authorization code flow with a client secret?
options: PKCE is faster because it skips the token exchange step
PKCE is required by all OAuth providers as of 2024
A SPA is a public client and cannot keep a client secret, so PKCE proves the same client started and finished the flow
PKCE removes the need for HTTPS on the redirect URL
mcq.explanation: A browser SPA cannot store a client_secret safely. PKCE replaces the secret with a per-session code_verifier/code_challenge pair, defeating authorization code interception even on a public client.

mcq.q: The MFA step is rendered at /login/mfa as its own route rather than as a piece of local state inside the LoginPage. Why?
options: URL-based steps survive a page refresh, so the user doesn't lose progress mid-MFA
It is required by WebAuthn spec to use a separate URL
React Router cannot animate transitions between local-state steps
It allows search engines to index the MFA page for SEO
mcq.explanation: Putting the step in the URL means a refresh, accidental back-button, or shared link still lands the user in a coherent place. Local state would be wiped on refresh, dumping the user back to the email form mid-flow.

mcq.q: Why does the design rate-limit by BOTH IP and account on the login endpoint, rather than just one?
options: GDPR requires per-IP and per-account counters separately
IP-only blocks botnets but not stuffing one account; account-only stops stuffing but lets a bot try millions of accounts from one IP
Redis sliding windows only work with composite keys
It is required to receive a CAPTCHA exemption from Cloudflare
mcq.explanation: Account-only protects a single account from password spraying but lets an attacker enumerate countless accounts from one IP. IP-only blocks the noisy attacker but a botnet trivially defeats it. You need both dimensions: per-IP general throttle plus per-account lockout after N failures.

mcq.q: When the AuthInterceptor sees a 401 on a normal API call, the design refreshes the token using a singleton in-flight promise. What problem does the singleton solve?
options: It avoids React strict-mode double effects
If many requests 401 simultaneously they would each call /refresh, racing and possibly invalidating each other's new refresh token
It is needed because fetch cannot be cancelled
It prevents the browser from caching the refresh response
mcq.explanation: Several queries may 401 in the same tick (page load fans out 5 requests). Without a singleton you fire 5 /refresh calls; refresh-token rotation can then invalidate all but one and log the user out. The fix is one shared promise; the rest await it and replay with the new token.

mcq.q: How does 'Remember me' actually translate to backend behavior in this design?
options: The frontend skips the /me check on subsequent visits
The refresh token cookie is issued with a long lifetime (e.g. 30 days) instead of a session-only cookie that dies on browser close
The frontend caches the user's password in IndexedDB
The browser is asked to save credentials via the Credential Management API
mcq.explanation: Remember me is a refresh-token-lifetime decision: long-lived persistent cookie vs a session cookie that disappears when the browser closes. The access token stays short-lived in both cases.

mcq.q: How does this design protect against CSRF given that authentication is cookie-based?
options: By storing the access token in localStorage as a backup
SameSite=Lax cookies + Origin/Referer check on the server, plus a CSRF token for cross-origin non-GET requests
By disabling all POST requests from the frontend
By using HTTPS — CSRF is impossible on HTTPS
mcq.explanation: SameSite=Lax stops the cookie from being attached on most cross-site POSTs. Server-side Origin/Referer enforcement is the second line of defense. For any genuinely cross-origin write you use a CSRF token. HTTPS by itself does nothing for CSRF.

mcq.q: After successful login on lolesports.com, the user visits valorantesports.com and is already logged in. What enables this in the design?
options: The frontend posts the access token via window.postMessage between tabs
Cookies are issued on the parent domain .riotgames.com from a shared auth service (auth.riotgames.com), so any subdomain receives them
Each game property mirrors the user table with eventual consistency
Service Workers share IndexedDB across origins
mcq.explanation: Cookies set on the parent .riotgames.com domain are sent to every subdomain. A shared auth.riotgames.com issues those cookies; each property validates against the same auth service. This is the SSO-across-properties pattern called out for Fernando.

recall.q: Why use HttpOnly cookies over localStorage for tokens, and what is the trade-off the frontend has to accept?
recall.model: HttpOnly means JavaScript can't read the cookie, so an XSS payload cannot exfiltrate the token. Secure forces HTTPS only; SameSite=Lax limits CSRF. The trade-off: the frontend can no longer attach an Authorization header itself, so all auth-touching fetches must use credentials: 'include' and the backend has to set the cookies on every login/refresh.

recall.q: Why is PKCE the right OAuth profile for a Riot SPA, and what attack does it actually prevent?
recall.model: A SPA is a public client — it can't keep a client_secret because everything ships to the browser. PKCE replaces the secret with a one-time code_verifier hashed into a code_challenge. It prevents authorization-code interception: even if an attacker grabs the code from the redirect URL, they can't exchange it without the verifier the original tab generated.

recall.q: Why model the MFA step as its own route (/login/mfa) rather than holding it in component state?
recall.model: URL state is the only state that survives refresh, deep-linking, and browser back/forward. If MFA is in local state, a refresh during the 6-digit-code step throws the user back to the email form, which is both annoying and a support cost. The URL also lets you preserve a ?redirect param across the MFA hop.

recall.q: Why rate limit on both IP and account, not just one?
recall.model: IP-only: a botnet defeats it trivially because every node is a different IP. Account-only: still allows an attacker to walk through millions of usernames from a single host (enumeration / credential stuffing of leaked dumps). You need both axes — a sliding-window per-IP throttle plus a per-account failure counter that escalates to CAPTCHA and then temporary lockout.

recall.q: Walk through the AuthInterceptor flow on a 401 and explain why a singleton refresh promise matters.
recall.model: On 401 the interceptor pauses the failing request, calls POST /api/auth/refresh, and on success replays the original request with the new cookie. A page often fans out many requests at once; without a singleton each 401 triggers its own /refresh, and with refresh-token rotation all but one of those refreshes will be invalidated, kicking the user out. The fix is a module-level Promise: the first 401 starts the refresh, every subsequent 401 awaits the same Promise and then replays.

recall.q: Why is auth status held in both AuthContext AND React Query in this design?
recall.model: React Query owns the network side: it actually fetches /api/auth/me, caches it, refetches on focus, and provides loading/error semantics for free. AuthContext is the convenient consumer surface — components call useAuth() and get a stable shape with login/logout helpers without learning the query key. You get cache + dedupe from RQ and ergonomics from Context.

recall.q: How is 'Remember me' implemented and why is it a backend, not frontend, concern?
recall.model: It is a refresh-token-lifetime flag sent in the login body. With remember me on, the backend issues a persistent refresh-token cookie with a long Max-Age (e.g. 30 days). Without it, the cookie is a session cookie that dies on browser close. The frontend just toggles a checkbox; the security boundary lives on the server.

recall.q: Why does the post-login redirect target live in a URL query param (?redirect=/matches) instead of context or sessionStorage?
recall.model: The redirect has to survive an OAuth round trip — the browser leaves Riot, hits Google, and comes back. Anything in JS memory or sessionStorage on the original tab may be lost (popup vs redirect, new tab, etc). A query param rides on the OAuth state and is restored on the callback, which is the only reliable way to remember 'where were you trying to go.'
keyPoints: Works for popup and full redirect

recall.q: Why does logout require server-side invalidation, not just clearing cookies?
recall.model: Clearing the cookie only logs out the current browser. The refresh token is still valid on the server and could be replayed by anyone who already stole it (e.g. via a malicious extension or session hijack). Logout has to delete the refresh-token row in Redis/Postgres so any future /refresh with that token fails. Same reason DELETE /sessions/:id exists — you need server-side revocation as the source of truth.

recall.q: How should the multi-tab logout problem be solved and why is it more than a cosmetic fix?
recall.model: Listen for storage events on a shared key, or use BroadcastChannel('auth'), so when one tab logs out the others clear their AuthContext and redirect. Beyond UX, it is a security issue: a tab still showing 'logged in' may keep firing background queries with stale assumptions, and on shared computers a user thinks they logged out everywhere when they didn't.

scenarios.q: Fernando: 'Suppose a user's refresh token is stolen. How does your design limit the damage?'
scenarios.model: Several layers. (1) Refresh tokens live in HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies, so XSS can't read them and they don't ride cross-site requests. (2) Refresh tokens are stored server-side in the sessions table with device, IP, and last-active metadata, and rotated on every use — so if both the attacker and the legitimate user use the token, rotation detects the reuse and we can revoke the entire family. (3) Access tokens are short-lived (minutes), so even with a stolen refresh token the attack window per use is bounded. (4) The user has /settings/sessions to see all active devices and 'sign out everywhere,' which deletes every refresh token for that user and forces re-login. (5) Risky operations (password change, payment) require step-up MFA so an attacker holding only a refresh token still can't do the worst things. I'd also alert the user by email on logins from new devices.

scenarios.q: PM says: 'For the password reset emails, just put the new password in the reset URL as a query param so users can click and be done.' Push back.
scenarios.model: Hard no, and here's why. (1) URLs are logged everywhere — browser history, ISP logs, CDN logs, referrer headers when the page loads any third-party asset, analytics. The password would leak in five places. (2) URLs end up in shoulder-surfing range and in screenshots people share with support. (3) It violates the principle that we never know the user's plaintext password — we only ever store a hash. The right design is what we already have: emailed link contains a single-use, short-TTL token bound to the user; clicking it lands on /reset-password/:token where the user types a new password into a POST body over HTTPS, the token is consumed server-side, and the new hash is stored. I'd offer the PM the actual goal — 'one-click recovery' — by making the token long-lived enough for a confused user, plus a clear UX.

scenarios.q: Riot is integrating a corporate SSO (e.g. Okta) for esports staff. What questions do you ask before designing the integration?
scenarios.model: Protocol: SAML or OIDC? OIDC plays nicer with the existing OAuth/PKCE plumbing; SAML needs an XML pipeline. IdP-initiated or SP-initiated, or both? Account model: do staff get separate corporate accounts, or are existing Riot accounts linked by email — and what happens on email collision with a player account? JIT provisioning or pre-provisioning via SCIM? Group/role mapping: how do Okta groups map to internal authz scopes? Session policy: does corporate SSO override our refresh-token lifetime, and does logout from Okta force logout from Riot (Single Logout)? MFA: do we trust the IdP's MFA and skip ours, or layer? Domain restriction: lock the SSO entry point to specific corporate email domains. Off-boarding: how fast must a deactivated Okta user lose Riot session — minutes via SCIM webhook, or wait for token expiry? Audit logging requirements. And finally environments: do we get sandbox + prod IdPs, and how do we test without real employee credentials?
keyPoints: OIDC vs SAML, IdP- vs SP-initiated

scenarios.q: During a Worlds-finals login spike, the auth service starts returning 429 to roughly 5% of legitimate users. What does the frontend do, and what should you NOT do?
scenarios.model: Frontend behavior: (1) Surface a calm, specific message — 'Riot is busy right now, retrying in Xs' — using the Retry-After header from the 429, not a generic error. (2) Implement client-side exponential backoff with jitter on the retry, capped, so the spike doesn't get worse. (3) Disable the submit button during the cooldown to stop users from hammering. (4) Don't drop the form state; preserve email so the user doesn't retype. (5) Log the 429 to telemetry so on-call sees the rate climbing. What NOT to do: don't auto-retry instantly in a tight loop (that's the thundering herd that caused this); don't fall back to a less-secure auth path; don't 'helpfully' silently retry across navigations because you'll create duplicate sessions; don't show 'wrong password' because that confuses users into changing their password during an outage.

requirements.preview: The functional and non-functional lines you'd draw before designing auth for a platform that handles Worlds-scale traffic.
requirements.priorQuestion: Before reading: you're scoping login for a gaming platform. Write two non-functional requirements you'd push for that a PM might not think to ask for.

recite.q: In your own words, explain why you'd clarify 'is SSO across LoL, Valorant, and TFT in scope' before drawing a single box on the whiteboard.
recite.model: SSO changes the whole architecture — it forces a dedicated auth service on a shared cookie domain instead of per-app auth. Discovering that requirement after designing a per-app system means restarting. Clarifying upfront shows I understand that scope drives structure and protects the interview's time budget.
keyPoints: shared cookie domain implies dedicated auth service

recite.q: If a teammate said 'let's skip MFA for v1, we can add it later,' how would you push back?
recite.model: MFA isn't a bolt-on — it changes the auth state machine (login has a mid-step), the session model (pre-MFA vs post-MFA tokens), and the UI (a /login/mfa route). Retrofitting means rewriting all three. Shipping MFA in v1 costs far less than retrofitting.

architecture.preview: How stateless auth servers, Redis, Postgres, and OAuth providers fit together to let one login unlock every Riot game.
architecture.priorQuestion: Before reading: why would you make an auth service stateless and put sessions in Redis instead of just keeping them in memory on the server that handled the login?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why sessions live in Redis while users live in Postgres — why not put both in the same place?
recite.model: Postgres gives me strong consistency and relational integrity for user identity, which I can't compromise on. Redis gives me sub-millisecond session lookups with automatic TTL expiry, which is what every request needs. Using both means each datastore does the job it's best at instead of forcing one to cover both.
keyPoints: Postgres for strong consistency on identity
Redis for fast session validation and TTL

recite.q: If a junior engineer asked 'why not just put the rate limiter inside the auth service code?', how would you answer?
recite.model: You can, but a shared Redis-backed rate limiter in front of the service lets every stateless auth pod see the same counters. If I kept counters in local memory, an attacker could hit different pods and dodge the limit. Centralizing counters in Redis is what makes horizontal scaling safe.
keyPoints: stateless pods share counters via Redis
local counters defeat rate limiting under load balancing

components.preview: The React tree that turns auth states — login, MFA, reset, session management — into routes and shared interceptors.
components.priorQuestion: Before reading: where in a React app would you put the piece that automatically retries a request after refreshing an expired access token?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why AuthProvider and AuthInterceptor are separate concerns instead of one big 'auth' component.
recite.model: AuthProvider owns application-level state — who is logged in, the user object, login/logout actions — and exposes it via context. AuthInterceptor is a network-layer concern — it catches 401s and triggers refresh. Mixing them means UI code knows about fetch retry logic. Splitting them keeps each piece focused on one layer of the stack.

recite.q: Why would you put MFA on its own route (/login/mfa) instead of conditionally rendering it inside the login page?
recite.model: A dedicated route means the MFA step survives page refreshes — if someone refreshes mid-flow, they stay on MFA instead of bouncing back to password entry. It also makes the auth state machine legible in the URL, which helps analytics, deep-linking, and debugging. Conditional rendering would lose state on reload.

state.preview: The state-placement decisions that make auth secure against XSS and CSRF without painful token plumbing in JS.
state.priorQuestion: Before reading: if a frontend engineer told you they store the JWT in localStorage 'for convenience,' what's the concrete attack scenario you'd walk them through?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why HttpOnly cookies beat localStorage for auth tokens — not just 'it's more secure.'
recite.model: HttpOnly means JavaScript physically can't read the cookie, so any XSS vulnerability still can't exfiltrate the token. With localStorage, a single XSS on any page that runs your JS hands the attacker long-lived credentials. The trade-off is that the backend sets cookies and the frontend must send credentials: 'include', but that's cheap compared to token theft.

recite.q: Why put the post-login redirect target in a URL query param instead of in memory or sessionStorage?
recite.model: The OAuth flow bounces the user off-site to Google/Apple/Discord and back. Anything in memory disappears across that round-trip, and sessionStorage is scoped per tab. A query param rides along in the redirect URL, so the state survives the provider bounce and the callback can read it when landing.

api.preview: The endpoint shapes and PKCE dance that let a pure SPA do OAuth safely without holding client secrets.
api.priorQuestion: Before reading: why can't a single-page app safely use the classic OAuth 'confidential client' flow with a client secret?

recite.q: Explain in your own words what PKCE protects against that plain authorization code flow doesn't.
recite.model: PKCE defends against authorization code interception. Without it, a malicious app on the same device could intercept the redirect and exchange the code for tokens. PKCE adds a verifier that only the app that started the flow knows, so an intercepted code is useless without the matching verifier. That's why it's the standard for public clients like SPAs and mobile apps.

recite.q: If a teammate asked 'why do we need a /refresh endpoint at all — why not just make access tokens live for 30 days?', how would you respond?
recite.model: Long-lived access tokens are dangerous because they can't be revoked — once leaked, an attacker has 30 days of access. Short access tokens plus a refresh endpoint gives us a revocation point: we invalidate the refresh token server-side and the access token dies within minutes. It's the trade-off of session longevity vs blast radius.
keyPoints: long access tokens can't be revoked quickly

data-flow.preview: The step-by-step choreography from 'click Log In' through MFA, token refresh, and the race conditions that bite in production.
data-flow.priorQuestion: Before reading: imagine five tabs open when the access token expires — they all fire API calls at once. What bad thing happens by default, and how would you stop it?

recite.q: In your own words, explain the singleton refresh promise pattern and why it matters.
recite.model: When multiple requests all hit 401 at the same time, naive code fires multiple refresh calls — which can invalidate each other or race. The singleton pattern means the first 401 starts one refresh promise; every later 401 awaits that same promise. Only one refresh hits the server, and all queued requests replay with the new token after it resolves.
keyPoints: one refresh promise shared across callers

recite.q: Why do the 'optimistic redirect' after login instead of waiting for /me to confirm?
recite.model: The login response itself proves auth succeeded — the server set cookies. Waiting for /me adds a round-trip of perceived latency for no new information. Redirecting immediately and letting /me hydrate user details in the background feels instant. If /me ever fails, we can handle that as an exception, but it almost never does.

performance.preview: The small wins — code splitting, prefetch, graceful 429s — that make a high-traffic login feel instant even at launch spikes.
performance.priorQuestion: Before reading: which parts of a login page are worth code-splitting, and which definitely aren't?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why you'd code-split the login pages from the main app bundle.
recite.model: Logged-in users — the vast majority of sessions on a mature product — never need login code. Bundling password reset, MFA, and OAuth SDKs with the main app makes them pay the download cost for something they'll never use. Splitting means the main app is leaner and login pages load on demand only for the people who actually need them.
keyPoints: login code is cold for most sessions

recite.q: If a junior asked 'why prefetch /home while the login request is still in-flight — isn't that wasted work if login fails?', how would you answer?
recite.model: Prefetch cost is tiny — a few JS chunks over an idle network — and it's only wasted if login fails, which is the uncommon case. On success (the common case), the post-login navigation is instant because chunks are already warm. Optimizing for the happy path is worth the small penalty in the error case.

edge-cases.preview: The subtle failure modes — multi-tab logout, blocked popups, SSO cookie domains — that separate a demo login from a production one.
edge-cases.priorQuestion: Before reading: a user logs out in Tab A. Tab B still shows them logged in and lets them take actions. How would you detect and fix this without polling?

recite.q: Explain in your own words how you'd make SSO work across lolesports.com and valorantesports.com using cookies.
recite.model: I'd centralize auth on auth.riotgames.com and set the session cookie with Domain=.riotgames.com so every subdomain receives it. Each game property validates the cookie against the shared auth service. The browser does the hard work — any request to any Riot subdomain automatically carries the session, so there's no second login.

recite.q: Why does SameSite=Lax on auth cookies give CSRF protection, and what's its limitation?
recite.model: SameSite=Lax means the browser won't send the cookie on cross-site subrequests — a form post from evil.com won't include the session. That kills classic CSRF. The limitation is that Lax still sends cookies on top-level GET navigations, so you can't rely on it alone for state-changing GETs, and for extra safety on mutations I'd add an Origin check or a CSRF token.
keyPoints: pair with Origin check or CSRF token

mcq.q: Why does live video go through the CDN as HLS/DASH segments while stats updates flow through the WebSocket gateway and never touch the CDN?
options: CDNs cannot serve binary data, only video
Video segments are large, immutable, and identical for every viewer (CDN-friendly); stats are small, per-event, and time-sensitive (push-friendly)
WebSockets are blocked by most CDNs
HLS and WebSocket cannot coexist in the same browser tab
mcq.explanation: HLS chunks are 2-6 second files that every viewer wants identically — perfect cache fan-out at the edge. Stats are small per-event deltas that need to push instantly to subscribed clients; sticking them behind a CDN would add latency and break the realtime feel. Two different transports for two different shapes of data.

mcq.q: The design fetches initial match state via GraphQL query and then subscribes via WebSocket for deltas. Why both, instead of WebSocket-only?
options: GraphQL is faster than WebSocket for the first message
WebSockets cannot send initial state — only deltas
It gives a fast cache-friendly initial render plus a clean polling-fallback path if the WebSocket drops
GraphQL is required for SEO indexing of match pages
mcq.explanation: REST/GraphQL gives you HTTP caching, React Query dedupe, and a sensible fallback when sockets are down. WebSocket-only means a cold tab has nothing to render until the next event arrives, which on a quiet game could be seconds. Hybrid: query for the snapshot, subscribe for the diff.

mcq.q: During a teamfight, the WebSocket can fire ~10 events per second. Why does the design batch updates with requestAnimationFrame rather than calling dispatch() per message?
options: requestAnimationFrame guarantees messages are delivered in order
Per-message dispatch causes React to re-render up to 10 times per second, thrashing reconciliation; rAF coalesces them into one frame
WebSocket onmessage is blocked outside rAF callbacks
It enables the React DevTools profiler
mcq.explanation: The user can only see ~60fps anyway. Coalescing N events into one BATCH_UPDATE per frame collapses 10 reconciliations into 1, while still feeling perfectly smooth. This is the canonical pattern Fernando will want to hear.

mcq.q: Why is the chat message list virtualized with a 500-message ring buffer instead of just rendering everything?
options: react-window is required by accessibility guidelines
DOM nodes for thousands of chat messages cause scroll jank and unbounded memory growth
WebSocket messages cannot be diffed efficiently outside virtualization
It is required to enable picture-in-picture
mcq.explanation: A high-traffic match might push thousands of messages over an hour. Mounting them all is both a memory leak and a scroll/layout disaster on mobile. Virtualization renders only the visible window; the ring buffer caps memory. Old messages scroll into nothing — and that's fine for chat semantics.

mcq.q: Spoiler-free mode hides scores AND pads VOD durations to a uniform length. Why pad the duration?
options: It reduces CDN bandwidth
A 25-minute VOD obviously means a stomp — the duration itself is a spoiler
Browsers cap video player duration display at 60 minutes
It is required by GDPR
mcq.explanation: A great example of 'thinking like a player.' If you only blur the score but show '23:14' on the scrubber, the user already knows what happened. True spoiler-free has to hide every signal of game length, including the scrub bar's total.

mcq.q: The selected POV (e.g., ?pov=faker) lives in a URL search param rather than React state. Why?
options: URL params are faster to read than useState
It makes the chosen POV shareable and survives refresh — 'watch Faker's POV' as a link
WebSockets cannot subscribe to POV channels otherwise
It is required to enable HLS
mcq.explanation: Anything a fan would screenshot and DM to a friend should live in the URL. POV selection is exactly that — a shareable handle to a specific viewing experience.

recall.q: Walk through the live stats data flow from game server to the user's GoldDiffGraph and explain why each hop exists.
recall.model: Game server emits raw events to Kafka (durable buffer, decouples producers from consumers). An event processor reads Kafka, enriches each event with player/team/champion metadata (so the WebSocket layer doesn't need DB lookups). The processor publishes to a Redis Pub/Sub channel like match:123:updates. The WebSocket gateway is subscribed to that channel and fans out to every connected client viewing match 123. The client's useMatchSubscription hook collects messages, the rAF batcher dispatches a BATCH_UPDATE to the reducer, and only the affected components (GoldDiffGraph, scoreboard) re-render thanks to React.memo.
keyPoints: WS gateway scales on connections, not CPU

recall.q: Why use React Query for the initial match snapshot AND a WebSocket for live deltas, instead of one or the other?
recall.model: React Query gets you a fast first paint via HTTP caching, retry, and dedupe; it also gives you a built-in polling fallback if the WebSocket drops. The WebSocket is the only sensible path for high-frequency low-latency deltas. Used together, the user always has data: cold load is snapshot-fast, live experience is push-fast, and a dropped socket gracefully degrades to background refetch instead of going blank.
keyPoints: Snapshot via RQ, deltas via WS
Reconciliation = replace local state on reconnect

recall.q: Why batch WebSocket events with requestAnimationFrame instead of dispatching per message?
recall.model: During a teamfight you can get 10+ events/second. Calling dispatch per message causes 10 re-renders per second of the reducer subtree, which thrashes React reconciliation and can drop frames. The user can only perceive ~60fps anyway, so coalescing N messages into one BATCH_UPDATE per frame collapses N reconciliations into 1 with zero perceived loss. It also lets you sort/dedupe events within the frame before applying them.
keyPoints: Smooth at the cost of 16ms latency

recall.q: Why is the chat list virtualized and capped at a 500-message ring buffer?
recall.model: Two failure modes you avoid. (1) Memory: a busy match generates thousands of messages per hour; keeping them all in the React tree leaks DOM nodes and JS objects. (2) Layout: react-window only mounts the visible rows, so scrolling stays at 60fps even on mobile. The ring buffer caps state size — old messages drop off because chat semantically doesn't need history. If a user wants to scroll back further, that's a different feature (chat replay) backed by the API.

recall.q: Why is the spoiler-free mode preference stored in localStorage + Context rather than just Context?
recall.model: Context alone gives you app-wide read/write but resets on every page load. Spoiler-free is a personal preference that should follow the user across visits — they shouldn't have to re-enable it every time they open the VOD page. localStorage persists the bit, Context broadcasts it to consumers without prop drilling. You hydrate Context from localStorage on mount and write through on toggles.

recall.q: Why does each WebSocket subscription unsubscribe on unmount, and what goes wrong if you forget?
recall.model: Subscriptions hold socket-side state per match, plus client-side reducers and timers. Forgetting to unsubscribe leaks memory, leaks bandwidth (you keep receiving updates for matches you closed), and worst, can fire setState on unmounted components (warnings + bugs). On the server it bloats Redis Pub/Sub fan-out lists. The cleanup function in the useEffect that opened the subscription must close it.

recall.q: Why do live HLS segments use a short TTL (2-6s) at the CDN while VODs are immutable and long-cached?
recall.model: Live segments are 2-6 seconds of footage and there's a new one every few seconds; the URL is reused as the playlist rolls. They need to expire quickly so stale ones don't get re-served. VODs are post-game artifacts with hashed/immutable URLs — once produced they never change, so cache them effectively forever. Same CDN, two cache policies because the data has two different shapes of mutability.

recall.q: Why is the WebSocket gateway a separate service from the API servers?
recall.model: Different scaling axis. API servers scale on request rate and CPU per request; the WebSocket gateway scales on number of open connections (memory + file descriptors) and is largely idle CPU-wise. Mixing them means you over-provision API capacity to host idle sockets, or vice versa. Splitting lets you autoscale each on its own metric, deploy them independently, and isolate failures: an API outage doesn't kill live updates, and vice versa.

scenarios.q: A user reports: 'During the game, my video froze for 20 seconds but the gold-diff graph kept updating.' What does this signal, and what should the UI do about it?
scenarios.model: The signal is that two transports are now out of sync: HLS playback is stalled (buffering, dropped CDN edge, ABR going to a quality the player can't fetch), but the WebSocket pipeline is still healthy because it's a totally separate path. From the user's perspective the stats are spoiling the game they haven't seen yet — every kill on the graph is a spoiler for video that hasn't played. UI fixes: (1) Detect HLS stall via the player's stalled/waiting events. (2) When stalled for >Ns, dim or 'fog' the stats panel with a 'video catching up...' label so live data doesn't spoil the broadcast. (3) Optionally buffer incoming WebSocket events into a queue and release them on a delay matching the measured video lag (the source already mentions this — buffer stats and release on a delay matching stream latency). (4) Give the user a 'sync to video' toggle. (5) Telemetry: report the divergence so we can see whether stalls are CDN or client side.

scenarios.q: Worlds finals: 10,000 chat users in a single match channel. Sketch the chat architecture and what the frontend has to do.
scenarios.model: Backend: chat is its own service, separate from match-stats WebSockets. Inbound messages hit a moderation pipeline (rate limit per user, profanity, sentiment, escalation) before being broadcast. Fan-out is via Redis Pub/Sub or a dedicated tier (NATS/Kafka) sharded by match. The chat WebSocket gateway shards connections by match-id and only fans messages out to subscribers of that shard, so no node holds the entire 10k. Probably you also enforce slow mode automatically when message rate per second exceeds a threshold. Frontend: separate WebSocket from the stats socket so chat backpressure doesn't slow stats. Virtualized list with a 500-msg ring buffer (already in the design). Optimistic render of the user's own message, but other users' messages only render after moderation clears them — never trust the local echo. Client-side rate limit (1 msg / 2s) plus a slow-mode indicator pushed by the server. Coalesce incoming messages with the same rAF batching trick used for stats so 100 msgs/sec doesn't drop frames. On reconnect, fetch the last N messages via REST so the user isn't dropped into nothing.

scenarios.q: Sketch how you would build picture-in-picture so a user scrolling away from the match can keep watching.
scenarios.model: Use the native Picture-in-Picture API where supported: video.requestPictureInPicture() on a button or on an IntersectionObserver that fires when the player scrolls out of view. Listen for enterpictureinpicture / leavepictureinpicture to keep React state in sync (a pipActive flag in the player ref). When PiP is active, the main page should NOT keep an oversized empty player slot — collapse it gracefully and keep the StatsPanel visible (the source explicitly says 'keep the stats panel visible in the main page'). Re-entering PiP on tab switch should be opt-in to avoid surprising users. Fallback for browsers without the API: a sticky mini-player component pinned bottom-right, using the same VideoPlayer instance moved into a portal so the underlying <video> element is not unmounted (unmounting kills HLS state and forces a rebuffer). Edge cases: PiP doesn't survive a navigation, so warn before route change; chat input still works because PiP doesn't capture keyboard; on mobile, prefer the native mobile PiP from the OS rather than DOM PiP. Telemetry on PiP enter/exit so we can size the feature.

requirements.preview: The functional and scale constraints that turn 'let users watch esports' into a system that survives Worlds finals.
requirements.priorQuestion: Before reading: what's one non-functional requirement for a live esports viewer that would fundamentally change your architecture if it were added late?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why 'spoiler-free mode' is a design constraint, not a feature flag.
recite.model: Spoiler-free has to be threaded through every surface that could reveal a result — VOD thumbnails, durations, scrub bars, even URL hover previews. If you treat it as a late feature, you'll hit it everywhere at once and miss spots. Designing it in from the start means the whole UI layer respects a single context that hides or masks result-bearing data.

recite.q: Why push for 'stats within 2-3 seconds of broadcast' as a hard non-functional requirement instead of 'eventually consistent'?
recite.model: Viewers experience the stream and the stats side by side. If stats lag the video by 10 seconds, every kill spoils itself before the viewer sees it — or worse, arrives after. Tight latency isn't a nice-to-have, it's what makes the overlay feel connected to the game. It also disciplines my pipeline choice toward WebSockets over polling.

architecture.preview: How Kafka, Redis Pub/Sub, a WebSocket gateway, and a CDN split the work between live data and VOD delivery.
architecture.priorQuestion: Before reading: why would you separate the servers handling WebSocket connections from the servers handling REST API calls, even if both could live in one service?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why live data flows through Kafka and Redis Pub/Sub before reaching the WebSocket gateway, instead of the game server pushing directly to clients.
recite.model: Game servers shouldn't know or care about client-facing infrastructure — coupling them means every client-side change reaches back into game systems. Kafka absorbs the producer side, lets us replay and transform events, and Redis Pub/Sub fans out to the WebSocket layer. This decouples lifecycles: the game server can evolve independently from the viewer frontend.

recite.q: Why does HLS's short segment TTL at the CDN edge actually scale to millions of viewers, instead of killing the origin?
recite.model: Even with a 2-6 second TTL, the CDN edge serves the same segment to every viewer in that window. One origin fetch per segment per edge location fans out to millions of clients at the edge. The short TTL just means fresh segments flow through quickly — it's still hugely cache-efficient because every viewer is watching approximately the same bytes at the same time.
keyPoints: one origin fetch per edge per segment

components.preview: The component tree that lets a match page juggle video, stats, chat, and spoiler guards without turning into a monolith.
components.priorQuestion: Before reading: on a live match page with video + stats + chat, which piece is most likely to cause re-render thrashing, and what's your first move to prevent it?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why ChatPanel is lazy-loaded and StatsPanel is not.
recite.model: Chat brings in heavy dependencies — emoji pickers, virtualization libraries, moderation UI — that many viewers never open, especially on mobile where chat is collapsed by default. Lazy-loading keeps the critical path lean. Stats are core to the viewing experience and need to render immediately, so they belong in the main bundle.
keyPoints: stats are on the critical path

recite.q: Why is SpoilerGuard a context wrapping the whole app rather than a prop drilled into each VOD card?
recite.model: Spoiler state affects many unrelated components — VOD grid, match cards, scrub bar, duration labels. Prop-drilling through every layer is noise and easy to miss. A context lets any component that renders result-sensitive data subscribe directly to the spoiler preference, which is exactly the sort of cross-cutting concern React context was built for.

state.preview: The hybrid React Query + WebSocket pattern that gives viewers live updates while still handling cold loads and reconnections.
state.priorQuestion: Before reading: if you have a WebSocket pushing live match updates, why would you still use something like React Query for the initial match data?

recite.q: In your own words, justify the 'React Query for initial load, WebSocket for deltas' pattern to a skeptical teammate.
recite.model: React Query handles the cold path — first paint, cache, refetching on focus, automatic polling if the socket dies. WebSocket handles the hot path — the low-latency stream of updates. Trying to use one for both means either re-implementing caching on top of a socket or paying polling latency for what should be instant. Letting each do what it's best at makes the reconnection story easier.
keyPoints: RQ for cache and cold load

recite.q: Why keep only the last 500 chat messages in a ring buffer instead of all of them?
recite.model: Chat during Worlds can produce thousands of messages per minute. Keeping everything balloons memory and slows virtualized list scrolling with offsets that grow forever. A ring buffer caps memory at a predictable size and matches how users actually read chat — only the recent stuff matters. Scrollback isn't a feature viewers expect in live chat.

api.preview: The GraphQL shapes — query for initial load, subscription for deltas, cursor pagination for VODs — that keep live and archive paths clean.
api.priorQuestion: Before reading: why might GraphQL subscriptions be a better fit than REST SSE for real-time match updates, or vice versa?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why initial match load uses a query but live updates use a subscription, instead of one unified channel.
recite.model: The initial load is a one-shot fetch that plays well with HTTP caching, CDN hints, and React Query's cache. A subscription is an ongoing stream of small deltas. Using separate channels lets the initial query be cacheable and retryable, while the subscription focuses on low-latency push. Unifying them would either force the query to be non-cacheable or force the subscription to re-ship the whole state on every tick.

recite.q: Why use cursor-based pagination for VODs instead of offset + limit?
recite.model: New VODs keep arriving, so offset pages shift under the user — page 2 today might overlap page 1 from yesterday. Cursors are opaque pointers into a stable ordering, so 'give me the next 20 after this cursor' always returns the right slice regardless of what arrived since. It also scales better for large result sets because the database doesn't count past all earlier rows.

data-flow.preview: The full path of a single kill event — from game server through Kafka, Redis, and requestAnimationFrame batching — into a re-rendered stat card.
data-flow.priorQuestion: Before reading: if a WebSocket is pushing 10 events per second during a teamfight, what goes wrong if each event triggers a React re-render, and how would you batch them?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why requestAnimationFrame batching is the right tool for WebSocket floods, not debouncing or throttling.
recite.model: Debouncing drops events until the stream pauses — bad, because during a teamfight it never pauses. Throttling caps frequency but decouples from frame timing. requestAnimationFrame batches all events that arrived since the last paint and flushes once per frame. The user perceives smooth 60fps updates and React reconciles once per frame instead of ten times, regardless of event rate.

recite.q: Why dispatch a single BATCH_UPDATE action with an array of events instead of looping dispatch calls?
recite.model: Each dispatch triggers a reducer run and potential re-render. Looping N dispatches means N reducer passes and N reconciliations, even inside a batch. One BATCH_UPDATE action processes all events in a single reducer pass and yields one re-render. It also gives the reducer a chance to collapse redundant updates — like two gold ticks into one.

performance.preview: The GPU-compositing, virtualization, and CDN tricks that let a mobile browser sustain a smooth live match view over spotty wifi.
performance.priorQuestion: Before reading: why would you animate a live gold-diff chart with CSS transforms instead of updating SVG path d attributes every frame?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why the gold-diff graph uses Canvas or SVG with CSS transforms rather than a heavy charting library.
recite.model: Libraries like Recharts re-render the whole chart component tree every tick, which is wasteful for a single appended data point. Canvas draws to a bitmap in one cheap call, and SVG with CSS transforms runs on the GPU compositor without layout or paint. Either approach skips React reconciliation for the chart interior and keeps the update cost independent of data size.

recite.q: Why would you serve completed match data via CDN but keep live data off of it entirely?
recite.model: Completed data is immutable — perfect for long-TTL edge caching with hashed URLs. Live data changes by the second; anything the CDN holds becomes wrong almost immediately and invalidation round-trips erase any benefit. Live data wants a persistent push channel like WebSocket, not a pull-through cache. Matching transport to data freshness is the whole game.

edge-cases.preview: The weird-but-unavoidable problems — stream delay, spoilered durations, PiP, multi-match split screen — that each earn player trust.
edge-cases.priorQuestion: Before reading: a user is watching a VOD in spoiler-free mode. What's one way the UI could still accidentally spoil the outcome, and how would you prevent it?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why even VOD duration is a spoiler, and how you'd hide it.
recite.model: A 22-minute LoL VOD is obviously a stomp — nobody plays a balanced game that short. The duration itself leaks the result before anyone hits play. I'd pad every VOD's displayed length to a uniform maximum and hide the scrub bar's total time in spoiler-free mode, exposing only elapsed time. The video plays out naturally without broadcasting how much is left.
keyPoints: hide total time on scrub bar

recite.q: Why coordinate WebSocket connections centrally when a user is watching two matches in split-screen, instead of letting each match page open its own?
recite.model: Each new socket adds handshake cost, server connection slots, and heartbeat traffic. Two matches probably share the same gateway anyway. A central connection manager can multiplex subscriptions over one socket — subscribe to match:1 and match:2 on the same pipe — which halves server-side fan-out state and keeps the client under browser connection limits.

mcq.q: Why does the dashboard split WebSocket subscriptions into 'all-matches-summary' and 'match:{id}:detail' instead of one fat 'everything' channel?
options: Redis Pub/Sub does not support large channels
Subscribing to full detail for every live match would flood the client with bandwidth and re-renders for data the user can't see
WebSockets can only handle two channels at a time per connection
It is required for permessage-deflate to work
mcq.explanation: The summary channel sends tiny score/status updates for every match (cheap, always-on). The detail channel sends rich per-tick data but only for the match the user is actually viewing. Subscribe-on-select / unsubscribe-on-deselect keeps bandwidth and CPU bounded regardless of how many matches are live.

mcq.q: During heavy events, the WebSocket can deliver gold-tick updates many times per second. Why does the design route them through requestAnimationFrame batching?
options: rAF guarantees ordering across multiple sockets
It coalesces N messages into one render per frame, capping work at the display refresh rate
It is required by React 18's concurrent renderer
rAF callbacks have higher network priority
mcq.explanation: Without batching, React reconciles per message — 100 messages/sec means 100 reconciliations and dropped frames. rAF coalesces them to one per frame, which is the most the user can perceive anyway.

mcq.q: Each StatsCard is wrapped in React.memo. What problem does that actually solve in this dashboard?
options: Memo makes WebSocket reads faster
When one card receives a new prop, the parent StatsGrid would normally re-render every sibling — memo lets unaffected siblings skip reconciliation entirely
Memo is required to use useReducer
It enables Suspense fallbacks
mcq.explanation: With a reducer holding match state, dispatching for a gold tick changes the state object reference. Any consuming component re-renders. React.memo on the leaves bails out cards whose specific props (e.g., kill score) didn't change, so only GoldDiffCard reconciles.

mcq.q: Why does the dashboard use a Canvas-based gold-diff chart instead of an SVG/D3/Recharts approach?
options: Canvas is required for permessage-deflate
Canvas avoids per-point DOM nodes and scales to thousands of data points without reconciliation cost
SVG cannot render lines, only shapes
Canvas is the only option that supports CSS animations
mcq.explanation: SVG/Recharts create one DOM node per point, which React must reconcile and the browser must layout. Canvas is one node, redrawn imperatively on rAF — orders of magnitude cheaper at high tick counts and the right call for a dashboard ticking many times per second.

mcq.q: On reconnect after a WebSocket disconnect, the design fetches GET /api/matches/123/state via React Query before resuming the subscription. Why?
options: WebSockets cannot reattach to the same channel after a disconnect
Events that arrived during the gap are lost from the local state — you have to replace local state with a fresh server snapshot to avoid divergence
It is required for permessage-deflate to renegotiate
React Query refuses to use stale data otherwise
mcq.explanation: Live state is delta-driven. Miss a delta and your local copy silently drifts from reality. The only safe recovery is: pull a full snapshot from the source of truth, replace local state, then resume the subscription. Trying to 'replay missed events' is a distributed-systems trap.

mcq.q: Why is the leaderboard fetched with React Query polling at 60s instead of via the WebSocket subscription?
options: WebSockets do not work for tabular data
Standings only change when a match ends — not in real time — so polling at 60s is sufficient and saves the realtime budget for stats that actually tick
React Query is required for GraphQL queries
Redis sorted sets cannot push updates
mcq.explanation: Match the transport to the data's actual update cadence. Standings move in coarse, infrequent steps. Polling at 60s with stale-while-revalidate is dramatically cheaper than holding a subscription, and the user never notices because the data doesn't actually change faster than that.

mcq.q: When the WebSocket dies and the dashboard falls back to polling every 10 seconds, what is the most important thing the UI must do?
options: Show a full-screen modal blocking interaction
Show a 'Live updates paused' indicator and keep showing the last known data, never a loading spinner over existing values
Force a full page reload
Switch the language to English
mcq.explanation: The degradation philosophy in the source is explicit: stale data is better than no data. You inform the user honestly with a status indicator, you keep rendering what you have, and you never blank-screen them mid-event. Spinners over existing data are the wrong move.

mcq.q: Why does the design enable permessage-deflate on the WebSocket connection?
options: It is required for HTTPS
JSON match deltas compress 50-80%, dramatically reducing bandwidth at 2M concurrent users
It allows binary frames in addition to text
It improves WebSocket security
mcq.explanation: Highly redundant JSON (repeated keys, similar structure) compresses extremely well. At Worlds scale (millions of subscribers, many ticks per second), payload reduction is the cheapest scale lever you have. Just be aware of the per-connection memory cost on the server.

recall.q: Why subscribe to a summary channel for all matches but a detail channel only for the selected match?
recall.model: Detail-for-everything would scale linearly with the number of live matches and obliterate bandwidth on a phone during Worlds. Summary is tiny (score, status) per match — cheap to keep open globally. Detail is rich (gold, KDA, items, events) and only matters for the match the user is actually staring at. Subscribe-on-select / unsubscribe-on-deselect enforces the cost: 1 summary + 1 detail at any time, regardless of how many matches are live.

recall.q: Walk through what happens from the moment a GOLD_TICK message arrives to the user seeing the line move, and explain why each step is needed.
recall.model: (1) WebSocket onmessage parses the JSON. (2) The message goes into a pending-updates ref, not directly into state, so we don't dispatch per message. (3) A requestAnimationFrame loop drains the buffer once per frame and calls dispatch({ type: 'BATCH_UPDATE', events }). (4) The reducer applies all events and produces a new state object. (5) GoldDiffCard re-renders because its slice changed; siblings bail out via React.memo. (6) Inside the card, the canvas chart redraws on its own rAF, appending the new point. (7) If the new value crosses a threshold, NotificationToast fires. The point of the architecture is: many messages, one render per frame, one card re-rendered, one canvas redraw.

recall.q: Why use a useReducer (event-sourcing style) for live match state instead of a bunch of useStates or React Query mutations?
recall.model: Live data is an event stream — each message is a typed action (KILL, OBJECTIVE, GOLD_TICK). A reducer is the natural shape: one switch handles every event type, state transitions are pure and testable, and BATCH_UPDATE collapses many events into a single transition. useState would balloon into a dozen setters with no atomicity (a kill update touches scoreboard + KDA + kill feed at once). React Query is wrong here because it's pull-based with a cache; live state is push-based with no canonical URL to invalidate.

recall.q: Why is React Query the wrong tool for the live stats stream, even though it's used elsewhere in the design?
recall.model: React Query is a cache for HTTP requests keyed by query keys. Its mental model is 'fetch this URL and remember the answer.' Live stats have no URL — they're a push stream of events. There's no key to invalidate, no idempotent fetch to retry, and the data is a sequence not a value. RQ is right for the initial snapshot and the polling-fallback path; the real-time path belongs to a WebSocket + reducer. Forcing live ticks through RQ would mean spamming setQueryData and fighting cache semantics for no benefit.

recall.q: Why must reconnect logic use exponential backoff with jitter, and what goes wrong without it?
recall.model: Without backoff, a server outage causes every disconnected client to reconnect immediately, then reconnect immediately again on the next failure — a thundering herd that prevents the server from ever recovering. Exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s ...) gives the server breathing room. Jitter (random ±) is critical because synchronized clients all retrying at exactly t=2s is itself a herd. With backoff + jitter you get a smooth, diffuse retry pattern that lets the server come back up.

recall.q: Walk through the reconciliation step on WebSocket reconnect and explain why you can't 'just resume.'
recall.model: While the socket was down, events were emitted on the server but never delivered to this client. Local state therefore lags reality by an unknown delta. Resuming the subscription would just continue from now, leaving the gap silently broken — gold history would have a hole, kill counts could be wrong forever. The fix: call GET /api/matches/123/state to get a full snapshot from the source of truth, replace local state with it, then resume the subscription. This is idempotent and correct, even if you don't know how long the disconnect was.

recall.q: Why does the design treat the connection-status indicator as a first-class UI element rather than a hidden technical detail?
recall.model: On a live-events product, trust comes from the user knowing whether what they're looking at is real-time or stale. A silent transport switch (WebSocket -> polling) without a visible signal makes the user blame us when their data is 'wrong.' A green 'Live' / yellow 'Reconnecting' / grey 'Showing cached data' chip turns a scary outage into an honest, calm experience: 'we know, here's the last known data, we're working on it.' It also lets us avoid worse fallbacks like full-screen errors or spinners over existing data.

recall.q: Why is the event ticker a virtualized ring buffer of the last 100 events?
recall.model: Events stream continuously; over a long match the total count is unbounded. Without a cap, memory grows forever and the DOM tree becomes a scroll-jank machine. Virtualization renders only the visible rows; the ring buffer caps state size to the window the user actually wants to see. Old events are semantically disposable on a ticker — anyone wanting history would use the dedicated event log feature, not the ticker.

recall.q: Why is the leaderboard polled at 60s with stale-while-revalidate instead of pushed?
recall.model: Leaderboard standings only change after a match ends, which is on the order of every 30+ minutes. Holding a WebSocket subscription for that update rate is wasteful — you'd pay realtime cost for data that almost never moves. RQ polling at 60s is cheap, and stale-while-revalidate means the user always sees the cached standings instantly while a refetch runs in the background. They never see a spinner, and the data is at most 60s stale, which is well within human perception for standings.
keyPoints: Save WS budget for hot data

recall.q: Why is the degradation strategy (Full -> Degraded WS -> Degraded API -> Minimal -> Offline) the most important architectural decision for a Worlds dashboard, per the source's explicit advice to Fernando?
recall.model: On a normal day the happy path is fine. On Worlds finals night with 2M concurrent users, something will be on fire — a WebSocket region, an API tier, a CDN edge. The dashboard's job in those moments isn't 'be perfect,' it's 'be informative and never lie.' Each tier defines exactly what the UI shows: live updates, then a polling fallback with a banner, then cached data with a timestamp, then last-known with a 'we're having trouble' message, then a Service Worker cache offline. The user always sees something useful and always knows how stale it is. That's what earns trust during the moments that matter most for the brand.

scenarios.q: Fernando: 'Imagine the WebSocket is delivering 100 gold-tick messages per second across multiple matches. Walk me through your render strategy end-to-end.'
scenarios.model: (1) Network: enable permessage-deflate so JSON deltas compress ~70%, cutting bytes on the wire. (2) Subscription scoping: only the selected match has a detail subscription; the other matches send tiny summary frames, so we're not actually rendering 100 detailed updates per second per card across N matches. (3) Ingest: onmessage parses and pushes into a useRef pending buffer — not state. No dispatch yet. (4) Coalesce: a single requestAnimationFrame loop drains the buffer once per frame, dispatches one BATCH_UPDATE action with the array of events, and clears the ref. So 100 msgs/sec collapses to ~60 dispatches/sec maximum. (5) Reduce: the reducer applies all events atomically; the state object's reference changes once. (6) Bail out: each StatsCard is React.memo'd on its specific slice, so only cards whose data actually changed re-render — a gold tick re-renders GoldDiffCard, not KillScoreCard. (7) Render: GoldDiffCard contains a Canvas chart that redraws imperatively on its own rAF, appending the new point — no per-point DOM. (8) Threshold notifications: in the reducer, check 'crossed 5000?' and surface via a separate toast queue. (9) If we ever fall behind: drop intermediate gold ticks (keep only the latest per matchId) inside the BATCH_UPDATE handler — gold diff is a level, not a counter, so coalescing is lossless. (10) Telemetry: measure dropped-frame rate and message-to-render latency; if it climbs, we have a feedback signal to tighten coalescing further or push more aggregation server-side.

scenarios.q: Mid-match, telemetry says the dashboard is running 5 seconds behind live for some users. What do you show the user, and what do you do under the hood?
scenarios.model: First principle: never silently lie. If we know we're behind, we say so. UI: drop the 'Live' indicator to 'Slight delay (~5s)' in yellow rather than green; tooltip explains 'showing data ~5s behind live, working on it.' On the chart, optionally render a faint 'live edge' line showing where real-time would be if we were caught up. Don't blank anything out — degraded > empty. Under the hood: (1) measure the server-time vs client-applied-time gap on each message and expose it as a useConnectionLag hook; the indicator reads from there. (2) If lag > threshold, increase rAF batch size — apply more events per frame to catch up faster — and stop animating CSS transitions on the chart so we render data, not animations. (3) If lag continues to grow, escalate to a snapshot fetch (GET /api/matches/123/state) and replace local state, treating it as a soft reconnect. (4) Telemetry: emit lag percentiles to our observability stack so we know if this is a single-user issue (their network) or a backend incident. (5) Suppress notification toasts older than the current lag (don't toast a pentakill that the user is about to see in the chart five seconds from now). The thread the user pulls on is honesty + fast catch-up; the thread the team pulls on is observability + snapshot refresh.

scenarios.q: Fernando: 'During Worlds, we have 2M concurrent users on this dashboard. As the frontend engineer, what observability do you put in place so we know whether the experience is healthy?'
scenarios.model: Three buckets. (1) Transport health, per user: WebSocket connect/disconnect/reconnect counts, time-to-first-message after subscribe, message-apply latency (server-stamped time vs client-applied time), backoff attempts. Bucket by match-id and region so we can see if one region is broken. (2) Render health: dropped frame rate (Long Tasks API + Performance.now around rAF batches), time from message receipt to paint, React commit duration on the dashboard route. Surface p50/p95/p99 — averages lie. (3) UX outcomes: how often we enter each degradation tier (Full / Degraded WS / Degraded API / Minimal / Offline), how long we stay there, and how that correlates with users leaving the page (page-hide events). Plus error rates by route, JS errors with breadcrumbs, and CLS/INP/LCP from web-vitals on the dashboard. Pipe everything into a streaming sink (e.g. ClickHouse / Honeycomb) with sampling — at 2M users you can't ship every event, so adaptive sampling: 100% of errors and degraded-tier transitions, 1-5% of healthy traffic. Build a Worlds-night dashboard for on-call with: connected users, p95 message-apply latency, % users in degraded tier, dropped-frame rate, top 10 error signatures. The success criterion: we should know within 60 seconds that something is wrong, and from where, before users start tweeting.

scenarios.q: A new PM proposes: 'Just push every event for every match to every client — it's simpler.' Walk through your pushback.
scenarios.model: I'd validate the goal — simpler client code, fewer subscription states — and then explain why the cost makes it a non-starter. (1) Bandwidth: at Worlds we have 10+ live matches, each ticking many events/sec; pushing all to every client is roughly 10x the bytes per user. On mobile networks during a stadium event that's actively painful and may evict users. (2) Client CPU: every message is parsed, reduced, possibly rendered. Even with rAF batching and React.memo, the reducer itself touches state for matches the user can't see — pure waste. Battery cost on phones is real. (3) Server fan-out: Redis Pub/Sub fan-out cost grows with subscribers per channel. Pushing all matches to all clients means every backend node fans the union of all match channels to every connection — that's how you melt a WebSocket gateway. (4) Failure blast radius: a noisy match (teamfight) now affects every client, not just the people watching it. (5) The current design isn't actually that complex for the client — one summary subscription that's always on, plus one detail subscription tied to ?match=. About 20 lines of cleanup logic. The 'simplicity' the PM wants is real but cheaper paid for with a small useEffect than with 10x infrastructure cost. I'd offer the PM the actual win they want — 'I want fast switching between matches' — by prefetching detail for hovered matches, which gives instant switch without paying the all-channels tax.

requirements.preview: The functional scope and brutal non-functional bar — 2M concurrent, 2-3s latency, graceful degradation — that frames every later decision.
requirements.priorQuestion: Before reading: for a live dashboard serving millions of concurrent users, what matters more — a beautiful happy path or a resilient failure path? Argue your side.

recite.q: In your own words, justify 'stale data is better than no data' as a non-functional requirement to a product manager who wants loading spinners to be honest.
recite.model: During a live event, an empty dashboard reads as 'the site is broken' and users leave. Showing last-known data with a timestamp reads as 'we're still with you, the stream hiccuped.' Users accept 30-second-old scores; they don't accept a blank panel. Honesty about staleness is a small label; honesty via spinners destroys the experience the product exists to deliver.

recite.q: Why is 'mobile-first' a harder requirement for this dashboard than for a normal web app?
recite.model: Most live-event viewers are on phones on cellular networks during a commute or couch watch — constrained bandwidth, constrained CPU, constrained screen. A desktop-first dashboard with dense grids, heavy charts, and multi-panel layouts falls apart. Mobile-first forces decisions about which stats are essential, how panels stack, and which live features gracefully degrade on slow connections.

architecture.preview: How two subscription tiers — summary-for-all, detail-for-one — keep bandwidth sane when millions of users watch many matches at once.
architecture.priorQuestion: Before reading: if every client subscribed to every live match's full stats, what breaks first — client CPU, client bandwidth, or the server fan-out?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why the dashboard uses two subscription tiers instead of one fat 'all matches, all detail' channel.
recite.model: One channel forces every client to pay bandwidth for every stat of every match, even though they're only looking at one at a time. Two tiers let the summary channel carry a tiny score/status payload per match — cheap to broadcast to everyone — while the detail channel streams heavy stats to only the clients watching that match. Total bandwidth drops dramatically and server fan-out stays proportional to actual interest.
keyPoints: detail scales to attention not to match count

recite.q: Why are leaderboard standings kept in Redis sorted sets instead of computed from Postgres on each request?
recite.model: Sorted sets give O(log N) inserts and rank lookups — a 10M-user ZRANK is about 23 operations. Postgres would need an index scan or a materialized view per query, and materialized views still lag behind writes. Redis sorted sets are purpose-built for ranking and handle both 'top 100' and 'where am I' in the same structure.

components.preview: The dashboard tree — sidebar, main panel, leaderboard, event ticker — built so only the card receiving data re-renders.
components.priorQuestion: Before reading: if a parent component renders ten stat cards and one card gets a new value, what's the default React behavior, and how do you prevent the other nine from re-rendering?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why each stat card in StatsGrid is wrapped in React.memo, and what would happen if it weren't.
recite.model: Without memo, any state update in the parent re-renders every child card, even ones whose data didn't change. With ten cards, that's ten times the reconciliation for every gold tick. React.memo gives each card its own render gate based on prop equality, so only the card whose props changed runs. It's cheap isolation and it's exactly what dashboards with high-frequency updates need.
keyPoints: parent re-render cascades to all children

recite.q: Why is ConnectionIndicator a top-level component rather than embedded inside each stat card that depends on the connection?
recite.model: Every card depends on the connection, so embedding a per-card indicator would be ten duplicate widgets saying the same thing. A single top-level indicator communicates the global state once, cleanly. If individual cards need to look visually 'paused' when disconnected, they can subscribe to the same context without rendering their own indicator UI.
keyPoints: cards subscribe without rendering their own indicator

state.preview: The split between React Query, WebSocket subscriptions, and ring buffers — each tuned to how fast its data actually changes.
state.priorQuestion: Before reading: why would you poll a leaderboard every 60 seconds instead of pushing updates over WebSocket?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why leaderboard standings use 60-second polling while match stats use WebSocket push.
recite.model: Standings change only when a match ends — maybe every 30 minutes during a tournament day. Push infrastructure for something that rarely changes wastes connections for no freshness benefit. Polling every 60s is well within the data's natural update rate, costs nothing extra, and matches HTTP caching cleanly. Match stats change every second, so only push keeps up.
keyPoints: push is overkill for low-frequency data

recite.q: Why unsubscribe from 'match:123:detail' the moment the user deselects that match?
recite.model: The detail channel ships heavy payloads. If I leave it subscribed after the user moves away, the server keeps fanning out data the client no longer needs, and the client keeps paying CPU to parse messages for an unrendered panel. Eagerly unsubscribing returns bandwidth and server state to the pool — critical when millions of clients are churning between matches.

data-flow.preview: The step-by-step path a gold-diff tick takes from WebSocket frame to a single re-rendered card, plus how reconnect reconciles state.
data-flow.priorQuestion: Before reading: after a WebSocket reconnect, your client might be missing events that arrived during the outage. What's the safest way to get back in sync?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why the reconnect path fetches full state via REST instead of asking the WebSocket to replay missed events.
recite.model: Replay requires the server to buffer events per client, which doesn't scale to millions, and makes the client reason about exactly-once delivery. A REST 'give me current state' request is cheap, idempotent, and authoritative — the client throws away its stale local state and starts fresh from the server's view. Then the WebSocket just needs to push forward from that moment, no replay needed.

recite.q: Why trigger a NotificationToast when gold diff crosses a threshold, instead of on every tick?
recite.model: Every-tick notifications are noise — they'd fire constantly and users would tune them out or disable them. A threshold crossing is an event: 'this match just became a stomp.' Toasting on state transitions rather than state values turns the feed into meaningful moments. It also means the toast logic reads current-vs-previous, not absolute value.

performance.preview: The render isolation, Canvas charting, compression, and SVG sprite tricks that let one dashboard survive a 2M-user spike.
performance.priorQuestion: Before reading: what kinds of data get the biggest win from permessage-deflate WebSocket compression, and which kinds get none?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why live charts use Canvas instead of SVG or DOM-based charts at this scale.
recite.model: DOM and SVG charts create a node per data point. A few hundred gold ticks turns into a few hundred DOM elements, which means layout, paint, and memory overhead per update. Canvas is a single <canvas> element with a bitmap; drawing thousands of points is just pixel operations and doesn't touch the DOM. That keeps the mobile browser happy even with long-running matches.
keyPoints: DOM element per point doesn't scale

recite.q: Why does permessage-deflate give big wins on WebSocket JSON payloads specifically?
recite.model: JSON is verbose — repeated field names, brackets, whitespace — and live update messages reuse the same schema over and over. Deflate thrives on that kind of redundancy, commonly cutting payload size 50-80%. Binary formats would compress less but are harder to debug; deflate-over-JSON is the sweet spot of developer ergonomics and bandwidth savings.

edge-cases.preview: The degradation tiers that define what 'live dashboard' means when WebSockets fail, APIs slow down, or the network dies entirely.
edge-cases.priorQuestion: Before reading: sketch four degradation tiers for a live dashboard, from 'fully healthy' down to 'offline.' What's the user-visible difference between each?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why the degradation strategy is 'the most important architectural decision' for this dashboard, not a side concern.
recite.model: At Worlds scale, something will break — a region's WebSocket fleet, a downstream service, a cellular network. The happy path is easy to design for because it's what demos look like. The degraded paths are where millions of users experience the product during the biggest moments. Designing them first means the system has known, tested behaviors under stress instead of improvising with bad outcomes.
keyPoints: happy path is the easy case

recite.q: If WebSockets drop but the API is healthy, why fall back to 10-second polling instead of 1-second polling to 'feel real-time'?
recite.model: 1-second polling from millions of clients is a DDoS against your own API — the API is healthy precisely because WebSockets are absorbing the fan-out load. 10-second polling is enough to keep the dashboard feeling alive while respecting that the API tier can't take socket-scale traffic. Paired with a banner, users understand updates are paused; they don't need per-second freshness during a fallback.
keyPoints: API tier sized for REST not push volume

mcq.q: Why is the Pick'Em prediction lock enforced server-side rather than relying on the client countdown?
options: Server-side enforcement is faster than client validation
Client clocks can be manipulated, so a client-only lock would let users submit after match start
React Query cannot run timers, so the server must enforce timing
GraphQL mutations are inherently more secure than REST
mcq.explanation: Never trust the client for timing enforcement. Users could change their system clock or modify JS to bypass a client-side lock and submit predictions after the match outcome is partially known. The server is the authority; the client countdown is cosmetic, with a ~5s buffer to avoid frustrating last-second rejections.

mcq.q: Why are the global leaderboard queries backed by a Redis sorted set rather than a Postgres ORDER BY?
options: Redis sorted sets persist data more reliably than Postgres
ZADD/ZREVRANGE/ZREVRANK give O(log N) ranking operations that scale to 10M users without a table scan
Postgres cannot store integer scores
Redis is the only datastore that supports cursor pagination
mcq.explanation: For 10M users, finding a user's rank in Postgres would require a full scan or an expensive materialized view. Redis sorted sets handle insert, top-N, and rank queries in O(log N) — about 23 operations for 10M users. That's why the leaderboard is the canonical Redis sorted set use case.

mcq.q: Why does the design forbid querying friends' predictions until the user has locked their own?
options: It reduces Postgres load
It prevents the user from copying friends' picks before submitting their own
It is required by GDPR
Friends' picks are stored in a separate database that is only available after lock
mcq.explanation: If you could see friends' picks before submitting yours, you could just copy the highest-rated player. The visibility constraint is enforced at the API layer (not just the UI) precisely because hiding the data in the UI is not actually hiding it.

mcq.q: Where should the selected leaderboard tab (Global / Friends / Group) be stored?
options: useState in LeaderboardPage
Redux global store
URL search param like ?tab=friends so the view is shareable and survives refresh
localStorage so the user's preferred tab persists across visits
mcq.explanation: URL state makes the view shareable, bookmarkable, and back/forward-friendly. A user pasting their friend group leaderboard link in Discord should land on exactly the right tab without extra clicks.

mcq.q: After a successful submitPrediction mutation, what should happen to the React Query cache?
options: Nothing — the optimistic update already matches reality
Force a hard reload of the page to refetch
Clear the entire query cache
Invalidate ['bracket', tournamentId] only — predictions are derived from the bracket
mcq.explanation: If the optimistic update accurately reflects what the server saved (selected team for that match), no visible state change is needed on success. The optimistic state already matches reality. You only roll back on failure.

mcq.q: Why batch rapid prediction submissions instead of firing one mutation per pick?
options: Browsers cap concurrent fetches at 6 — one big request avoids the limit
Reduces network chatter and lets the server validate the picks atomically when the user makes 5 picks within 2 seconds
Optimistic updates only work with batched mutations
GraphQL does not support multiple mutations per request
mcq.explanation: Users often make many picks in quick succession. Debouncing and sending submitPredictions with all the queued picks reduces chatter, gives clean atomic semantics, and simplifies retry. Single-pick mutations still work — the batch is an optimization for rapid input.

recall.q: Why is the bracket structure rendered as SVG and pre-laid-out at build time?
recall.model: The bracket layout is static for a known tournament format — only the team data and results change. SVG scales cleanly across devices, animates well for selection states, and pre-rendering the layout means the dynamic data overlay (predictions, results) is the only runtime work. This keeps render cost low even on mobile.
