keyPoints: Layout is structural, not data — pre-render it
Only predictions and results are dynamic

recall.q: Why should the lock countdown UI flip to 'Locked' a few seconds before the actual server lock time?
recall.model: The server is the authority and rejects writes after the real lock time. If the client lets users submit at T-0.5s, network latency means the request can land after T+0 and get rejected — confusing the user. A 5-second client buffer (UI says 'locked' at T-5s) prevents these last-second failures while still being technically permissive on the server.
keyPoints: Network latency can push submissions past lock
Client buffer prevents 'failed at the buzzer' UX
UI is a cosmetic guard, not enforcement

recall.q: Why is React Query an appropriate fit for the bracket and predictions data, vs Redux?
recall.model: Both bracket and predictions are server state with clear cache keys. React Query handles caching, background revalidation, optimistic updates with rollback, and request deduping out of the box. Redux would require hand-rolling all of that. React Query also keys by tournament/user ID so navigating between tournaments hits cache.

recall.q: Why use cursor pagination for the global leaderboard rather than offset pagination?
recall.model: The leaderboard is millions of rows and constantly being updated by the worker that processes match results. Offset pagination drifts as ranks shift between requests (you can see the same user twice or miss someone). Cursor pagination over the sorted set produces a stable, repeatable scroll. It also pairs naturally with ZREVRANGE.
keyPoints: Sorted set reshuffles as scores update
Offsets are unstable under concurrent writes

recall.q: Why does the design queue predictions to localStorage when offline?
recall.model: Mobile is the dominant platform and connectivity drops in arenas, on transit, etc. Losing a pick to a transient network failure feels like a bug. Persisting queued mutations to localStorage with a PredictionSync component that listens for the 'online' event lets the app replay the writes when the connection returns — without the user noticing.
keyPoints: Don't lose work to a dropped connection

recall.q: Why virtualize the global leaderboard table and add a 'Jump to my rank' affordance?
recall.model: Rendering millions of DOM rows would crash the browser and waste memory. Virtualization renders only the visible window. But for a giant list, scrolling to your own rank manually is hostile — 'Jump to my rank' (and an around-me view of ±25) gives the user the one position they actually care about in one click.
keyPoints: DOM cannot hold millions of rows
Around-me view satisfies the main use case

recall.q: Why is 'engagement after bracket bust' an important design problem, and how does the daily-pick layer solve it?
recall.model: Most brackets bust by day three of a major tournament. Once a user's main bracket is dead they have no reason to come back, killing retention through the rest of the event. Layering per-match daily predictions on a separate points track gives a parallel game: even with a busted bracket, you can climb a daily leaderboard. Two games in one product.
keyPoints: Brackets bust early — main game ends fast
Need a parallel reason to return
Daily picks on separate points track
Long-term + short-term game in one app

recall.q: Why should lock times be displayed in the user's local timezone instead of the tournament's home timezone?
recall.model: Pick'Em is a global product. Telling a user 'locks at 2 PM PDT' forces them to do timezone math and risks them missing the window. Showing 'Locks at 11:00 PM your time' is unambiguous and matches their actual schedule. The server still stores everything in UTC; the conversion is a render-time concern.
keyPoints: Global users can't all do PDT math

scenarios.q: A user taps a team to lock their pick at the buzzer, but their network drops mid-request. Walk through the UX you'd build for that moment.
scenarios.model: Optimistically highlight the picked team immediately and show a subtle 'Saving...' indicator. Fire submitPrediction. On network failure, do not silently roll back at the buzzer — instead, queue the mutation in localStorage via PredictionSync, keep the optimistic UI, and show 'Saved locally — will sync when online.' When the 'online' event fires, replay. If the server rejects on replay because the lock has passed, then roll back the highlight and surface a clear 'Couldn't lock in time' error with the actual reason. The 5-second client buffer before lock makes this case rare in the first place.
keyPoints: Persist queue to localStorage; don't drop the pick
Clear rollback message only if server actually rejects

scenarios.q: Under peak Worlds load the leaderboard service is straining. PM asks: is it OK if leaderboards are 30-60 seconds stale? Make the call and explain.
scenarios.model: Yes — embrace eventual consistency for the leaderboard. Points are recomputed by a background worker after each match anyway, so 'fresh' is already a soft concept. Cache the top N pages in Redis with a 30-60s TTL and serve from cache. The personal 'my rank' card can refetch on focus or have a slightly shorter TTL. The user-visible cost is minimal: ranks barely shift between matches, and being one minute behind is invisible compared to the cost of a melted Redis cluster. Be explicit in the UI with a 'Updated 30s ago' label so users understand.
keyPoints: Personal rank can be slightly fresher

scenarios.q: Sketch how you'd let users change a prediction freely up until match start, including the data flow and any race conditions.
scenarios.model: Each tap on a TeamOption replaces the prediction for that match. Optimistic update flips the highlight, fire submitPrediction (or a debounced batch if they're click-storming). React Query keys by ['predictions', userId] so the local cache always reflects the latest pick. Server-side, accept any submission until match.status flips to LOCKED — the lock job is the only writer to that flag. Race condition: a pick fired at T-1s arrives at T+0.2s. Server rejects with 'LOCKED'; client rolls back to the previously confirmed pick (snapshot via React Query onMutate context), shows a toast. The client buffer at T-5s makes this nearly impossible in practice. For changes-while-offline, the localStorage queue replays on reconnect and accepts the 'LOCKED' rejection gracefully.
keyPoints: Client buffer hides race in normal cases

requirements.preview: The functional scope of a bracket game plus the one non-negotiable: locks must happen before match start, no grace-period exploits.
requirements.priorQuestion: Before reading: why does 'lock reliably before match start' force architectural decisions that a simple form-submission app wouldn't need?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why 'engagement after elimination' is a requirement, not a growth-hack afterthought.
recite.model: Most brackets bust by day 3 of a tournament. If the app is only about the main bracket, 70% of users have no reason to return after the first weekend — and they're exactly the users you want coming back. Baking in daily predictions, per-match picks, or second-chance brackets from the start means the product has a reason to exist for eliminated users, not just survivors.
keyPoints: retention drop cliff without secondary game

recite.q: Why argue with a PM who says 'let's give users a 30-second grace period after lock time so we don't frustrate last-second submitters'?
recite.model: A grace period creates a soft boundary adversaries exploit — someone can watch the match start, see an early play, and still submit a 'prediction.' The integrity of the whole leaderboard depends on hard lock enforcement. Better to solve the frustration with a client-side 5-second buffer that locks early, not with a server-side grace period that lets information leak.

architecture.preview: How Postgres, Redis sorted sets, and a server-authoritative lock check combine to make leaderboards fast and predictions honest.
architecture.priorQuestion: Before reading: leaderboard of 10M users, 'what's my rank?' query. Postgres or Redis? Name one reason per side.

recite.q: Explain in your own words why predictions live in Postgres but leaderboards live in Redis — and why you need both.
recite.model: Predictions need transactional guarantees: 'lock all predictions for a match atomically, reject late writes, never lose a submission.' That's Postgres territory. Leaderboards need fast ranked reads across millions of users, which Postgres does poorly without expensive indexes or materialized views. Redis sorted sets give O(log N) rank lookups. Each store owns the job it's best at; neither could do both well.

recite.q: Why say 'the server is the authority' for lock timing, even though the client shows a visible countdown?
recite.model: Anything the client enforces can be tampered with — clocks changed, JS disabled, requests replayed. A client countdown is a UX affordance to tell the user what's happening; the real check happens when the server receives the mutation and compares against match.start_time. That's what makes cheating impossible even if a user opens devtools.

components.preview: The component tree for a bracket, leaderboard, and group experience — with a background sync layer for offline picks.
components.priorQuestion: Before reading: a user makes a pick on the subway with no signal. Where in your component tree would you put the logic that syncs it once they're back online?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why PredictionSync is a top-level background component instead of logic inside BracketPage.
recite.model: Picks might be made on the bracket page, but they need to survive navigation. If the user makes a pick and immediately taps through to the leaderboard, sync logic scoped to BracketPage unmounts with the route and the pick is lost. A top-level component lives as long as the app and owns the pending queue across routes. It's infrastructure, not page logic.

recite.q: Why render the bracket as SVG rather than nested divs and CSS grid?
recite.model: Brackets have lines connecting matches across rounds — trivial in SVG with <path>, painful in DOM with absolutely-positioned pseudo-elements. SVG also scales cleanly on retina and small screens without rebuilding layout math. The match nodes can still be interactive via <foreignObject> or by layering SVG lines behind DOM nodes. It's the right tool for 'graphic with connections.'

state.preview: Where each piece of state lives — React Query, URL params, local countdowns — and why lock timing is the only one that must be dead-correct.
state.priorQuestion: Before reading: you're showing a countdown to lock time. If the user's device clock is 3 minutes fast, what should your UI do?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why friends' predictions are fetched only after you've locked your own — and why the server enforces it, not just the UI.
recite.model: If the UI alone gated visibility, anyone with devtools could fetch the predictions endpoint directly and see friends' picks without submitting theirs. That lets them copy. Enforcing at the API means the endpoint checks your own submission state before returning data. The UI hint is a usability thing; the API check is the actual rule. Never rely on the client to enforce a game rule.

recite.q: Why use optimistic mutations for prediction submissions instead of waiting for server confirmation?
recite.model: Picking a team should feel immediate — any latency makes the bracket feel laggy, especially on mobile. Optimistic updates flip the UI right away and fire the mutation in the background. On success, the optimistic state matches reality and nothing changes. On failure, revert and show an error. The user gets snappy feedback 99% of the time and explicit feedback in the error path.

api.preview: The GraphQL shapes for brackets, predictions, and leaderboards — including the batch submit that handles rapid multi-pick flows.
api.priorQuestion: Before reading: a user makes 15 picks in 10 seconds. Should each one be its own API call, or batched? What's the trade-off?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why SubmitPredictions takes an array and returns both 'submitted' and 'failed' lists.
recite.model: Rapid picking produces bursts you want to batch to save round-trips, but inside a batch some picks might fail — maybe one match just locked. A single boolean response is too coarse; either all or nothing. Returning per-prediction submitted/failed lets the client keep the successful ones and surface exactly which pick failed and why, without retrying everything.

recite.q: Why does GetLeaderboard return both myRank and an edges array in the same query?
recite.model: Users almost always care about two things: 'where am I' and 'who's around the top.' Two separate queries means two round-trips and two loading states. One response that contains myRank (for the 'your rank' badge) and the top edges (for the leaderboard list) means one request, one render, no flicker. It mirrors the dual nature of the UI.
keyPoints: users want both views at once

data-flow.preview: The full pick lifecycle — optimistic update, batch debounce, offline queue, online replay — that makes picking picks feel instant even on the subway.
data-flow.priorQuestion: Before reading: user taps a team to pick it, app is offline. What exactly should happen in the next 100ms, and what should happen when the network comes back?

recite.q: In your own words, justify debouncing rapid picks into a batched submitPredictions call instead of firing one mutation per tap.
recite.model: One mutation per tap means 15 round-trips for a rapid fill-in — expensive and prone to ordering issues if some land out of order. Debouncing by 2 seconds lets us collect a burst and send one mutation, which is faster on the wire, cheaper on the server, and reorder-proof. The small delay is invisible because optimistic UI already reflects the picks.

recite.q: Why queue offline picks in localStorage and replay on 'online' event instead of asking the user to retry manually?
recite.model: Users on mobile constantly lose and regain connectivity — elevators, tunnels, spotty wifi. Asking them to retry kills the experience. Queueing to localStorage means picks survive tab reloads and device sleeps; listening for the 'online' event and replaying is automatic. The user experiences 'I picked, it saved' even if reality is 'I picked, it queued, then saved 3 minutes later.'

performance.preview: The SVG bracket, virtualized leaderboard, and pre-rendered layouts that let the app handle tournament-day spikes without breaking.
performance.priorQuestion: Before reading: a leaderboard of 10 million rows. How do you render it so scrolling stays smooth and the initial load is fast?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why the global leaderboard is virtualized and includes a 'jump to my rank' button.
recite.model: Ten million DOM rows would crash the browser and make scrolling useless anyway. Virtualization renders only the visible window, so memory and render cost stay constant regardless of list size. 'Jump to my rank' solves the navigation problem virtualization creates — users can't scroll to rank 4,223,118, but they can teleport there. Around-me view then shows context without infinite scrolling.

recite.q: Why pre-render known bracket layouts at build time instead of computing them on the client?
recite.model: Bracket structure — which match feeds which, how rounds connect — doesn't change during the tournament. Computing it at runtime means every client pays the same layout cost repeatedly. Pre-rendering at build gives a static layout that the client just drops data into. The dynamic parts (predictions, results) are tiny overlays on a fixed skeleton. Faster first paint for everyone.
keyPoints: build-time computes once for all clients
dynamic data is a small overlay

edge-cases.preview: The messy realities — time zones, delayed matches, tiebreakers, bracket death — that separate a toy Pick'Em from a product that earns return visits.
edge-cases.priorQuestion: Before reading: a match is delayed 20 minutes and picks have already locked. Who decides what happens next — client, server, or user? Why?

recite.q: In your own words, explain how you'd handle a match that gets delayed after its lock time has already passed.
recite.model: The server is the source of truth for lock time, so it can extend the lock and re-open submissions if the match is delayed — rarely done, but it's possible. Clients poll or subscribe to updated lock times rather than caching them as immutable. The UI shows the new countdown if it's extended. Trusting the client's original cached lockTime would mean some users miss the reopen.
keyPoints: caching lock times client-side is fragile

recite.q: Why display all lock times in the user's local timezone instead of a canonical one like PDT or UTC?
recite.model: Users don't mentally translate 'locks at 2 PM PDT' when they're in Seoul or Berlin — they miss the lock because the math is annoying. Showing 'locks at 6 AM your time tomorrow' removes the cognitive tax and the off-by-one-day bugs. Time zone formatting is a small code change for a big usability and fairness win in a global product.
keyPoints: timezone math is error-prone for users

mcq.q: Why is 300ms the chosen debounce window for the search input?
options: It is the minimum delay React can handle
It is long enough to skip mid-word keystrokes but short enough to feel responsive — balancing API load with perceived latency
Browsers throttle requests below 300ms
Elasticsearch requires at least 300ms between queries
mcq.explanation: 300ms is a well-known sweet spot: shorter than human typing rhythm pauses, so it skips intermediate keystrokes, but short enough that the suggestions feel live. Combined with the <200ms autocomplete latency target, total perceived response stays well under half a second.

mcq.q: Why use AbortController (via React Query's signal) for in-flight search requests?
options: To save the user's bandwidth
To kill stale requests so old results don't flash in over new ones — race-condition safety
Elasticsearch charges per request
Required for SSR to work
mcq.explanation: Without abort, a slow request for 'T' can resolve after a fast request for 'T1' and overwrite the newer results. AbortController cancels in-flight requests when a newer one fires, eliminating that race entirely.

mcq.q: Why is the URL the single source of truth for query, filters, sort, and page?
options: URLs are faster than React state
Shareability + back/forward + cacheable per-URL React Query keys + crawlable for SEO
It is the only way to persist state across refresh
It avoids the need for a backend
mcq.explanation: URL state gives you four things at once: a paste-able link reproduces the exact view; browser back/forward works perfectly; React Query can cache results per unique URL; and Google can crawl each unique search permutation.

mcq.q: Why server-side render the initial search results page?
options: SSR is faster than CSR for all pages
Google indexes the rendered HTML, making search result pages crawlable for SEO
It eliminates the need for React Query
It avoids the 200ms autocomplete budget
mcq.explanation: Search result pages are valuable SEO surface area — 'T1 Worlds' should land on the search page. SSR gives Google the first page of results in HTML it can index, while subsequent interactions hydrate to client-side React for snappy filtering.

mcq.q: Why does the design recommend pagination for desktop and infinite scroll for mobile?
options: Mobile browsers don't support pagination
Pagination gives shareable per-page URLs and SEO; infinite scroll matches mobile's touch UX better
Infinite scroll is faster than pagination
React Query only supports infinite scroll on mobile
mcq.explanation: Each model wins on its own platform. Pagination gives crawlable per-page URLs and a 'page 3 of 10' mental model good for desktop research. Infinite scroll fits thumb-flicking on mobile. The design uses matchMedia to switch strategy.

mcq.q: Why is the React Query queryKey derived from the full set of URL params?
options: It is a React Query API requirement
It makes the cache automatically deduplicate identical searches and instantly serve cache hits when the user navigates back
URL params are the only stable strings in React
It avoids the need for a debounce
mcq.explanation: Keying on the full URL means each unique search has its own cache entry. Going back to a previous search is an instant cache hit (no spinner). It also dedupes — if two components ask for the same search, only one network request fires.

recall.q: Why use stale-while-revalidate (keep old results visible while new ones load) instead of clearing the result list?
recall.model: Blanking the screen between searches breaks the user's mental thread and feels like the app is broken. Keeping the previous results visible — perhaps with a subtle loading shimmer at the top — preserves context, lets the user keep reading, and makes the new results feel like a refinement rather than a hard reset. React Query's keepPreviousData enables this pattern.
keyPoints: Blank state breaks user's reading context
Loading indicator above results, not over them

recall.q: Why do recent searches live in localStorage but trending searches come from the server?
recall.model: Recent searches are personal — they should persist for me across sessions but stay private to my device. localStorage is the right home: per-user, no backend needed, survives refresh. Trending searches are computed from aggregate global search logs — that has to come from a backend job (hourly Redis cache) because no client has a global view of all users' queries.

recall.q: Why prefetch the detail page when a user hovers over a search result?
recall.model: Hover is a strong intent signal — most users who hover are about to click. Firing the detail query on hover means by the time they actually click, the data is already in React Query's cache and the page renders instantly. The cost is low (a few extra requests for indecisive users) and the perceived speed gain is dramatic.
keyPoints: Cheap on desktop, skip on mobile

recall.q: Why does the design have a separate empty state for 'no query yet' vs 'query returned zero'?
recall.model: They're completely different moments. 'No query yet' is opportunity — show trending, recent, popular content to inspire a search. 'Zero results' is failure — show 'did you mean', spelling suggestions, and which filters might be too restrictive. Treating them the same with a generic 'No results' page wastes the empty-query slot and gives no help on the failed-query slot.

recall.q: Why does the suggestions endpoint need its own short-TTL Redis cache?
recall.model: Autocomplete fires on every typing pause across millions of users — most of them typing the same prefixes ('T', 'Fa', 'lol'). Without a cache the Elasticsearch cluster burns CPU repeating the same completion lookups. A 60s TTL Redis cache absorbs the popular prefixes and protects ES, while the short TTL keeps suggestions fresh enough that new content shows up quickly.
keyPoints: Hot prefixes hit cache, cold ones hit ES

recall.q: Why are filters represented as removable chips in addition to the FilterDrawer?
recall.model: Chips make active filters visible at a glance — the user immediately knows why their result count is small. Tapping a chip removes the filter without re-opening the drawer. The drawer is for adding filters; chips are for understanding and removing them. Together they give a clean add/remove loop without the user wondering 'why am I seeing this set?'.
keyPoints: Drawer for add, chips for remove
Avoids 'why is the result set weird' confusion

recall.q: Why is keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Enter, Escape) explicitly called out for the suggestions dropdown?
recall.model: Power users and accessibility users live on the keyboard. A search box that forces a mouse move to pick a suggestion is broken for both groups. Arrow keys to move within suggestions, Enter to select, Escape to dismiss, and Tab to leave the input (not move within suggestions) match every other autocomplete pattern users already know — Google, GitHub, VS Code.
keyPoints: Tab leaves the input, not within it
No mouse required for the whole flow

recall.q: Why escape special characters and handle CJK characters explicitly in the URL?
recall.model: Search queries contain &, =, #, +, and spaces, which are URL-meaningful. Without encoding, 'T1 vs Gen.G & DRX' becomes a broken URL. CJK characters (Korean rosters, Japanese player names) need UTF-8 encoding. Escaping is non-negotiable for an esports product whose audience is heavily Korean/Japanese/Chinese — and for shareable URLs to actually round-trip.
keyPoints: CJK is core audience — must work

scenarios.q: Users report that old results 'flash in' over their new search before disappearing. Diagnose and fix.
scenarios.model: Classic stale-response race. The user types 'T', request fires; types '1', second request fires; the first request is slow and resolves after the second, overwriting the newer results. Two fixes that should both be in place: (1) AbortController via React Query's signal — when the new query starts, the old one is canceled and its response is dropped. (2) Stable React Query keys per unique input/URL params — even if a stale response slips through, it lands in a different cache entry and never overwrites the live one. Together they make the bug structurally impossible. As a debugging step I'd verify queryKey actually includes every input that affects the request.

scenarios.q: The search backend has 2-second p95 latency that you can't fix this quarter. What UX strategies make 2s feel acceptable?
scenarios.model: Several layers. (1) Skeleton screens immediately on input — perceived progress beats blank screens. (2) Stale-while-revalidate so previous results stay readable while new ones load. (3) Aggressive React Query caching — back-navigation and re-typing the same query are instant. (4) Prefetch on hover for detail pages so the next click feels free. (5) Show results progressively if the API supports streaming (matches first, articles second). (6) Optimistic suggestion population from localStorage recent searches before the network round-trip. (7) Honest 'Searching...' indicator with a subtle progress affordance after 1s so users know it's still working. The actual latency is 2s, but the perceived latency drops to near-zero for cached cases and feels manageable elsewhere.
keyPoints: Cache cold path to make warm path instant

scenarios.q: Marketing wants every filter change tracked as an analytics event, but filters live in URL state. Where do you fire the events?
scenarios.model: URL state is the source of truth, but the URL change itself is the event. I'd add a small effect that subscribes to searchParams changes and dispatches analytics: useEffect(() => analytics.track('search_filter_change', { q, filters, sort }), [searchParams]). This automatically captures every state change regardless of how it happened — typed, clicked, deep-linked, back-button. The alternative — instrumenting every onClick on every filter chip — is fragile and misses paste-in URLs and history navigation. One place, one source of truth, captures everything. Debounce the analytics dispatch by ~500ms so a user toggling three chips quickly fires one event, not three.
keyPoints: useEffect on searchParams catches every path
Captures back/forward and shared links too

requirements.preview: Before any architecture talk, scoping the feature set that makes an esports search actually feel alive — autocomplete, filters, shareable URLs.
requirements.priorQuestion: Before reading: if you had 5 minutes to list non-functional requirements for a public search page, which three would you lead with and why?

recite.q: In your own words, why does 'URL-persisted search state' belong in the requirements list alongside functional things like filters and sorting?
recite.model: Because a shareable URL isn't a nice-to-have for search — it changes the entire contract of the feature. If a fan can't send a Discord link to 'all T1 matches at Worlds 2025,' the feature is half-built. It also ties directly to SEO and back-button behavior, both of which show up in non-functional requirements.

recite.q: If Fernando said 'why are you calling out <200ms autocomplete as a hard NFR?', how would you defend it?
recite.model: Autocomplete is the only UI that reacts to every keystroke, so anything over ~200ms feels laggy and users start ignoring the dropdown. It's also the cheapest query to serve — it can hit a Redis cache or a completion suggester — so a tight budget is realistic. Results pages can be slower because users expect a beat after hitting Enter.

architecture.preview: How Elasticsearch, Kafka indexing, Redis, and SSR combine into a search stack that's fast, fresh, and crawlable.
architecture.priorQuestion: Before reading: why would a search team choose Elasticsearch over just running SQL LIKE queries against Postgres? Give two concrete reasons.

recite.q: Explain to a backend engineer why you'd want an async Kafka indexing pipeline between Postgres and Elasticsearch instead of writing to both stores in the same request.
recite.model: Dual-writing couples your write latency and availability to Elasticsearch — if indexing is slow or down, user writes fail. Kafka decouples them: the write commits to Postgres, an event flows through, and the index catches up. You accept eventual consistency in exchange for reliability and the ability to replay events to rebuild the index.

recite.q: If a junior engineer asked 'why bother server-rendering the search page when React can just render it client-side?', what would you tell them?
recite.model: Because Google crawls search result pages, and if the initial HTML is empty, those pages don't rank. SSR also gets the first page of results visible before JS hydrates, so users on slow connections see content immediately. For a gaming site where organic search drives traffic, losing that is a real revenue hit.

components.preview: The component tree that turns 'search for T1' into a header, filter bar, result tabs, and typed result cards — without the tree getting out of hand.
components.priorQuestion: Before reading: if you were sketching a search page, how would you split the 'filters' UI from the 'results' UI so they can evolve independently?

recite.q: In your own words, why does the tree have separate MatchResult, PlayerResult, TeamResult, and ArticleResult components instead of one generic ResultCard that branches internally?
recite.model: Each result type has genuinely different data and layout — a match needs scores and tournament context, a player needs a headshot and role, an article needs an excerpt. Forcing one component to handle all four leads to a mess of conditionals and prop unions. Separate components keep each layout simple and let designers iterate on one type without risking the others.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'we can just put the filter chips inside FilterDrawer — why have them at the top too?', how would you push back?
recite.model: Chips at the top show active filters without the user opening the drawer, so they always know what's narrowing their results. They also let users remove a filter in one click instead of reopening the drawer. The drawer is for picking filters; the chip bar is for seeing and clearing them — two different jobs.

state.preview: Why URL state, debounced local state, React Query cache, and localStorage all live in different places when you're building search.
state.priorQuestion: Before reading: you're building a search page where users can share their filtered view via URL. Where would you store the filters, and where would you store the text they're currently typing?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why search filters belong in the URL but the in-progress text input doesn't.
recite.model: Filters are stable state the user wants to share or bookmark — they're part of 'what view am I looking at.' The typing text is ephemeral; it changes many times per second and would pollute history and the URL. Each piece of state goes where its lifetime fits.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'let's just store recent searches in React state, not localStorage,' how would you push back?
recite.model: Recent searches should survive refresh and new sessions — that's the whole point. React state dies on reload, so users would lose their history every time they close the tab. localStorage persists across sessions and is perfect for small, non-sensitive data like a list of recent queries.

data-flow.preview: The actual sequence from keystroke to rendered results — debounce, suggest, URL update, AbortController, cache write.
data-flow.priorQuestion: Before reading: walk through what should happen in the 300ms after a user types 'T1' but before any API call fires. What are you doing with that time?

recite.q: If a teammate said 'React Query dedupes requests so we don't need AbortController,' how would you push back?
recite.model: Dedup only stops duplicate keys — it doesn't help when the user types 'val' then 'valorant,' because those are two different query keys. Without AbortController, the slower 'val' response can arrive after 'valorant' and clobber the fresh results. AbortController cancels the old request at the network layer so it can't win a race.
keyPoints: race condition where old response wins

recite.q: Explain in your own words why you'd show recent searches immediately on keystroke but wait 300ms before hitting the suggestions API.
recite.model: Recent searches are already in localStorage — filtering them is free and instant, so there's no reason to delay. The suggestions API costs a network round trip and server work, so debouncing avoids firing a request on every keystroke when the user is still typing. Cheap things happen now; expensive things wait for intent.

performance.preview: Debounce, stale-while-revalidate, prefetch-on-hover, and why pagination plus infinite scroll can coexist on one site.
performance.priorQuestion: Before reading: name three things that can make a search page feel slow even when the backend is fast. How would you mitigate each on the frontend?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why stale-while-revalidate makes search feel faster even though it doesn't reduce actual request time.
recite.model: It removes the blank-screen moment between queries. When the user changes a filter, the previous results stay on screen while the new ones load in the background, so the UI never feels empty. Perceived speed is mostly about not showing loading states where the user expects content.

recite.q: If Fernando asked 'why would you ever use infinite scroll on mobile but pagination on desktop?', how would you answer?
recite.model: Pagination has better SEO — each page is a distinct URL Google can index — and gives desktop users a clear mental model of 'page 3 of 10.' On mobile, touch scrolling is the dominant gesture and tapping tiny page numbers is awkward, so infinite scroll wins on ergonomics. You can detect the device with matchMedia and pick the right pattern.

edge-cases.preview: Empty queries, zero-result filter combinations, CJK characters in team names, and abuse — the cases that separate a shipped search from a prototype.
edge-cases.priorQuestion: Before reading: if a user types a query with no results, what should you show them? List two things that are more useful than 'No results found.'

recite.q: Explain in your own words why an empty query should show trending searches and popular content instead of an empty state.
recite.model: An empty query isn't a failure — it's a user who opened search without a plan. Trending and popular content turn that moment into discovery instead of a dead end. For an esports site, it's a huge opportunity to surface live matches and featured tournaments to users who'd otherwise bounce.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'CJK characters in search are an edge case, we can skip them for v1,' how would you push back at Riot specifically?
recite.model: Riot's esports audience is global — LCK team and player names are Korean, LJL is Japanese, LPL is Chinese. Skipping CJK means breaking search for entire regions on day one. It's a product-critical requirement, not an edge case. You have to URL-encode properly and make sure Elasticsearch analyzers handle those languages.

mcq.q: Why does the design use three different save strategies (auto-save, explicit save, multi-step) on one page?
options: React Query requires it
Different control affordances suit different risks: toggles want zero friction, text fields want review, dangerous actions want confirmation
Auto-save is faster than explicit save
It is required for accessibility
mcq.explanation: Forcing a 'save' button on a notification toggle is hostile (people flip ten and forget). Auto-saving a display name change on every keystroke is dangerous (typos go live). Auto-saving a password change is catastrophic. The risk profile dictates the save pattern.

mcq.q: Why does the avatar upload use a presigned S3 URL instead of routing the file through the app server?
options: S3 is cheaper than app servers
Avoids streaming large binaries through app servers, removes app-server bandwidth and timeout pressure, scales better
Presigned URLs are required for image previews
App servers cannot handle multipart uploads
mcq.explanation: Presigned URLs let the client upload directly to S3 with a short-lived credential. The app server never touches the bytes — no bandwidth hit, no timeout risk, no need to scale app instances for upload load. A background worker generates thumbnails after the upload lands.

mcq.q: Why must the password change form require the current password even though the user is already logged in?
options: It's a Postgres constraint
Re-authentication for sensitive actions — protects against session hijack and 'evil maid' shoulder-surfing
It's required for React Hook Form validation
It's needed to compute the password strength score
mcq.explanation: A logged-in session is not proof of identity strong enough to change credentials. If your session token leaks (or an attacker walks up to an unlocked laptop), you don't want them able to lock you out by changing your password. Re-authentication is the standard mitigation.

mcq.q: Why use react-hook-form per section instead of putting form values in React Query?
options: React Query cannot store strings
Form state is in-progress edit state — separating it from server state avoids polluting the cache and gives access to dirty/touched/validation tracking
react-hook-form is faster than useState
It is required for accessibility
mcq.explanation: Server state (what's saved) and form state (what the user is currently editing) are fundamentally different. Mixing them means a typo in the input visibly mutates the cached server view. RHF gives dirty tracking, validation, and touched state with minimal rerenders — exactly what server state tools don't do.

mcq.q: What is the right place for the unsaved-changes warning in the design?
options: componentDidUnmount in every form section
useBlocker (React Router) plus a beforeunload event listener, gated on form dirty state
Redux middleware
A polling check every second
mcq.explanation: useBlocker handles in-app navigation (clicking another tab in the SettingsNav). beforeunload handles browser-level navigation (closing the tab, hitting back, typing a new URL). You need both, and you only enable them when the form is actually dirty so you don't spam users with false alarms.

mcq.q: Why is the avatar preview generated with URL.createObjectURL before upload?
options: It's faster than uploading
It gives an instant local preview with no server round trip — the user sees their cropped image immediately
S3 cannot generate previews
It is required by react-easy-crop
mcq.explanation: URL.createObjectURL creates a blob URL pointing at the local File object. The img tag renders it instantly — no upload, no thumbnail job, no waiting. The actual upload happens in parallel and replaces the preview with the CDN URL on success.

recall.q: Walk through the optimistic notification toggle pattern with React Query, including rollback.
recall.model: onMutate runs first: cancelQueries(['settings']) to stop racing refetches, snapshot the current cache value, then setQueryData to flip the toggle in the cache. Return the snapshot in the mutation context. The UI re-renders instantly to the new state. If the mutation succeeds, do nothing — optimistic state already matches reality. If it errors, onError reads the snapshot from context and setQueryData restores the old state, then surfaces a toast. Optionally invalidate queries in onSettled to reconcile with server reality.
keyPoints: Snapshot via getQueryData, return in context

recall.q: Why does the password change form live in a separate section from profile edits?
recall.model: It has a fundamentally different risk and save model. Profile edits are reversible, low-risk text changes that auto-save or batch save. Password change requires re-authentication, irreversible after success, and has its own validation flow (current/new/confirm) and strength meter. Mixing it into the profile form would either expose dangerous behavior to the wrong save pattern or contaminate the simple profile form with re-auth ceremony.

recall.q: Why should the GDPR data export run as an async job rather than synchronously?
recall.model: Compiling a user's data hits multiple databases and services and can take minutes for long-tenured users. Blocking on a HTTP request would time out, hold a connection open, and lock the user into a single tab. Async job + emailed download link decouples the work from the request, lets the user close the tab, and gives them a reliable delivery channel. Link expires in 48h to limit exposure.

recall.q: Why use a 30-day grace period for account deletion?
recall.model: Deletion is irreversible and people change their minds — angry users come back, mistakes happen, accounts get hijacked and recovered. A 30-day soft-delete with a banner ('Your account is scheduled for deletion on X. Cancel?') gives users a clear undo without burdening engineering with restore-from-backup tickets. After 30 days a scheduled job cascades the hard delete across services.

recall.q: Why compress the avatar client-side with canvas.toBlob before upload?
recall.model: Phones produce 4MB+ photos. Uploading the original wastes the user's data plan, slows the upload, and is wasted bandwidth — the server is going to resize to 500x500 anyway. Resizing client-side via a canvas reduces the payload by 10-20x, makes upload progress feel snappy, and removes server-side work. The full-resolution original isn't useful for an avatar.

recall.q: How should display name availability checking work?
recall.model: Debounce 500ms after the user stops typing — longer than search debounce because users care less about live feedback here. Fire a lightweight availability endpoint, cache the result for 60s in React Query (prevents repeat checks for the same string), and show inline status: checking spinner / available checkmark / taken X. Don't block submit on it — let the server be the final authority since names can be claimed between check and submit.

recall.q: Why should each settings section be code-split?
recall.model: Most users open settings to do exactly one thing — change a notification preference, update an avatar, link Discord. They don't need the rich-text-heavy danger zone download/delete code or the MFA QR generation library on the wire. Code splitting per route means a tap on 'Notifications' downloads only that section. Smaller initial bundle, faster TTI, especially on mobile.
keyPoints: Users visit one section at a time

recall.q: Why is the MFA setup wizard step stored in URL state rather than local component state?
recall.model: Multi-step flows are exactly the case where users refresh, share screens, or get interrupted by a verification SMS. Local state is lost on refresh, forcing them to restart the wizard. URL state ('?step=verify') survives reload, lets users bookmark mid-flow if needed, and makes deep-linking from email-based verification flows possible. Trade-off: you must guard against jumping straight to step 3 without prerequisites.

scenarios.q: User is editing their email, types in a new address, then closes the tab without clicking save. What happens, and what should happen?
scenarios.model: With the current design, the form is dirty so beforeunload fires the browser's native 'Leave site?' prompt. If they confirm leave, the change is lost — and the email form should not autosave because the submit triggers re-authentication and a verification email, both heavy actions. So losing the input is correct. Belt-and-braces: stash the in-progress new email in sessionStorage so when they reopen the tab, the form repopulates with a 'You had unsaved changes — continue?' inline note. Critically, do NOT use localStorage (cross-tab leak risk for partially-typed personal data) and do NOT auto-fire the change-email mutation. The dirty-warning + sessionStorage rehydrate is the right balance.
keyPoints: Don't auto-save email changes (heavy side effects)

scenarios.q: A notification toggle is optimistically flipped, but the save fails. Walk through the exact UX you'd build.
scenarios.model: Visual: the toggle snaps back to its previous state (rollback via the snapshot in onMutate context). Toast appears immediately: 'Couldn't save — tap to retry.' Toast persists ~6s, longer than the success toast, because it's actionable. Tapping retry refires the mutation. If the second attempt also fails, escalate to a more visible inline error in the row itself ('Save failed — check your connection') so the user understands it's not a flaky one-off. Critically, don't queue silently and pretend it worked — settings changes the user thinks they made but didn't are a trust killer. Instrument the failure rate; if it spikes, that's a backend health signal.
keyPoints: Rollback to snapshot, visible toggle flip
Persistent inline error on second failure
Instrument failure rate as a health signal

scenarios.q: User has settings open in two tabs. They change their display name in tab A. How do you reconcile tab B?
scenarios.model: Two complementary mechanisms. (1) React Query refetchOnWindowFocus: when the user clicks back to tab B, React Query refetches ['settings'] and the cache updates with the new name. Cheap and zero cross-tab coordination. (2) BroadcastChannel API: tab A posts a 'settings-updated' message after a successful mutation; tab B subscribes and invalidates its settings query immediately, even without focus. BroadcastChannel is great because it's same-origin only and doesn't need a server round trip. If tab B has a dirty form with an old name, treat the conflict explicitly: show a banner 'Your display name was updated in another tab. Reload form?' rather than overwriting their in-progress edit.
keyPoints: Don't blow away dirty form — show conflict banner

requirements.preview: The deceptively hard scope of a settings page — profile, security, notifications, privacy, GDPR, and the dangerous stuff.
requirements.priorQuestion: Before reading: which parts of a settings page are safe to auto-save and which absolutely require a confirmation step? Why the split?

recite.q: In your own words, why is 're-authenticate before sensitive changes' a requirement and not just a polish detail?
recite.model: Session tokens can leak through shared devices, XSS, or a forgotten logout. Re-asking for the password before changing email, password, or deleting the account closes the gap where a drive-by attacker could take over an account. It also creates an audit trail: the user, not just the session, explicitly approved the change.

recite.q: If a junior engineer asked 'why is accessibility called out as a non-functional requirement on settings specifically?', what would you say?
recite.model: Settings is where users manage their identity, security, and privacy — excluding screen reader or keyboard-only users from those controls is a real harm, not a minor bug. It's also where dangerous actions live, so ambiguous labels or inaccessible modals can cause someone to delete their account by accident. A11y on settings is a correctness issue.

architecture.preview: Why settings needs almost no fancy backend — except for the three operations that do: avatar upload, GDPR export, and account deletion.
architecture.priorQuestion: Before reading: if you were asked to handle a 'download all my data' request at Riot, what pieces of the backend would that job need to touch?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why avatars upload via presigned S3 URLs instead of going through your app servers.
recite.model: Routing large files through app servers wastes compute, eats memory, and blocks request workers while the upload streams. Presigned URLs let the browser send bytes directly to S3, freeing your API entirely. Your backend just hands out a signed URL and later hears about the finished object — it never touches the file bytes.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'just hard-delete the account when the user clicks delete,' how would you push back?
recite.model: Users change their mind, get phished, or rage-click. Hard delete with no grace period means one bad click is irreversible. A 30-day soft delete gives users a chance to recover, gives support a window to intervene, and still satisfies GDPR. The scheduled cron does the real deletion after the grace period.

components.preview: The settings component tree — nested routes, section-scoped forms, and the UnsavedChangesGuard that watches the whole page.
components.priorQuestion: Before reading: why would you make each settings section its own route instead of rendering them all in one long scrollable page?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why nested routes per settings section is better than one giant form with tabs in component state.
recite.model: Routes give each section a deep link — users can bookmark /settings/notifications or share it with support. They also enable per-route code splitting, so opening the Profile section doesn't download the MFA wizard. And browser back/forward works naturally, which is what users expect on any web page.

recite.q: If Fernando asked 'why does UnsavedChangesGuard live above all the routes instead of inside each form?', how would you answer?
recite.model: Navigation can happen from anywhere — sidebar nav, browser back, closing the tab — and any form on the page might be dirty. A guard above the routes can watch the overall dirty state and intercept any navigation regardless of which section the user is in. Pushing it into each form duplicates logic and misses cross-section navigation.
keyPoints: one place to watch dirty state

state.preview: Three save strategies, react-hook-form per section, and how React Query and form state stay out of each other's way.
state.priorQuestion: Before reading: if you're tracking 'is this form dirty,' is that server state or local state? Why does the answer matter?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why you'd use auto-save for notification toggles but an explicit save button for the display name field.
recite.model: Flipping a toggle is a decisive action — the user has already made the decision and just wants it to stick. Typing a display name is an exploratory action — the user might try several before committing. Auto-saving every keystroke there would save garbage; an explicit button lets them review before they commit.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'let's just put form state in React Query too, it's all server data,' how would you push back?
recite.model: Form state and server state have different lifecycles. Server data is 'what's saved right now'; form state tracks 'what the user is editing, what's dirty, what's validated.' Mixing them means the UI can't tell whether a field is the saved value or a pending edit. react-hook-form owns the edit buffer; React Query owns the source of truth.

api.preview: The GraphQL surface for settings — one fat query, many small mutations, and the async job pattern for GDPR exports.
api.priorQuestion: Before reading: why might a settings page fetch everything in one big query but then use many small, scoped mutations for updates?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why RequestDataExport returns a jobId instead of the actual data.
recite.model: Compiling a user's entire data history can take minutes — it hits multiple databases and services. Holding a GraphQL request open for that long would time out and waste server resources. Returning a jobId immediately lets the client know the work started, and the user gets the result by email or polling when it's ready.

recite.q: If a junior engineer asked 'why does UpdateNotificationPreference take category, channel, and enabled instead of the whole preferences object?', how would you answer?
recite.model: Sending the whole object means every toggle fires a mutation that rewrites all preferences, which opens the door to last-write-wins bugs if two toggles happen close together. A scoped mutation describes exactly the change — 'set email for match-results to true' — so concurrent toggles don't clobber each other and the audit log is precise.

data-flow.preview: The optimistic toggle pattern — snapshot, update, mutate, rollback on error — and why it's the gold standard for preferences.
data-flow.priorQuestion: Before reading: describe what the user should see in the 100ms after they flip a notification toggle. Be specific about the order of events.

recite.q: Explain in your own words why onMutate snapshots the previous settings value before updating the cache.
recite.model: If the mutation fails, you need to put the UI back to where it was. Without a snapshot, you'd have to refetch the server — slow and reveals the failure in an ugly way. Snapshotting lets onError roll back instantly to the exact previous state, so the user sees a clean 'toggle flipped back + error toast' and no flicker.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'we should just wait for the server response before flipping the toggle — it's safer,' how would you push back?
recite.model: Waiting kills the feel of the app. A 200ms server round trip on a toggle feels sluggish, especially on mobile, and users flip multiple toggles in a row. Optimistic updates give instant feedback and are safe as long as you handle errors with a rollback and a clear toast. The server is still authoritative; the UI just assumes success.

performance.preview: Code-splitting sections, client-side avatar compression, debounced availability checks — small wins that add up on a page no one thinks about performance for.
performance.priorQuestion: Before reading: name one performance trap unique to a settings page that wouldn't apply to a typical content page. Why does it bite?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why you'd compress an avatar client-side with canvas.toBlob before uploading.
recite.model: Most avatars end up resized to 500x500 or smaller anyway — uploading a 10MB phone photo wastes the user's bandwidth and your upload pipeline. Client-side compression shrinks it to the actual target size before the bytes leave the device, so uploads are faster, cheaper, and work better on flaky mobile connections.

recite.q: If Fernando asked 'why code-split settings sections when the whole page is small?', how would you answer?
recite.model: Most users who open Settings only visit one or two sections in a session. Shipping the MFA wizard, avatar cropper, and GDPR export code to someone who just wants to change their display name wastes kilobytes and parse time. Code-splitting means each section loads its own bundle on demand, and the common case stays lean.

edge-cases.preview: Cross-tab concurrent edits, unsaved changes guards, password strength, grace periods — the unglamorous work that keeps trust.
edge-cases.priorQuestion: Before reading: a user opens settings in two tabs and changes their display name in one. What should the other tab do, and how would you implement it?

recite.q: Explain in your own words how you'd handle the 'user has settings open in two tabs' scenario and why it matters.
recite.model: You either refetch settings when a tab regains focus (React Query's refetchOnWindowFocus) or broadcast changes between tabs with BroadcastChannel. Without either, the stale tab shows outdated data and a save from that tab can overwrite the fresh change. It matters because settings is where users make security-sensitive decisions — showing them stale state is a trust bug.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'useBlocker on every page is overkill — beforeunload is enough,' how would you push back?
recite.model: beforeunload only fires when the tab is closing or the URL changes to a different origin — it doesn't fire for in-app navigation through React Router. Without useBlocker, a user can click a sidebar link and lose their unsaved edits silently. You need both: useBlocker for SPA navigation, beforeunload for tab close and hard reload.

mcq.q: Why store article content as JSON AST rather than HTML?
options: JSON is smaller than HTML
Structured data lets you validate, diff for version history, and render to multiple formats (web/AMP/email/RSS)
HTML cannot store images
React only renders JSON
mcq.explanation: HTML is a serialized output. A JSON document tree (e.g. ProseMirror's schema) is structured, validatable, diffable per-node for version history, and can be rendered to multiple targets. String-diffing HTML is brittle; node-diffing JSON gives you meaningful change tracking.

mcq.q: For a 50-100-user internal CMS, why is pessimistic locking preferred over CRDTs/OT for concurrent editing?
options: CRDTs are fundamentally broken
Pessimistic locking is dramatically simpler to implement and operate, and at this scale collisions are rare enough that 'one editor at a time' is acceptable UX
CRDTs cannot handle text
Pessimistic locking is faster
mcq.explanation: CRDTs (Yjs) and OT (Google Docs) are complex to operate, debug, and reason about. For an internal tool with low concurrency, the chance of two editors hitting the same article at the same time is small, and a Redis-backed lock with a clear 'John is editing' UI is dramatically simpler. Save the complexity for when you actually need it.

mcq.q: Why does the edit lock use a TTL with a heartbeat instead of a permanent lock?
options: Postgres requires it
If an editor walks away or their browser crashes, a permanent lock would orphan the article forever — TTL + heartbeat ensures abandoned locks expire
Redis cannot persist locks
It is required for Yjs
mcq.explanation: Without a TTL, a crashed tab leaves the article locked indefinitely. With TTL, even if the heartbeat stops the lock self-releases after 5 minutes, and another editor can take over. The heartbeat refreshes the TTL while the editor is actively present.

mcq.q: Why must role-based UI gating (RoleGate) be paired with server-side authorization?
options: RoleGate is buggy
The UI is cosmetic — an attacker can inspect the DOM, find the publish endpoint, and call it directly. The server must be the actual authority
RoleGate cannot read user roles
It is required by GraphQL
mcq.explanation: Hiding a button hides nothing from someone who knows where to look. RoleGate is a UX nicety — it stops users from seeing actions they can't perform. The mutation endpoint must independently verify role on every call and return 403 to an Editor calling Publish, regardless of what the client did or did not show.

mcq.q: Why is the rich text editor lazy-loaded with React.lazy?
options: It cannot run on the server
TipTap/Slate/Lexical are heavy bundles — the content list page doesn't need the editor, so deferring the import drops initial bundle size and TTI
React.lazy is required for SSR
Editors are insecure unless lazy-loaded
mcq.explanation: Rich text editors are some of the heaviest dependencies in a frontend (ProseMirror schema + plugins + decorators easily exceeds 200KB). The dashboard, content list, calendar, and media library don't need the editor at all. Lazy-loading it on /content/:id/edit defers the cost to the moment it's actually useful.

mcq.q: Why debounce autosave at 5 seconds rather than firing on every keystroke?
options: 5s is the Postgres write rate limit
Balances data safety with backend load — 5s of typing is one save instead of dozens, and you still don't lose more than ~5s of work
Editors lock up if you save more often
React Query doesn't allow faster mutations
mcq.explanation: Per-keystroke saves DDoS the backend and create version churn. A 5-second debounce coalesces a paragraph of typing into one save — the worst-case loss window is ~5 seconds, which combined with localStorage backup and beforeunload-flush is acceptable for editorial work. Plus localStorage as the safety net.

mcq.q: Why is the Draft → Review → Approved → Published workflow modeled as an explicit state machine instead of free-form 'edit and toggle published'?
options: GraphQL requires it
Explicit states enforce role gates, give clear audit trail, and prevent invalid transitions like a Reviewer publishing directly
It is required for SEO
It is the only way to support drafts
mcq.explanation: An explicit state machine encodes the editorial process: only Editors can move Draft→Review, only Reviewers can Approve, only Publishers can Publish. Each transition is a server-enforced rule and an audit log entry. A free-form boolean 'published' provides none of that and breaks the moment two roles disagree.

recall.q: Compare TipTap (ProseMirror), Slate.js, and Lexical for the rich text editor choice.
recall.model: TipTap is built on ProseMirror — battle-tested, opinionated schema, good plugin ecosystem, comes with collaborative editing via Yjs, slightly steeper learning curve but very stable. Slate.js is more flexible/lower-level — easier to bend to custom needs but you build more yourself, history of breaking changes. Lexical (Meta) is newer, performance-focused, smaller bundle than ProseMirror, good Yjs integration, but younger ecosystem. For a CMS I'd lean TipTap: stable schema, mature plugins, easy upgrade path to collaborative editing later, JSON output that maps cleanly to our storage.
keyPoints: TipTap is the safest CMS pick

recall.q: Why does the autosave fall back to localStorage in addition to the server save?
recall.model: Network failures happen. If a save errors out and the user closes the tab thinking they saved, that work is lost. Persisting the latest editor state to localStorage on every (or every-few) keystrokes means even a crashed tab can be recovered: 'You have unsaved changes from your last session. Restore?' is the standard pattern. The server is the canonical store; localStorage is the safety net.

recall.q: Why does the design recommend version history with diffs rather than just a single 'undo'?
recall.model: Editorial work is iterative and collaborative. 'Undo' is short-term and disappears on reload. Version history is the permanent record: who changed what, when, and (with diffs) exactly what changed between versions. Editors need to compare drafts, revert specific changes from yesterday, and answer 'who introduced this typo'. Storing every save as a version is cheap (JSON deltas) and earns trust from the content team.

recall.q: Why is the preview generated as a static page on save and opened in a new tab?
recall.model: Preview must render the draft exactly as it would appear on the live site, which means using the production rendering pipeline — not a fake in-editor approximation. Generating a static preview build on save and opening it in a new tab guarantees fidelity (same templates, same CSS, same data shape), keeps the editor tab clean, and makes the preview shareable via URL. Caching the preview with a short TTL avoids re-rendering on every refresh.
keyPoints: Use production renderer, not editor approximation
New tab keeps editor focus intact

recall.q: Why use server-side pagination for the content table instead of loading everything client-side?
recall.model: The content table can grow to thousands of items. Loading them all blows up memory, slows initial render, and forces every filter/sort to happen client-side on a giant array. Server-side pagination + server-side filter/sort scales naturally, keeps payloads small, and lets the database do the work it's good at. 25 items per page is enough for the user's working window.

recall.q: Why include featuredImage and SEO metadata in the MetadataPanel sidebar rather than inline in the editor?
recall.model: Metadata is structurally separate from content body — it has its own validation rules (SEO title length, image dimensions), its own storage shape, and the editor shouldn't need to know about it. A sidebar lets editors fill it out without breaking their writing flow, and lets the rich text editor stay focused on prose. It also makes templating per content type easier: a banner has different metadata than an article.

recall.q: Why does the design release the edit lock on beforeunload and on component unmount?
recall.model: Holding a stale lock blocks other editors. The TTL is the safety net, but proactively releasing on tab close (beforeunload) and on navigation away from the editor (unmount cleanup) frees the lock immediately so the next editor doesn't have to wait 5 minutes. The TTL still saves you when the browser crashes or the user kills the tab without firing beforeunload.
keyPoints: Don't make others wait for TTL
TTL is the crash safety net

recall.q: Why should bulk operations (publish 50, delete 50) report partial failures explicitly?
recall.model: Some items in a batch will inevitably fail — already-deleted, missing required fields, in use by the homepage, role permission denied for a specific item. Showing 'Done!' would hide those failures and confuse editors when they look for items that 'should have been' published. Explicit '47 of 50 published. 3 failed: [reason]' lets them fix the failures individually and trust the operation. Pair with a progress bar for long batches.

recall.q: Why is autosave's diff check (only send changed deltas) called out as a performance concern?
recall.model: Autosave fires on every debounce window. If the user has paused but not actually changed anything (just clicked around), sending the full document is wasted bandwidth and a wasted version row in the audit log. Diffing the current state against the last-saved state and skipping no-op saves drops backend load substantially. Sending only the delta (TipTap/Yjs supports this) compounds the savings for large documents.

scenarios.q: Two editors open the same article at the same time. Walk through the design.
scenarios.model: Editor A clicks 'Edit' — AcquireEditLock fires, server creates a Redis key with 5-min TTL keyed on the article ID. A heartbeat refreshes it every 2 minutes. Editor B clicks 'Edit' on the same article — AcquireEditLock fails because the key exists. Server returns the lock owner ('Acquired by John, expires at HH:MM'). UI shows 'John is currently editing this article. Open in read-only mode?' with a 'Request lock' button. Read-only mode renders the latest saved JSON in a non-editable view. If A walks away and the heartbeat stops, the lock self-expires after 5 minutes; B can then take over. If A explicitly clicks away or closes the tab, beforeunload/unmount releases the lock immediately and B doesn't have to wait. This is pessimistic locking — simple and right for an internal tool of 50-100 users. If we ever needed real-time co-editing, swap in Yjs + TipTap collaboration.
keyPoints: Acquire lock via Redis key with TTL
Second editor sees 'John is editing' read-only mode
Yjs is the upgrade path if needed

scenarios.q: An editor accidentally hits Publish on an unfinished article. What safety nets prevent disaster, and how do you recover?
scenarios.model: Several layers should make this hard and fixable. (1) Role gating: only Publisher role sees the Publish button. Editors can only Submit for Review. (2) Confirmation modal on Publish ('Publish this article? It will be live immediately.'). (3) State machine requires the article to be in APPROVED state before Publish is allowed — the server rejects Publish from a Draft. (4) After publish, version history captures the published state — Unpublish is one click and restores Draft status. (5) CDN cache invalidation is the slowest part — it might take 30s for the article to disappear from edge caches. For recovery: click Unpublish, status flips to Draft, CDN purge fires, audit log records the unpublish. If they want to roll back content too, the version history lets them restore the previous version. The 5-minute legal takedown scenario is a reason to invest in a fast unpublish + CDN purge path.
keyPoints: State machine requires APPROVED before Publish
CDN purge is the slowest hop — instrument it

scenarios.q: Autosave is firing too often and overwhelming the backend during a large editorial sprint. Diagnose and fix without losing the safety guarantees.
scenarios.model: First, verify the symptom: instrument the save endpoint to confirm it's autosave traffic and find which editors/articles dominate. Likely causes and fixes: (1) Debounce too short — push from 5s to 10-15s. The longer window is fine because we have localStorage backup. (2) No-op detection missing — diff against last-saved JSON and skip if unchanged. (3) Send deltas instead of full documents (TipTap/Yjs delta). (4) Coalesce concurrent writes per article on the server (last-write wins within a small window). (5) Per-user rate limit at the API gateway as a safety floor. (6) Dedicate the autosave endpoint to its own service/queue so it can't starve interactive CRUD. (7) On the client, batch the autosave with the heartbeat so we make one request instead of two. The localStorage safety net means we can be aggressive with debounce and no-op skipping without losing work — the worst case is recovering from local on next visit.
keyPoints: Increase debounce window — localStorage covers gap
No-op skip via diff against last-saved
Separate autosave service from interactive CRUD

scenarios.q: Legal calls and demands a published article be taken down within 5 minutes. What architecture makes that possible, and where are the gotchas?
scenarios.model: The CMS Unpublish button must do more than flip a database flag. The full chain: (1) UI: prominent Unpublish action behind a confirmation, role-gated to Publisher/Admin. (2) Server: state transition Published→Draft (or a dedicated TAKEN_DOWN state to preserve audit context), audit log entry with user/reason. (3) Immediately invalidate the article in the CDN — this is the long pole. CDN purge APIs can take 10-30s globally; some edge nodes longer. Use a purge-by-tag system (tag every cached asset with the article ID) and fire the purge synchronously from Unpublish before declaring 'taken down' to the user. (4) Also invalidate any aggregate pages that include this article (homepage, category pages, RSS). (5) Search index removal — delete from Elasticsearch synchronously, not via the lazy indexing pipeline. (6) Audit log captures the takedown for legal evidence. (7) Confirm to the user only after CDN purge + ES delete are both done. Gotchas: stale RSS feeds, social media unfurl caches (no control), Google cached snapshots (file a removal request), mobile app caches with their own TTLs. The 5-minute SLA is the CMS to CDN to ES path; downstream systems we don't control are best-effort with manual followup.
keyPoints: Unpublish is more than a DB flag
CDN purge by tag is the long pole
Sync ES delete, not via lazy pipeline
Confirm to user only after purge completes

requirements.preview: Scoping a CMS dashboard where non-engineers publish esports content under real workflow and permissions — draft, review, publish.
requirements.priorQuestion: Before reading: what's the difference between 'the author can publish' and 'the author can submit for review'? Why does a CMS need both concepts?

recite.q: In your own words, why is 'concurrent editing' called out as a non-functional requirement for a CMS with only 50-100 users?
recite.model: Because the users overlap — two editors often touch the same tournament preview or match recap, especially around big events. Even with a small team, the cost of silently losing someone's work is huge: it breaks trust in the tool. You need a strategy (locking or CRDTs), and deciding which is a real architectural requirement.

recite.q: If Fernando asked 'why is autosave in the requirements rather than a polish feature?', how would you answer?
recite.model: Editors write long-form content — losing an hour of work to a crashed tab or expired session is catastrophic. Autosave is the difference between a tool editors trust and one they work around by drafting in Google Docs. It also enables other features like version history and 'restore unsaved changes,' so it's foundational, not cosmetic.

architecture.preview: Why an internal tool gets a boring stack on purpose — Postgres, Redis locks, presigned S3 — and what you deliberately leave out.
architecture.priorQuestion: Before reading: if a CMS only has 100 users, which pieces of 'web scale' architecture can you skip? What's the reasoning?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why there's no Kafka or WebSocket in this architecture even though other walkthroughs use them.
recite.model: Kafka and WebSocket solve real-time fan-out and event streaming problems that matter at millions of users. For an internal CMS with 100 users, polling or a refetch on focus handles 'stay fresh' just fine, and a single REST/GraphQL API covers all the writes. Adding streaming infrastructure would mean more operational burden than problems solved.

recite.q: If a junior engineer asked 'why does Redis hold edit locks instead of Postgres?', what would you tell them?
recite.model: Edit locks are short-lived with a TTL — exactly what Redis is good at. A Postgres-based lock needs its own expiry logic, cleanup cron, and transactional overhead. Redis keys with TTL expire themselves, are cheap to read and write, and naturally handle the 'editor walked away' case. It's the right tool for the job.

components.preview: The big component tree — routes, role gates, rich text editor, media library, calendar — and why RoleGate is a UI concern only.
components.priorQuestion: Before reading: you're building a dashboard where an Editor can draft but only a Publisher can publish. How do you keep the Publish button from appearing for Editors?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why RoleGate is called 'cosmetic' even though it hides buttons based on user role.
recite.model: RoleGate only controls what the DOM shows — a determined user can inspect the page and fire the mutation anyway. The real permission check has to live on the server, which should return 403 for unauthorized actions. RoleGate is about a clean UX (don't tease a button you can't click), not security.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'let's make the ContentEditorPage render differently for new vs edit mode with two separate components,' how would you push back?
recite.model: The editor surface — toolbar, title, rich text, metadata, status bar — is identical in both modes. The only difference is whether there's an existing content ID to load and lock. Splitting into two components duplicates all the UI and doubles the maintenance cost for a trivial logic difference. One component with a routed ID param is cleaner.

state.preview: The state map for a CMS — React Query for lists, TipTap's internal state for documents, Redis-backed locks, and derived dirty flags.
state.priorQuestion: Before reading: if you were using TipTap or Slate, would you lift the document state into Redux or leave it inside the editor? Why?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why you'd store content as a JSON document tree instead of HTML.
recite.model: JSON is structured data you can validate, migrate, and diff meaningfully — two versions of an article can be compared node by node. It also lets you render the same document to HTML, email, AMP, or RSS without re-parsing. HTML is a serialization format, not a data model, so using it as the source of truth ties you to one render target.

recite.q: If Fernando asked 'why not manage the editor's document in React state so everything is controlled?', what would you say?
recite.model: Rich text editors handle thousands of keystrokes, cursor positions, and selection ranges per session — re-rendering React on every change kills performance and fights the editor's own input handling. TipTap and Slate own their document internally for good reasons. You read from them when you need to save, not on every change.

api.preview: The mutation surface — saveDraft, submitForReview, publish, acquireEditLock — and why each workflow step is its own mutation.
api.priorQuestion: Before reading: why would you design separate mutations for SubmitForReview, Approve, and Publish instead of a single UpdateStatus mutation?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why acquireEditLock is a mutation that the client must call explicitly before editing.
recite.model: Locking is a state change on the server — it reserves the document for this user and blocks others. That's a mutation by definition, not a query. Making it explicit means the client knows whether the lock succeeded before loading the editor, and can fall back to read-only mode or a 'request lock' flow if someone else holds it.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'just use one generic updateContent mutation for draft, review, and publish,' how would you push back?
recite.model: Each step has different preconditions, side effects, and permissions. Publish triggers CDN invalidation and sets publishedAt; submitForReview changes reviewer queues; saveDraft just bumps the version. Collapsing them hides those semantics and lets a bug in one path affect all three. Separate mutations are explicit and give the server clean authorization checks.

data-flow.preview: The full editing lifecycle — acquire lock, heartbeat, autosave debounce, version check, publish, CDN invalidate.
data-flow.priorQuestion: Before reading: sketch what should happen when an editor closes their tab mid-edit. What needs to clean up, and who cleans it up?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why the lock needs a heartbeat and not just a long TTL.
recite.model: A long TTL locks out other editors even when the original editor has walked away — that's bad UX. A short TTL with a heartbeat keeps the lock alive only while the tab is actually open and active. If the editor closes the tab or crashes, the lock expires within a minute or two and someone else can take over.

recite.q: If Fernando asked 'what happens if the client version is behind the server version when autosave fires?', how would you handle it?
recite.model: That means someone else saved in between — you can't just overwrite or you'd destroy their work. The server rejects the save with a version conflict, and the client shows a conflict resolution UI: 'the document was updated elsewhere, here's the diff, choose how to merge.' It's the CMS equivalent of a Git merge conflict.

performance.preview: Lazy-loading the editor, virtualizing the media grid, delta autosaves — keeping a feature-heavy dashboard feeling light.
performance.priorQuestion: Before reading: what's the single heaviest dependency on a CMS dashboard, and what's the cheapest way to keep it from slowing down the rest of the app?

recite.q: In your own words, explain why you'd lazy-load the rich text editor but not the content table.
