recite.model: The content list page is the entry point — everyone hits it, and it has to feel fast. The editor is huge (TipTap/ProseMirror pulls in a lot) and only runs when someone actually opens a document. Lazy-loading it means the content list stays light, and the editor downloads right when it's needed. Table loads eagerly, editor loads on demand.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'autosave every keystroke is fine, React Query dedupes it,' how would you push back?
recite.model: Dedup only catches identical concurrent requests — it won't stop a save firing on every letter the user types. That hammers the server and creates meaningless versions every character. Debouncing 5 seconds after the last keystroke coalesces a burst of typing into one save, and sending only the delta keeps each save small.

edge-cases.preview: Lock expiry, bulk failures, versioning, localStorage recovery — the paranoid scenarios that make editors actually trust the CMS.
edge-cases.priorQuestion: Before reading: an editor loses network mid-write, then it comes back. What are two ways you'd make sure they don't lose work?

recite.q: Explain in your own words why you'd fall back to localStorage for unsaved changes even when you have autosave to the server.
recite.model: Autosave can fail — network drops, server errors, expired sessions — and if the tab is then closed, the in-memory state dies with it. Writing the latest editor state to localStorage on every debounced save gives you a recovery path: on next visit, detect the orphaned draft and offer 'restore unsaved changes?' It's a belt-and-suspenders safety net.

recite.q: If Fernando asked 'how would you handle a bulk unpublish of 50 articles where 3 fail because they're pinned to the homepage?', what would you say?
recite.model: You don't want to abort the whole batch — the 47 that can unpublish should. Fire the batch, show a progress bar, and collect failures. At the end, report '47 succeeded, 3 failed (in use by homepage)' with a clear list and next steps. Partial success beats all-or-nothing for bulk ops on an internal tool.

mcq.q: According to publicly reported Blind posts, what is the overall shape of a Riot Games SWE onsite (including Web roles)?
options: 3 hours, 2 coding rounds + 1 system design
5 hours virtual: 2 behavioral + 1 evaluated lunch + 1 coding/depth + 1 systems design
8 hours in-person: 4 coding rounds + 2 system design + 2 behavioral
2 hours total, just system design and behavioral
mcq.explanation: Multiple Blind sources describe a 5-hour virtual loop: two behavioral interviews, an evaluated (informal but graded) lunch, a coding/depth round (often a take-home review if a take-home was done), and one systems design round. Culture fit is weighted heavily across all of them.

mcq.q: Blind posts describe Riot's 'deep tech' round. What should you expect in the final ~15 minutes?
options: A pure trivia quiz on your language of choice
A live-debugging scenario — 'how would you debug this production issue?'
Another behavioral question
A whiteboard algorithm challenge
mcq.explanation: Verbatim from Blind: 'in the last 15 minutes, there may be a question on how you would debug a live issue.' Come in with one frontend prod-bug story pre-loaded in SAO format: observable symptom → hypothesis → bisect → root cause → fix → prevention. Good categories: hydration mismatch, CDN cache invalidation, memory leak from listeners, bundle-size regression, race condition in state updates.

mcq.q: Riot's deep-tech system-design conversation has a specific 'shape' according to Blind candidates. Which framing matches?
options: Tidy diagrams are required; no diagrams = fail
Memorize one canonical architecture and recite it
High-level discussion about microservices, protocols, classes — diagrams NOT required, depth of reasoning is
Pure algorithms and Big-O analysis
mcq.explanation: Verbatim Blind quote: 'system design question that may not be in-depth (no diagrams required), but rather a high-level discussion about what microservices might be needed, what microservices are, and what types of protocols would be used.' You may also be asked to sketch OOP classes mid-conversation. Translation: conversational depth > clean whiteboard.

mcq.q: Riot's public FE job descriptions and Prepfully question summaries point to one surprising expectation for FE candidates. Which is it?
options: Full-system literacy — be ready to draw the backend first, then zoom into the client, even on an 'FE round'
Memorize React internals and fiber reconciler
Only discuss CSS and accessibility
Focus purely on component trees; never mention APIs or backend
mcq.explanation: Riot's Prepfully-reported SWE SD bank skews backend (rate limiter, Twitter-like feed, time-series pipeline, cloud monitoring, friend recommendation). On a Web role these can surface on a 'FE round,' so be ready to sketch backend first, then trace the FE client into it. The League Client FE JD literally says 'build outstanding system designs to serve millions of players reliably.'

mcq.q: What diagramming tool does Riot reportedly use during virtual system design rounds?
options: Miro
Excalidraw
Draw.io / Diagrams.net
Figma
mcq.explanation: Draw.io / Diagrams.net is the tool reported across multiple Blind threads, with Google Meet for video. Have it open in a second tab BEFORE the interview starts — no time wasted fumbling with URLs or accounts.

mcq.q: A search result labeled 'frontend-interview-challenge' comes up for 'Riot frontend challenge' and it's hosted at github.com/tryriot/... — is this from Riot Games?
options: Yes, this is the official Riot Games take-home
No — 'tryriot.com' is an unrelated HR-software company; do NOT prep this as Riot Games intel
Yes, but only for senior roles
It's a third-party recreation of Riot's real prompt
mcq.explanation: Critical disambiguation: tryriot.com is an HR-software company. Its 'frontend-interview-challenge' (Vue 3 + Tailwind employees table matching a Figma spec) is NOT Riot Games. AI summarizers frequently conflate them. The real Riot Games FE take-home lives at github.com/RiotGamesMinions/frontend_test. Also unrelated: github.com/riot/riot is the Riot.js micro-UI library.

recall.q: What are the only publicly verifiable primary-source FE prompts from Riot Games, and what do they reveal about Riot's taste in 'good' FE code?
recall.model: Three take-home prompts live at github.com/RiotGamesMinions/frontend_test (verified real Riot Games org, still live): (1) Image Rotator — cycle Katarina skins every 3 seconds with correct title/description; bonus for animation between slides. (2) Tabbed Content — functional tabbed container where clicking a tab swaps content. (3) Text Search — count occurrences of a word or phrase, with the subtle trap that 'phrases may cross HTML element boundaries' so innerHTML.match is wrong. README bans third-party libraries and says 'shouldn't take longer than a few hours.' These are TAKE-HOMES, not onsite FE system design — but they're the only primary-source signal on what Riot considers a strong FE submission: vanilla-JS fluency, creativity/finesse, and catching subtle prompt constraints.
keyPoints: Image Rotator, Tabbed Content, Text Search
take-home, NOT onsite FE system design

recall.q: Explain the 'cross-boundary text search' trap in the Riot take-home and why innerHTML.match is the wrong answer.
recall.model: The prompt: count occurrences of a word or phrase inside a DOM subtree, where 'words can be contained within multiple levels of HTML elements, and phrases may cross those HTML element boundaries.' The naive solution — read innerHTML as a string and regex-match — breaks because innerHTML embeds literal tag markup ('', '') in between the letters of a phrase, so 'Riot Games' spanning 'Riot Games' won't match 'Riot Games' in the innerHTML string. The correct approach: walk the DOM text nodes (TreeWalker or recursive childNodes traversal), concatenate their textContent in document order into a flattened string, then search that. This is the level of 'catches the subtle constraint' detail Riot rewards.
keyPoints: phrases broken by tags won't regex-match
walk DOM text nodes (TreeWalker or childNodes)
concatenate textContent in order, then search
this is the exact signal Riot grades on

recall.q: Is github.com/tryriot/frontend-interview-challenge a Riot Games prompt? What's the risk if you prep it?
recall.model: NO. tryriot.com is an unrelated HR-software company. Its challenge (Vue 3 + Tailwind employees table with an active/deactivated filter matching a Figma Community spec) has nothing to do with Riot Games. The risk: AI search summarizers and Google results frequently conflate 'tryriot' with 'Riot Games' because of the name collision. If you prep as if it were Riot's, you waste time on the wrong framework (Vue 3 + Tailwind is NOT Riot's stack) and walk in with false expectations about the prompt shape. The real Riot Games FE take-home is at github.com/RiotGamesMinions/frontend_test. Also unrelated: github.com/riot/riot is the Riot.js micro-UI library.
keyPoints: tryriot.com = HR software company, NOT Riot Games
Vue 3 + Tailwind is NOT Riot's stack

recall.q: What evaluation signals do Riot's public FE JDs and the RiotGamesMinions README point to — what are you actually being graded on?
recall.model: (1) Vanilla-JS fluency — the take-home explicitly bans third-party libraries, so expect 'how does React batch this' / 'what does this selector cost at 10k rows' rather than framework API trivia. (2) Creativity and finesse on open-ended prompts — the README says it explicitly. (3) Catching subtle prompt constraints — the text-search cross-boundary trap is the canonical example. (4) Full-system literacy on FE rounds — be ready to draw backend first, then zoom into client. (5) Scale framing for 'millions of players' (verbatim from the League Client FE JD). (6) Trade-off articulation out loud ('why X over Y'). (7) Cross-functional collaboration embedded in design discussion (designer wants X but API can't, how handle?). (8) Live-debug / observability story for the final ~15 min.
keyPoints: scale framing for millions of players

recall.q: Summarize the round-format intel from Blind for Riot's senior SWE Web onsite. What's the shape of the day and what should you prep for each piece?
recall.model: 5 hours total, fully virtual post-COVID. Panel shape: (1) 2 behavioral interviews — culture is weighted heavily, prep STAR stories around player-focus, conflict, and collaborative wins. (2) 1 evaluated lunch — informal conversation but STILL a signal; treat it as a behavioral round with no notebook. (3) 1 coding + depth-of-engineering round — often a take-home review with follow-ups if a take-home was submitted. (4) 1 systems design round — either a lightning round with various tech questions OR a full FE system design. Tool: Draw.io / Diagrams.net on Google Meet, 1-on-1 or 2-on-1 panel. Have Draw.io open in a second tab before the call.
keyPoints: lunch is evaluated, not just social
SD may be lightning-round OR deep
Draw.io on Google Meet, open in advance

recall.q: What backend-weighted prompts from Riot's Prepfully question bank should a Web candidate be ready to handle even on an 'FE system design' round?
recall.model: Prepfully summaries from Riot candidates surface these generic SWE prompts that aren't explicitly FE-tagged but show up in Web-role prep pages: (1) Develop a micro-posting service similar to Twitter. (2) Build a distributed system for time-series data. (3) Design a recommendation system for finding friends on social networks. (4) Design a platform for real-time cloud-infrastructure monitoring. (5) Design a rate limiter (the canonical Riot backend SD prompt, repeated across summaries). These are NOT confirmed as FE-round prompts, but they signal that Riot's SD bank skews backend. Your move: when you get an FE-ish prompt, sketch the backend pipeline first (queue, processor, hot store, fan-out gateway), then zoom into the client. Don't wait to be asked about the backend — narrate it voluntarily.
keyPoints: not confirmed FE-tagged but signal backend skew

scenarios.q: Hard truth: you have zero verbatim Riot FE system design prompts — deep web research (Glassdoor, Blind, Reddit, LeetCode, Medium, GitHub, Prepfully) turned up nothing. The interview is tomorrow. What's your honest prep move?
scenarios.model: Stop hunting for 'real prompts' — they're not findable. Instead: (1) Use the 7 walkthroughs in this study site as the scaffolding (Login, Match Viewing, Dashboard, Pick'Em, Search, Settings, CMS) — they already cover the framework shapes Riot would test. (2) Mentally apply gaming framing on the fly: when Fernando says 'design a search,' narrate it as 'search for champions, pro players, or matches' — Riot domain context shows you've pictured their users. (3) Prep ONE frontend prod-bug story in SAO format for the last 15 min. (4) Have Draw.io open in a second tab before the call starts. (5) Be ready for backend-weighted prompts even on an FE round — if a backend smell appears, draw backend first, then zoom into client. (6) Rehearse the player-first reframe: every technical decision ends with 'which means for the player, X.' That's the cultural tell Riot grades on and it's free points.
keyPoints: no real prompts exist — stop hunting
add gaming framing on the fly

scenarios.q: Fernando asks: 'Tell me about a time you debugged a production frontend issue.' You have ~15 minutes. What's your structure and what story categories make strong picks?
scenarios.model: Structure (SAO format): (1) Situation — one sentence on the observable symptom, no jargon ('users on iOS Safari started seeing a blank screen after our Friday deploy'). (2) Hypothesis — what you initially suspected and why (framework, recent commits, error shape in telemetry). (3) Bisect — how you narrowed it (git bisect, feature-flag toggle, rollback test, reproducing in a clean env, reading the exact stack trace). (4) Root cause — the actual mechanism, named precisely, not 'some race condition.' (5) Fix — what you shipped and why that was the minimal correct change. (6) Prevention — the systemic follow-up (regression test, monitoring alert, lint rule, code-review checklist). Strong category picks: hydration mismatch (SSR-specific, shows framework depth), CDN cache invalidation (shows infra literacy), memory leak from uncleaned event listeners (shows lifecycle understanding), bundle-size regression from a transitive dep (shows build-chain awareness), race condition in async state updates (shows concurrency reasoning). Avoid: 'I Google'd it and found the answer on Stack Overflow' — that shows no diagnostic process.
keyPoints: symptom → hypothesis → bisect → root cause → fix → prevention
strong categories: hydration, CDN cache, listener leaks, bundle regression, async race
avoid passive 'found it on SO' framing

data-flow-narrative.preview: How to turn 'we use fetch' into a five-layer narrative that proves you think about the full path from API to pixel.
data-flow-narrative.priorQuestion: Before reading: a PM asks you in 30 seconds 'how does data get from your API to the user's screen?' Write the first sentence of your answer.

recite.q: If a teammate said 'we don't need skeleton screens, spinners are fine,' how would you push back — and what would you tell them to say to a PM?
recite.model: Spinners replace content with nothing, destroying layout context and making the page feel like it froze. Skeletons preserve the shape of what's coming so the user's eye stays anchored and the load feels fast even when it isn't. The framing for the PM: 'layout stability IS the perceived performance win — players keep their eye where they were looking instead of rescanning the page when data lands.'

recite.q: In your own words, why split components into 'container' (data-owning) and 'presentational' (pure)? What does it buy you in a code review?
recite.model: Container components know how to fetch; presentational components know how to render. That split means presentational components are trivially reusable, testable with just mock props, and any loading/error/empty state handling lives in one place. In code review, it shows you're applying single-responsibility to UI instead of letting data fetching leak through your leaves.

recite.q: Explain the four render states (loading / error / empty / success) to Fernando as if he asked 'why not just show a spinner and the data?'
recite.model: Every data-dependent view has four legitimate states, not two. Spinner-then-data ignores error (user sees a spinner forever on failure) and empty (blank screen when there's no data). Treating each as a distinct render branch means the user never sees a white screen and never sees a frozen spinner — there's always something actionable, whether that's a retry button, a 'no matches yet' CTA, or the real content.

auth-deep-dive.preview: The real answer to 'design a login' — what happens after you get the token, not before. JWT + silent refresh is the pattern that impresses.
auth-deep-dive.priorQuestion: Before reading: a player's access token expires while they're mid-way through configuring their match settings. Describe exactly what they see, and what your code does.

recite.q: Explain the JWT + silent refresh pattern like you would to a junior engineer who just suggested storing tokens in localStorage.
recite.model: Two tokens in HttpOnly cookies: a short-lived access token (~15 min) and a longer-lived refresh token (~7-30 days). A fetch interceptor catches 401 responses, calls /auth/refresh to get a new access token, then retries the original request transparently. The user never sees the session die — the UI doesn't flicker, nothing is lost. localStorage is out of the picture because JS access makes any XSS vulnerability a nuclear one: any compromised script can exfiltrate the token.

recite.q: If Fernando asked 'what's the real tradeoff between HttpOnly cookies and localStorage for auth tokens?' — give the full answer, not the memorized one.
recite.model: HttpOnly is XSS-proof but opens you to CSRF, which you solve with SameSite=Lax plus double-submit CSRF tokens on state-changing requests. localStorage is CSRF-proof but XSS-vulnerable — any third-party script ever loaded on your page (analytics, A/B testing, ad tags) can read it. In a browser context, XSS is the higher-frequency attack vector, so HttpOnly + CSRF defense beats localStorage for anything that matters. The JWT + silent refresh pattern sits on top of the HttpOnly foundation.
keyPoints: localStorage: XSS-vulnerable via any third-party script
XSS is higher-frequency threat in browsers

recite.q: Reframe 'we use HttpOnly cookies to prevent XSS token theft' in player-first language, like you'd say it to Fernando.
recite.model: If a third-party script on our page is ever compromised — an analytics tag, an ad, anything — a player's session can't be stolen from them. Their account stays theirs, full stop. The technical reason is that JavaScript cannot read HttpOnly cookies, but the reason I actually care is that it's the difference between a security incident where 'some tokens leaked' and one where 'no players were affected.'

player-first-framing.preview: The one-sentence structure that turns every technical answer into a Riot-interviewer answer. This is the meta-skill that separates good from great.
player-first-framing.priorQuestion: Before reading: explain in one sentence why React Query's stale-while-revalidate is good. Write it exactly as you'd say it in the interview.

recite.q: Reframe this sentence in player-first language: 'We debounce search input 300ms to reduce API call volume.' Make it sound like something you'd say to Fernando.
recite.model: 300 ms of debounce so that when a player types 'faker' during game one of Worlds, the search feels instant and the pro's card pops up — not a stutter-storm of requests fighting each other. The API cost savings are a nice side effect; the real win is that the interface keeps up with a fast typist under pressure.
keyPoints: lead with the player's specific moment
use a concrete scenario (Worlds, typing fast)
technical benefit framed as side effect
show you've pictured the human using it

recite.q: Explain why the rule 'finish every design decision with "which means for the player, X"' works as an interview signal at Riot specifically.
recite.model: It forces you to prove that your technical choice has a concrete human benefit, which is the level of thinking senior engineers at a player-obsessed company are expected to reach. Fernando doesn't need to be convinced that code splitting is good — he needs to see that you think about why it matters to someone holding a phone in line at Worlds. The one-sentence formula is a discipline that makes player-obsession visible in every answer.
keyPoints: proves you connect code to outcome

recite.q: Pick one technical decision you'd make in the Login walkthrough and walk through the player-first reframing out loud — start technical, end with the player.
recite.model: 'I'd use the JWT + silent refresh pattern with HttpOnly cookies and a fetch interceptor that transparently retries 401s.' Then close: 'which means a player who opens the client and leaves it sitting for an hour can come back and click into a match without being kicked to a login screen. Their session recovers silently — they never even know it refreshed. Every time that works invisibly, it's a small trust win, and trust wins compound.'
keyPoints: pivot with 'which means for the player'
